HIS olde Pandion, this king, gan wepe for tendernesse of herte, for to leve His doghter goon, and for to yive her leve; Of al this world be lovede nothing so; But at the laste leve hath she to go. for Pbilomene, with salte teres eke, Gan of her fader grace to beseke To seen ber suster, that ber longeth so; And bím embraceth with her armes two. And therwithal so yong and fair was she That, whan that Terëus saw ber beautee, And of array that ther was noon ber liche, And yit of bountee was she two so riche He caste his fyry herte upon ber so That he wol have her, how so that hit go Hnd with bis wyles kneled and so preyde, Cil at the laste Pandion thus seyde: O, sone, quod he, that art to me so dere, I thee betake my yonge doghter here, That bereth the key of al my hertes lyf. And grete wel my doghter and thy wyf, And yive her leve somtyme for to pleye, That she may seen me ones er I deye. Hnd soothly, he bath mad bim riche feste Hnd to his folk, the moste and eek the leste, That with hiím com; and yaf hím yiftes grete, And him conveyeth through the maistersstrete Of Athenes, and to the see him broghte, And turneth hoom; no malice he ne thoghte. HE ores pulleth forth the vessel faste, And into Trace arriveth at the laste, And up into a forest he her ledde, And to a cave privily hím spedde; And, in this derke cave, yif her leste, Or leste noght, he bad her for to reste; Of whiche her herte agroos, and seyde thus: Wheris my suster, brother Tereus? And therwithal she wepte tenderly, And quook for fere, pale and pitously, Right as the lamb that of the wolf is biten; Or as the colver, that of the egle is smiten, And is out of his clawes forth escaped 442 Yet hit is sore afered and awhaped Lest hit be hent eftsones, so sat she. But utterly hit may non other be. That be bath reft her of ber maydenbede, Lof here a dede of men, and thatarighti H belpeth nat; and yet this false theer Hath doon this lady yet a more mischeef, for fere lest she sholde bis shame crye, And doon hím openly a vilanye, And with bis swerd her tong of kerveth he, And in a castel made her for to be ful privily in prison evermore, And kepte her to bis usage and bis store, So that she mighte bim nevermore asterte. O sely Philomenel wo is thyn herte; God wreke thee, and sende thee thy bonel Now is hit tyme I make an ende sone. HIS Tereus is to bis wyf ycome, And in his armes hath bis wyf ynome, And pitously be weep, and shook his heed, And swor her that be fond her suster deed; for which this sely Progne hath swich wo, That ny her sorweful berte brak atwo; And thus in teres lete I Progne dwelle, And of her suster forth I wol yow telle. HIS woful lady lerned bad in youthe So that she werken & embrouden couthe, And weven in her stole the radevore As hit of women hath be woned yore. And, shortly for to seyn, she hath her file Of mete and drink, and clothing at her wille And coude eek rede, and wel ynogh endyte, But with a penne coude she nat wryte; But lettres can she weven to and fro, So that, by that the yeer was alago, She had ywoven in a stamin large How she was broght from Athenes in a barge And in a cave how that she was broght; And al the thing that Tereus hath wroght, She waf hit wel, and wroot the story above, How she was served for her suster love; And to a knave a ring she yaf anoon, And prayed hím, by signes, for to goon Anto the quene, and beren ber that clooth, And than by signes swor him many an ooth She sholde him yeve what she geten mighte dighte, And took bit her, and al the maner tolde. And, whan that Progne hath this thing beholde No word she spak, for sorwe and eek for rage; But feyned her to goon on pilgrimage To Bachus temple; and, in a litel stounde, Her dombe suster sitting hath she founde, Weping in the castel her aloon. Allas1 the wo, the compleint,and the moon That Progne upon ber dombe suster makethi In armes everich of hem other taketh, And thus I lete hem in hir sorwe dwelle. E remenant is no charge for to telle, for this is al & som, thus was sheserved ever harm agilte ne deserved this cruel man, that she of wiste. y be war of men, yif that yow liste. I he that he wol nat, for his shame, Doon so as Tereus, to lese his name, Ne serve yow as a mordrour or a knave, ful litel wbyle shul ye trewe him have, That wol I seyn, al were he now my brother, But hit so be that he may bave non other. Explicit Legenda Philomene. ![](images/4dcc522c70c01240cb674ced56f335497d127f02be4b589731061b5532876da6.jpg) ![](images/0597770461ea38d0193dd087266c78280bd3678f28d09b9849bc82379fd29981.jpg) That wikked fruit cometh of a wikked tree, That may ye finde,if that it lyketh yow. But for this ende I speke this as now, To telle you of false Demophon. In love a falser berde I never non, But if hit were his fader Theseus. God, for his grace, fro swich oon kepe us Thus may thise women prayen that hit here. Now to theffect turne I of my matere. ESTROγED is of Troye the citee; This Demophon com sailing in the see Toward Hthenes, to his paleys large; With him com many a ship and many a barge ful of bis folk, of wbich ful many oon Is wounded sore,and seek,and wo begoon. And they ban at the sege longe ylain. Behinde him com a wind and eek a rain That shoof so sore, his sail ne mighte stonde Him were lever than al the world alonde, So hunteth him the tempest to and fro. So derk hit was, he coude nowher go; And with a wawe brosten was his stere. His ship was rent so lowe, in swich manere, That carpenter ne coude hit nat amende. The see, by nighte, as any torche brende for wood, and posseth him now up now doun, Til Neptune hath of him compassioun, And Thetis, Chorus, Triton, and they alle, And maden him upon a lond to falle, Wherof that Phillis lady was and quene, Ligurgus doghter, fairer on to sene Than is the flour again the brighte sonne. Annethe is Demophon to londe ywonne, Wayk and eek wery, and bis folk forpyned Of werinesse, and also enfamyned; And to the deeth he almost was ydriven. His wyse folk to conseil han him yiven To seken belp and socour of the queen, And loken what his grace mighte been, And maken in that lond som chevisaunce, To kepen hím fro wo and fro mischaunce. for seek was he, and almost at the deeth; Unnethe mighte he speke or drawe his breeth, And lyth in Rodopeya him for to reste. Whan he may walke, him thoughte hit was the beste Unto the court to seken for socour. Men knewe him wel, and diden him honour; forat Hthenes duk and lord was he, Hs Theseus his fader hadde ybe, That in bis tyme was of greet renoun, No man so greet in al his regioun; And lyk his fader of face and of stature, And fals of love; hit com him of nature; Hs doth the fox Renard, the foxes sone, Of kinde he coude his olde faders wone Withoute lore, as can a drake swimme, Whan hit is caught and caried to the brimme. This honourable Phillis doth him chere, Her lyketh wel his port and his manere. But for I am agroted heerbiforn To wryte of hem that been in love forsworn, And eek to haste me in my legende, Which to performe God me grace sende, Therfor I passe shortly in this wyse; Ye han wel herd of Theseus devyse In the betraising of fair Adriane, That of her pite kepte him from his bane. Ht shorte wordes, right so Demophon The same wey, the same path hath gon That dide his false fader Theseus. for unto Phillis hath he sworen thus, To wedden her, and her his trouthe plighte, And piked of her al the good he mighte, Whan he was hool and sound and hadde his reste; And doth with Phillis what so that him leste. And wel coude I, yif that me leste so, Cellen al his doing to and fro. E seide, unto his contree moste he saile, for ther he wolde her wedding apparaile As fil to her honour and his also. And openly he took his leve tho, And hath ber sworn,be wolde nat sojorne But in a month be wolde again retorne. And in that lond let make bis ordinaunce As verray lord, and took the obeisaunce for unto Phillis yit ne com be noght. Allasi that, as the stories us recorde, She was her owne deeth right with a corde, AT to bim first she wroot and faste him prayed Hs I reherse shal a word or tweyne. he wolde come, and ber deliver of peyne Me list nat vouchesauf on bim to swinke, Ne spende on bim a penne ful of inke, for fals in love was be, right as his syre; The devil sette hir soules bothe afyrel But of the lettre of Phillis wol I wryte A word or tweyne, althogh bit be but lyte. DYN bostesse, quod she, O Demophon, Thy Phillis, which that is so wo begon OfRodopeye,upon yowmoot compleyn Over the terme set betwix us tweyne, That ye ne holden forward, as ye seyde; Your anker, which ye in our baven leyde, Highte us, that ye wolde comen, out of doute Or that the mone ones wente aboute. But tymes foure the mone hath bid her face Sin thilke day ye wente fro this place, And foure tymes light the world again. But for al that, yif I shal soothly sain, Yit hath the streem of Sitho nat ybroght from Athenes the ship; yit comth hit noght. And, yif that ye the terme rekne wolde, As I or other trewe lovers sholde, I pleyne not, God wot, beforn my day. UT al her lettre wryten I ne may By ordre, for hit were to mea charge; Her lettre was right long and therto large But here and there in ryme I have hit laid, Ther as me thoughte that she wel hath said. HE seide: Thy sailes comen nat again, Ne to thy word ther nis no fey certein; But I wot why ye come nat, quod she; for I was of my love to you so free. And of the goddes that ye han forswore, Yif that hir vengeance falle on yow therfore Ýe be nat suffisaunt to bere the peyne. To moche trusted I, wel may I pleyne, Upon your linage and your faire tonge, And on your teres falsly out ywronge. How coude ye wepe so by craft? quod she May ther swiche teres feyned be? Now certes, yif ye wolde have in memorie, Hit oghte be to yow but litel glorie To have a sely mayde thus betrayed To God, quod she, preye I, and ofte have prayed That hit be now the grettest prys ofalle, And moste honour that ever yow shal befalle And whan thyn olde auncestres peynted be In which men may hir worthinesse see, Than, preye I God, thou peynted be also, That folk may reden, forby as they go, Lol this is he, that with his flaterye Betrayed bath and doon ber vilanye That was bis trewe love in thoghte and dede But sothly, of oo point yit may they rede, That ye ben lyk your fader as in this; for he begyled Adriane, ywis, With swiche an art and swiche sotelte As thou thyselven bast begyled me. As in that point, althogh bit be nat fayr, Thou folwest him, certein, and art his eyr. INCIPILEGENDA YPERMISCRE. ![](images/21dd0747ffb464cbbc63c32604b9fe2e607331104ac33f670ed743d0170ccacf.jpg) But sin thus sinfully ye me begyle, By body mote ye seen, within a whyle, Right in the haven of Athenes fletinge, Withouten sepulture and buryinge; Thogh ye ben barder than is any stoon. Hnd, whan this lettre was forth sent anoon And knew how brotel and how fals he was, She for dispeyr fordide herself, allas1 Swich sorwe hath she, for she besette her so. Be war, ye women, of your sotil fo, Sin yit thís day men may ensample see; And trusteth, as in love, no man but me. Explicit Legenda Phillis. GRECE WDYLOM WEREN brethren twO, Of whiche that oon was called Danao, Chat many a sone hath of his body wonne, As swiche false lovers ofte conne. Among his sones alle ther was oon Chataldermost he lovede of everichoon. And whan thís child was born, this Danao Shoop him a name, and called him Lino. That other brother called was Egiste, That was of love as fals as ever hím liste, And many a doghter gat he in his lyve; Of which he gat upon his righte wyve Adoghter dere, and dide her for to calle Ypermistra, yongest of hem alle; The whiche child, of her nativitee, Toalle gode thewes born was she, As lyked to the goddes, or she was born, That of the shefe she sholde be the corn; The Wirdes, that we clepen Destinee, Hath shapen her that she mot nedes be Pitouse, sadde, wyse, and trewe as steel; And to this woman hit accordeth weel. for, though that Venus yaf her greet beautee, With Jupiter compouned so was she Chat conscience, trouthe, and dreed of shame, And of her wyfhood for to kepe her name, This, thoughte her, was felicitee as here. And rede Mars was, that tyme of the yere, So feble, that his malice is him raft, Repressed hath Venus bis cruel craft; What with Venus and other oppressioun Of houses, Mars hís vením is adoun, Chat Ypermistra dar nat handle a knyf In malice, thogh she sholde lese her lyf. But natheles, as heven gan tho turne, To badde aspectes hath she of Saturne, That made her for to deyen in prisoun, Hs I shal after make mencioun. O Danao and Egistes also, Althogh so be that they were brethren two, for thilke tyme nas spared no línage, Hit lyked bem to maken mariage Betwix Ypermistra and him Líno, And casten swiche a day bit shal be so; And ful acorded was hit witterly; The array is wroght, the tyme is faste by. And thus Lino hath of his fadres brother The doghter wedded, and eche of hem bath other. HE torches brennen & the lampes brighte, The sacrifices been fulredy dighte; Thencens out of the fyre reketh sote, The flour, the leef is rent up by the rote To maken garlands and corounes hye; ful is the place of soun of minstralcye, Of songes amorous of maríage, As thilke tyme was the pleyn usage. And this was in the paleys of Egiste, That in his hous was lord, right as him liste And thus the day they dryven to an ende; The frendes taken leve, and hoom they wende. The night is come, the bryd shal go to bedde; Egiste to his chambre faste him spedde, And privily he let his doghter calle. Whan that the hous was voided of hem alle, He loked on his doghter with glad chere, And to her spak, as ye shulafter here. Yrighte doghter, tresor of myn herte Sin first that day that shapen was my sherte, ![](images/dbc30ff34ef41a9d0e9230fae6c0a651b6cc8998465efe8cd340db5ae87b2073.jpg) ![](images/032bfcad7f30bf44d166fdfd07b0d769e3538364dbec4a2009c481224294a8dc.jpg) Or by the fatal sustren had my dom, So ny myn herte never thing me com As thou, myn Ypermistra, doghter dere Tak heed what I thy fader sey thee here, And werk after thy wyser evermo. for alderfirste, doghter, I love thee so That al the world to me nis half so leef; Ne I nolde rede thee to thy mischeef for al the gode under the colde mone; And what I mene, hit shal be seid right sone, With protestacioun, as in this wyse, Chat, but thou do as I shal thee devyse, Thoushaltbedeed,by him thatalhath wroght At shorte wordes, thou nescapest noght Out of my paleys, or that thou be deed, But thou consente and werke after my reed; Tak this to thee for ful conclusioun. grene; Hyd this, quod be, that hit be nat ysene; And, whan thyn husbond is to bedde ygo, Whyl that he slepeth, cut his throte atwo. for in my dremes hit is warned me How that my nevew shal my bane be, But whiche I noot, wherfor I wol be siker. Yif thou sey nay, we two shul have a biker As I have seyd, by him that I have sworn. HIS Ypermistra hath ny her wit for-7lorn; And, for to passen barmles of that place, Deed wex her hewe, and lyk as ash to sene, And seyde: Lord and fader, al your wille, After my might, God wot,I shal fulfille, So hit to me be no confusioun. She graunted hím; ther was non other grace. And therwithal a costrel taketh he, And seyde: Herof a draught, or two or three Yif him to drinke, wban he goth to reste, And be shal slepe as longe as ever thee leste, The narcotiks and opies been so stronge: And go thy wey, lest that him thinke longe. UT comth the bryd, & with ful sober chere, NIL, quod be, have noon excepcioun; And out be caughte a knyf, as rasour As is of maidens ofte the manere, To chambre is broght with revel and with songe, And shortly, lest this tale be to longe, This Lino and she ben sone broght to bedde; And every wight out at the dore him spedde. Ascold as any frost now wexeth she; forpitebytheberteberstreyneth so, And on ber bandes faste loketh she. Hllas and shulmy bandes blody be? Iam a maid, and, as by my nature, And by my semblant and by my vesture, Myn bandes been nat shapen fora knyf, As for to reve no man fro his lyf. What devil have I with the knyf to do? And shal I bave my throte corve atwo? Than shal I blede, allas and me beshende; And nedes cost this thing mot have an ende; Orheor I mot nedes lese our lyf. Now certes, quod she, sin I am his wyf, And bath my feith, yit is it bet for me for to be deed in wyfly honestee Than be a traitour livíng in my shame. Be as be may, for ernest or for game, He shal awake, and ryse and go his way Out at this goter, or that hit be day1 And weep ful tenderly upon bis face, And in her armes gan him to embrace, And him she roggeth and awaketh softe; And at the window leep he fro the lofte Wban she bath warned him, and doon him bote. HIS Lino swifte was, and light of fote, And from his wyf he ran a ful good pas. This sely woman is so wayk, allas And helples so, that, or that she fer wente, Her cruel fader dide ber for to hente. Allas1 Linol why art thou so unkinde? Why ne haddest thou remembred in thy minde To taken her, and lad ber forth with thee? for, whan she saw that goon awey was he, And that she mighte nat so faste go, Ne folwen him, she sette her doun right tho, Til she was caught and fetered in prisoun. This tale is seid for this conclusioun.... Unfinished. ![](images/e42b2da1a5ea74c4c72e800cf12abe53c2107ce630b706a79a60bedb6ff83935.jpg) Incipit Liber PrimusProem. ![](images/ab6405eeb4abd240960b1220c4589b08ea28a1afdfa57c274c4d594dd7205d1f.jpg) CARNEASEVERYDREEMTOGODEI for hit is wonder, by the rode, To my wit, what causeth swevenes Either on morwes, or on evenes; And why the effect folweth of somme, And of somme hit shal never come; Why that is an avisioun, And this a revelacioun; Why this a dreem, why that a sweven, And nat to every man liche even; Why this a fantom, these oracles, I noot; but whoso of these miracles The causes knoweth bet than I, Devyne be; for I certeinly Ne can hem noght, ne never thinke To besily my wit to swinke, To knowe of hir signifiaunce The gendres, neither the distaunce Of tymes of hem, ne the causes forwhy this more than that cause is; As if folkes complexiouns Make hem dreme of reflexiouns; Or elles thus, as other sayn, for to greet feblenesse of brayn, By abstinence, or by seeknesse, Prison, stewe, or greet distresse; Or elles by dísordinaunce Ofnaturelacustomaunce That som man is to curious In studie, or melancolious, Or thus, so inly ful of drede, That no man may him bote bede; Orelles, that devocioun Of somme, and contemplacioun Causeth swiche dremes ofte; Or that the cruel lyf unsofte Which these ilke lovers leden That hopen over muche or dreden, That purely hir impressiouns Causeth hem avisiouns; Orif that spirits have the might Tomake folk to dreme anight Orif the soule, of propre kinde, Be so parfit, as men finde, That hit forwot that is to come, And that bit warneth alle and somme Ofeveriche of hir aventures Byavisiouns, or by figures, But that our flesh ne bath no might Tounderstonden bit aright, for hit is warned to derkly; But why the cause is, noght wot I. Wel worthe, of this thing, grete clerkes, That trete of this and other werkes; for lof noon opinioun Nilas now make mencioun, But only that the holy rode Curne us every dreem to godel fornever, sith that I was born, Neno man elles, me biforn, Mette, I trowe stedfastly, So wonderfula dreem as I The tenthe day dide of Decembre, The which, as I can now remembre, Iwol yow tellen every del. AT at my ginning, trusteth wel, I wol make invocacioun, With special devocioun, Anto the god of slepe anoon, That dwelleth in a cave of stoon Apon a streem that comth froLete, That is a flood of helle unswete; Besydea folk men clepe Cimerie, Ther slepeth ay this god unmerie With his slepy thousand sones That alway for to slepe hir wone is; And to thís god, that I of rede, Preve I, that be wol me spede My sweven for to telle aright, If every dreem stonde in his might. And be, that mover is of al That is and was, and ever shal, So yive hem joye that hit here Ofalle that they dreme toyere, And for to stonden alle in grace Of hir loves, or in what place That hem wer levest for to stonde, And shelde hem fro povert and shonde, And fro unhappe and ech dísese, And sende hem al that may hem plese, gg1 TheHous offame. LiberI. That take hit wel, and scorne hit noght, Ne hit misdemen in her thoght Chrough malicious entencioun. And whoso, through presumpcioun, Or hate or scorne, or through envye, Dispyt, or jape, or vilanye, Misdeme hit, preye I Jesus God That (dreme he barfoot, dreme he shod) Chat every harm that any man Hath had, sith that the world began, Befalle him therof, or he sterve, And graunte be mote hit ful deserve, Lol with swich a conclusioun Hs had of his avisioun Cresus, that was king of Lyde, Chat high upon a gebet dydel Chis prayer shal he have of me; I am no bet in charite1 Now herkneth, as I have you seyd, Wbat that I mette, or I abreyd. The Dream. f Decembre the tenthe day, Whan hit was night, to stepeI lay Right ther as I was wont to done, And filonslepe wonder sone, As he that wery was forgo On pilgrimage myles two To the corseynt Leonard, To make lythe of that was hard UT as I sleep, me mette I was Withín a temple ymad of glas; In whiche ther were mo images Of gold, stondinge in sondry stages, And mo riche tabernacles, And with perre mo pinacles, And mo curious portreytures, And queynte maner of figures Of olde werke, then I saw ever. for certeynly,I niste never Wher that I was, but wel wiste I, Hit was ofVenus redely, The temple; for,in portreyture, I saw anoonsright hir figure Naked fletinge in a see. And also on hír heed, parde, Hirrosesgarlond whyt andreed, And hir comb to kembe hir heed, Hír dowves, and daun Cupído, Hir blínde sone, and Vulcano, That in his face was ful broun. AT as I romed up and doun, I fond that on a wal ther was Thus writen, on a table of bras: I wol now singe, if that I can, The armes, and also the man, That first cam, through his destinee, fugitif of Troye contree, In Itaile, with ful moche pyne, Unto the strondes of Lavyne. And tho began the story anoon, ![](images/2566956d36ca5bca526bb83393116ea108699e3640d4aea6dea9a6884d010bde.jpg) TheHous offame. LiberI. As I shal telle yow echoon. IRST saw I the destruccioun Of Troye, through the Greek 园 Sínoun, That with his false forsweringe, And hís chere and his lesinge Made the hors broght into Troye, Thorgh which Troyens loste al hir joye And after thís was grave, allas1 How Ilioun assailed was And wonne, and kíng Príam yslayn, And Polites his sone, certayn, Dispitously, of dan Pirrus. ND next that saw I how Venus, Wban that she saw the castel brende, And bad bir sone Eneas flee; And how he fledde, and how that he Escaped was from al the pres, And took his fader, Hnchises, And bar bím on bis bakke away, Cryinge, Hllas, and welaway The whiche Anchises in his honde Bar the goddes of the londe, Thilke that unbrende were. ND I saw next, in alle this fere, How Creusa, daun Eneas wyf, SSZ Wbich that he lovede as his lyf, And bir yonge son Julo And eek Hscaníus also, fledden eek with drery chere, That hit was pitee for to here; And in a forest, as they wente, At a turninge of a wente, How Creusa was ylost, allas1 That deed, but noot I how, she was; How he hir soughte, and how hir gost Bad him to flee the Grekes ost, And seyde, he moste unto Itaile, Hs was his destínee, sauns faille; That hit was pitee for to here, Whan hir spirit gan appere, The wordes that she to him seyde, And for to kepe hir sone hím preyde. Ther saw I graven eek how he, Hís fader eek, and bis meynee, With his shippes gan to sayle Toward the contree of Itaile, As streight as that they mighte go. HER saw I thee, cruel Juno, That art daun Jupiteres wyf, Chat bast yhated, al thy lyf, Hl the Troyanisshe blood Renne and crye, as thou were wood, On Eolus, the god of windes, To blowen out, of alle kindes, So loude, that he shulde drenche Lord and lady, grome and wenche Of al the Troyan nacioun, Withoute any savacioun. HER saw I swich tempeste aryse, That every herte mighte agryse, To see hit peynted on the walle. aNEK saw I graven eek withalle, Venus, how ye, my lady dere, Wepinge with ful woful chere, Prayen Jupiter an bye To save and kepe that navye Of the Troyan Eneas, Sith that he hir sone was. hER saw I Joves Venus kisse, And graunted of the tempestliss Ther saw I how the tempest sten And how with alle pyne he wente, And prevely took arrivage In the contree of Cartage; And on the morwe, how that he And a knight, bight Achatee, Metten with Venus that day, Goinge in a queynt array, Hs she had ben an bunteresse, With wind blowinge upon hir tresse; How Eneas gan him to pleyne, Whan that he knew bir, of bis peyne; And how his shippes dreynte were, Or elles lost, he niste where; How she gan him comforte tho, And bad bim to Cartage go, And ther he shuldë his folk finde, That in the see were left bebinde. 3 ![](images/5b39b5173a84f15ca837b2de18b931151b2f4b93c5aa582c8094e536121b7ed1.jpg) O, how a woman doth amis, To love him that unknowen is for, by Crist, lol thus hit fareth; Hit is not al gold, that glareth for, also brouke I wel myn beed, Ther may be under goodlibeed Kevered many a shrewea vyce, Therfor beno wight so nyce, Cotakea love only for chere, for speche, or for frendly manere; for this shal every woman finde That som man, of bis pure kinde, Wolshewen outward the faireste, Tilbe have caught that what him leste; And thanne wol be causes finde, And swere how that she is unkinde, Or fals, or prevy, or double was. Althis seye I by Eneas And Dido, and bir nyce lest, That lovedeal to sone a gest; Cherfor I wolseyea proverbe, Chat He that fully knoweth therbe May saufly leye bit to his yë Withoute dreed, this is no lye. AT let us speke of Eneas, How he betrayed hir, allas1 And lefte hir ful unkindely. So whan she saw alsutterly, That he wolde hir of trouthe faile, Thus we be served everichone. How sore that ye men conne grone, Anoon, as we have yow receyved, Certeinly we ben deceyved; for, though your love laste a sesoun, Wayte upon the conclusioun, And eek how that ye determynen, And for the more part diffynen. TheHous offame. LiberI. O, Demophon, duk of Hthenis, How he forswor him ful falsly, And trayed Phillis wikkedly That kinges doghter was of Trace, And falsly gan hís terme pace; And when she wiste that be was She heng birself right by the bals, for he had do hir swich untrouthe; Lol was not this a wo and routhe? EEK lo1 how fals and reccheles Was to Briseida Achilles, ![](images/f87ae2789e5e2e8ec32645908ffb84d253395266d5ab10783b01b6b28b96268c.jpg) And alle the batailles that he Was at bimself, and eek bis knightes, Or he had al ywonne bis rightes; And how be Turnus refte bis lyf, And wan Lavyna to his wyf; Andal the mervelous signals Of the goddes celestíals; How, maugre Juno, Eneas, foral hir sleighte and hir compas, Acheved al his aventure; for Jupiter took of him cure Atthe prayere of Venus; The whiche I preye alway save us, And us ay of our sorwes lightel HHN I bad seyen al this sighte In this noble temple thus, H,Lord1 thoughteI,that mades Yet saw I never swich noblesse Of images, ne swich richesse, As I saw graven in this chirche; But not woot I who dide hem wirche, Ne wher I am, ne in what contree. But now wol I go out and see, Right at the wiket, if I can See owher stering any man, That may me telle wher I am. bEN I out at the dores cam, CC I faste aboute me beheld. Chen saw I but a large feld, CHE HOAS Of fHME LIBER SECANDAS. And ye, me to endyte and ryme Hs fer as that I mighte see, Withouten toun, or hous, or tree, Or bush, or gras, or ered tond; for al the feld nas but of sond Hs smal as man may see yet lye In the desert of Libye; Ne I no maner creature, Chat is yformed by nature, Ne saw, me for to rede or wisse. O Crist, thoughte I, that art in blisse, fro fantom and illusioun Me savel and with devocioun Myn yën to the heven I caste. HO was I war, lol at the laste, Chat faste by the sonne, as hyë As kenne mighte I with myn yë, Me thoughte I saw an egle sore, But that hit semed moche more Then I had any egle seyn. But this as sooth as deeth, certeyn, Hit was of golde, and shoon so brighte, That never saw men such a sighte, But if the heven hadde ywonne Alnewe of golde another sonne; So shoon the egles fethres brighte, And somwhat dounward gan hit lighte. Explícit Liber Primus. And París to Oënone; And Jason to Isiphile; And eft Jason to Medea; And Ercules to Dyanira; for he lefte hir for Iöle, That made hím cacche hís deeth, parde. O fals eek was be, Theseus; Chat, as the story telleth us, How he betrayed Hdriane; The devel be his soules bane1 for had be laughed, had he loured, He mostë have beal devoured, If Hdriane ne had ybe1 And, for she had of hím pitee, She made him fro the dethe escape, And he made hír a ful fals jape; for after this, within a whyle He lefte hir slepinge in an yle, Deserte alone, right in the see, And stal away, and leet hir be; And took hír suster Phedra tho With him, and gan to shippe go. And yet he bad ysworn to here, On al that ever he mighte swere, That, so she saved bim his lyf, He wolde have take hir to his wyf; for she desired nothing elles, In certeín, as the book us telles. Bad hím go into Itaile, And leve Huffrykes regioun And Dido and hir faire toun. HO saw I grave, how to Itaile Daun Eneas is go to saile; And how the tempest al began, And how he loste his steresman, Wbich that the stere, or he took keep, Smot overbord, lol as he sleep. ND also saw I how Sibyle And Eneas, besyde an yle, To helle wente, for to see His fader, Anchises the free. How he ther fond Palínurus, And Dido,and eek Deiphebus; And every tourment eek in belle Saw he, which is long to telle. Which whoso willeth for to knowe, He moste rede many a rowe On Virgile or on Claudian, Or Daunte, that hit telle can. HO saw I grave al tharívaile Chat Eneas had in Itaile; And with king Latine his tretee, DERKNECR,EVERYMHNERMHN Chat English understonde can, And listeth of my dreem to lere; fornow at erste shul ye here So selly an avisioun, That Isaye, ne Scípioun, Ne king Nabugodonosor, Pharo, Curnus, ne Elcanor Nemette swich a dreem as this1 Now faíre blisful, O Cíprís, So be my favour at this tyme1 Helpeth, that on Parnaso dwelle By Elicon the clere welle. CHOUGHT,thatwrootalthatImette, And in the tresorie bit shette Of my braynI now shal men see If any vertu in thee be, To tellen al my dreem aright; Now kythe thyn engyn and might1 The Dream. HIS egle,of which I have yow told, Chat shoon with fethres as of gold, Which that so hyë gan to sore, I gan beholde more andmore, To see bir beautee and the wonder; ![](images/ff5323667d0b18242f1d1ca1e1c86f1252bea5877e874a29fd1e020788e35855.jpg) ![](images/843d2be25cc1c74727aa9a6eb4179cbe470f79998f29a0d3d62ed0a278e35951.jpg) But never was ther dint of thonder, Ne that thing that men calle foudre, That smoot somtyme a tour to poudre, And in bis swifte coming brende, That so swythe gan descende, Hs this foul, whan hit behelde Chat I aroume was in the felde; And with his grimme pawes stronge, Within his sharpe nayles longe, Me, fleinge, at a swappe he hente, And with his sours agayn up wente, ![](images/bb68fbb452cf401c01ac5fda62fe0d5eeeb726d390d331c7ba2e4dfb6f32d67c.jpg) And sayde twyës: Seynte Mariel Thou art noyous for to carie, And nothing nedeth hit, parde1 for also wis God helpe me Hs thou non harm shalt have of this; And this cas, that betid thee is, Is for thy lore and for thy prow; Let seel darst thou yet lokenow? Be ful assured, boldely, I am thy frend And therwith I Gan for to wondren in my minde. O God, thoughte I, that madest kinde, Shal I non other weyes dye? Wher Joves wol me stellifye, Or what thing may this signifye? I neither am Enok, ne Elye, Ne Romulus,ne Ganymede That was ybore up, as men rede, To hevene with dan Jupiter, And maad the goddes boteler. O1 thís was tho my fantasyel But he that bar me gan espye That I so thoghte, and seyde this: Thou demest of thyself amis; for Joves is not theraboute, I dar wel putte thee out of doute, To make of thee as yet a sterre. But er I bere thee moche ferre, Me caryinge in his clawes starke Hs lightly as I were a larke, How high, I can not telle yow, for I cam up, Iniste how. for so astonied and asweved Was every vertu in my heved, What with his sours and with my drede, Chat al my feling gan to dede; forwhy hit was to greet affray. HAS I longe in his clawes lay, Til at the laste he to me spak In mannes voís, and seyde: Hwak1 And be not so agast, for shamel And called me tho by my name. And, for I sholde the bet abreyde, Me mette, Awak, to me he seyde, Right in the same vois and stevene That useth oon I coude nevene; And with that vois, soth for to sayn, My mínde cam to me agayn; for hit was goodly seyd to me, So nas hit never wont to be. ND herwithal I gan to stere, 是 Cilthhtettete And felte eek tho myn berte bete. And tho gan he me to disporte, And with wordes to comforte, TheHous fame. LiberII. I wol thee telle what I am, And whider thou shalt, and why I cam Co done this, so that thou take Good berte, and not for fere quake. Gladly, quod INow wel, quod he: firstI, that in my feet have thee, Of which thou bast a feer and wonder, Am dwelling with the god of thonder, Which that men callen Jupiter, That dooth me flee ful ofte fer Codo al his comaundement. And for this cause be bath me sent To thee: now herke, by thy trouthel Certeyn, he bath of thee routhe, That thou so longe trewely hast served so ententifly his blinde nevew Cupido, And fair Venus goddesse also, Withoute guerdoun ever yit, And nevertheles hast set thy wit, Although that in thy bede ful lyte is, Tomake bokes, songes, dytees, In ryme, or elles in cadence, Asthou best canst, in reverence Of Love, and of his servants eke, That have his servise soght, and seke; And peynest thee to preyse his art, Althogh thou haddest never part; Wherfor, also God me blesse, Joves halt hit greet humblesse And vertu eek, that thou wolt make Anight fulofte thyn beed to ake, In thy studie so thou wrytest, And evermo of love endytest, In honour of him and preysinges, Andin his folkes furtheringes, Andin bir matere al devysest, And noght him nor his folk despysest, Although thou mayst go in the daunce Of hem that him list not avaunce. And also, beau sir, other thinges; Chatis, that thou bast no tydinges OfLoves folk, if they be glade, Neof noght elles that God made; And noght only fro fer contree That ther no tyding comth to thee, Butof thy verray neyghebores, That dwellen almost at thy dores, Thou herest neither that ne this; for whan thy labour doon al ís, And hast ymaad thy rekeninges, In stede of reste and newe thinges, Thou gost hoom to thy hous anoon; And,also domb as any stoon Thou sittest at another boke, Cil fully daswed is thy loke, And lívest thus as an hermyte, Although thyn abstinence is lyte. ND therfor Joves, through his 2 Wol t t I bere thee to a lace, gg4 Wbich that hight The Hous of fame, To do thee som disport and game, In som recompensacioun Of labour and devocioun Chat thou hast had, lol causeles, To Cupido, the reccheles1 And thus this god, thorgh bis meryte, Wol with som maner thing thee quyte, So that thou wolt be of good chere. for truste wel, that thou shalt here, When we be comen ther I seye, Mo wonder thinges, dar I leye, Of Loves folkemo tydinges, Bothe sothssawes and lesinges; And mo loves newe begonne, And longe yserved loves wonne, And mo loves casuelly That been betid, no man wot why, But as a blind man stert an bare; And more jolytee and fare, Wbyl that they finde love of stele, As thinketh hem, and overal wele; Mo discords, and mo jelousyes, Mo murmurs, and monovelryes, And mo dissimulaciouns, And feyned reparaciouns; And mo berdes in two houres Withoute rasour or sisoures Ymaad, then greynes be of sondes; And eke mo holdinge in hondes, And also mo renovelaunces Of olde forleten aqueyntaunces; Mo loverdayes and acordes Then on instruments ben cordes; And eke of loves mo eschaunges Than ever cornes were in graunges; Unethe maistow trowen this? Quod he No, helpe me God so wis1 Quod INo? wby? quod he for bit Were impossible, to my wit, Though that fame hadde al the pyes In al a realme, and al the spyes, How that yet she shulde here al this, Or they espye hitO yis, yis Quod he to me, that can I preve By resoun, worthy for to leve, So that thou yeve thyn advertence To understonde my sentence. IRsshalt tou eren wher she And so thyn owne book bit telleth Hir paleys stant,as I shal seye, Right even in middes of the weye Betwixen hevene, erthe,and see; That, whatsoever in al these three Is spoken, in privee or aperte, The wey therto is so overte, And stant eek in so juste a place, That every soun mot to hit pace, Or what so comth fro any tonge, Be hit rouned, red, or songe, Or spoke in sëurtee or drede, Certein, hit moste thider nede. O herkne wel; forwhy I wille Tellen thee a propre skile, The Hous of fame. LiberII. Hnd worthy demonstracioun In myn imagynacioun. EffREY, thou wost right wel this, Chat every kindly thing that is, Hath a kindly stede ther he May best in bit conserved be; Unto which place every thing, Chrough his kindly enclyning, Moveth for to come to, Whan that hit is awey therfro; Hs thus; lo, thou mayst al day see That any thing that hevy be, As stoon or leed, or thing of wighte, And ber hit never so hye on bighte, Lat go thyn band, bit falleth doun. IGhT so seye I by fyre or soun, Or smoke, or other thinges lighte, Hlwey they seke upward on bighte; Whyl ech of hem is at his large, Light thing up, and dounward charge. ND for this cause mayst thou see, That every river to the see Enclyned is to go, by kinde. And by these skilles, as I finde, Hath fish dwellinge in floode and see, And treës eek in erthe be Thus every thíng, by thís resoun, Hath bís propre mansioun, To which hit seketh to repaire; Hs ther hit shulde not apaire. Lo, this sentence is knowen couthe Of every phílosophres mouthe, Hs Hrístotle and dan Platon, And to confirme my resoun, Thou wost wel this, that speche is soun, Or elles no man mighte hit here; Now herkne what I wol thee lere. OUN is noght but air ybroken, 福 Loud or privee, foul or fair, Hnd every speche that is spoken, In hís substaunce is but air for as flaumbe is but lighted smoke, Right so soun is air ybroke. But this may be in many wyse, Of which I wil thee two devyse, As soun that comth of pype or harpe; for whan a pype is blowen sharpe, The air is twist with violence, And rent; lo, thís is my sentence; Eek, whan men harpesstringes smyte, Whether hit be moche or lyte, Lo, with the strook the air tobreketh; Right so hit breketh whan men speketh. Thus wost thou wel what thing is speche. OW hennesforth I wol thee teche, How every speche, or noise, or soun, Through bis multiplicacioun, Thogh hit were pyped of amouse, Moot nede come to fames House. I preve hit thus, tak hede now, 456 By experience; for if that thou Throwe on water now a stoon Wel wost thou, hit wol mabe ormare anoon H lítelroundelas a cevel. A lítel roundel as a cercle, Jaraventure brood as a covercle. And right anoon thou shalt s And that the thridde, and so forth Every cercle causing other. Wyder than himselve was: Ech aboute other αo Ech aboute other goinge, Caused of othres steringe. And multiplying evermo, Til that hit be so fer ygo That hit at bothe brinkes be Althogh thou mowe hit not vsee Hbove, hit goth yet alway under. Hlthough thou thenke hit a ar Hnd wboso seith of troutha a gret wonder. Bid bim proven the contrarie That loude or prívee spoken is. Moveth first an air aboute Ana or this moving, out of doute, Hs I have of the water preved That every cercle causeth other. Right so of air, my leve brother: Everich air in other stereth More and more, and speche up bereth. Or vois, or noise, or word, or soun, Hy through multiplicacioun, Til hit be atte Hous of fame: Tak bit in ernest or in game. Mov fames place. Moveth up on high to pace KindelyLE me this feithfully, Vhave I not preved thus simply, Withouten any subtiltee ennoche.or gret prolixitee stormes of philosopbye, od be H good persuasioun, WA Dow speche or soun, of pure kinde ssgures of poetrye, dt bit is: and lyk to be lours of rethoryke? nhteo as thou hast preved me. rudee bit oghte thee to lyke; hinkth thee my conclusioun? Hath hís kíndeliche stede: Enclyned is upward to meve This, mayst thou fele, wel I preve. suptelme this, now pray I thee, hard langage and bard matere NannGod. guod he, and as I leve, Thousbalt have yit, or hit be eve, ombrous for to here amost thou not wel this? lnable they shulden be. L ewedly to a lewed man T answerde, and seyde, Yis. foverv word of this sentence hemay sbake hem by the biles, ntquod he,lo, so I can cnhe.and shewe bim swiche skiles, Inreve. by experience; And that the mansioun, ywis, Chat every thing enclyned to is, Indwith thyn eres heren wel Ton and taíl, and everydel, Thatevery word that spoken is Comthinto fames Hous, ywís, Hel have seyd; what wilt thou more? @And with this word upper to sore began,and seyde:By Seynt Jame1 Now wil we speken al of game. O farest thou? quod be to me. how fer that thou art now therfro. AndI anoon shal telle thee ND I adoun gan loken tho, el, quodINow see, quod be, By tby trouthe, yond adoun, And bebeld feldes and plaines, Wber that thou knowest any toun, Orhous, or any other thing. And now billes, and now mountaines, Now valeys, and now forestes, And whan thou hast of ought knowing, Andnow,unethes, gretebestes; Nowriveres, now citees, Now tounes, and now grete trees, That highte Pheton, wolde lede Whan the sonnes sone, the rede, Algate hís fader cart, and gye. The cartshors gonne wel espye That ones was ybrent with hete, Now shippes sailinge in the see. That he ne coude no governaunce, AT thus sone in a whyle he And gonne for to lepe and launce, Was flowen fro the grounde so Callen bit Watlinge Strete: EyDENayNowondernis, Quod he, for balf so bigh as this Nas Hlexander Macedo; Ne the king, dan Scípio, Chat saw in dreme, at point devys, Helle and erthe, and paradys; Ne eek the wrecche Dedalus, Ne hís child, nyce Icarus, That fleigh so highe that the hete His winges malt, and he fel wete Inmid the see, and ther he dreynte, for whom was maked moch compleynte. O turn upward, quod he, thy face, And behold this large place, This air; but loke thou ne be Hdrad of hem that thou shalt see; for in this regioun, certein, Dwelleth many a citezein, Of which that speketh dan Plato. Chese ben the eyrish bestes, lo1 ND so saw I al that meynee Bothe goon and also flee. Now, quod he tho,cast up thyn yë; See yonder, lo, the Galaxye, Wbich men clepeth the Milky And beren him now up, now doun, That al the world, as to myn yë, Nomore semed than a prikke; Or elles was the air so thikke ThatI ne mighte not discerne. With that he spak to me as yerne, And seyde: Seestow any toun Orought thou knowest yonder doun? for hit is whyt: and somme, parfey, Til that he saw the Scorpioun, TheHous of fame. LiberII. Which that in heven a signe is yit. And he, for ferde, loste his wit, Of that, and leet the reynes goon Of his hors; and they anoon Gonne up to mounte, and doun descende Til bothe the eyr and erthe brende; Than sheweth hit, withouten dred That kindely the mansioun Of every speche, of every soun, Be hit either foul or fair, Hath his kínde place in air. And sin that every thing, that is Out of his kinde place, ywis, Moveth thider for to go If hit aweye be therfro, As I before have preved thee, Hit seweth, every soun, pardee, Moveth kindely to pace Al up into his kindely place. And this place of which I telle, Ther as fame list to dwelle, Is set amiddes of these three, Heven, erthe, and eek the see, As most conservatif the soun. Chan is thís the conclusioun, That every speche of every man, Hs I thee telle first began, Til Jupiter, lo, atte laste, Him slow, and fro the carte caste. Lo, is it not a greet mischaunce, To lete a fole han governaunce Of thing that he can not demeine? ND with this word, soth for to seyne, He gan alway upper to sore, And gladded me ay more and more, So feithfully to me spak he. O gan I loken under me, And bebeld the eyrish bestes, Cloudes,mistes,and tempestes, Snowes,hailes,reines,windes, And thengendring in bir kindes, And al the wey through whiche I cam; O God, quod I, that made Hdam, Moche is thy might and thy noblessel , With fetheres of Philosophye, The Hous To passen everich element; of fame. And whan be hath so fer ywent, Liber II. Than may be seen, behínd his bak, Cloud, and al that I of spak. HO gan I wexen in a were, And seyde: I woot wel I am bere; But wher in body or in gost I noot, ywis; but God, thou wost1 for more cleer entendement Nadde he me never yit ysent. And than thougbte I on Marcian And eek on Anteclaudian, That sooth was hir descripcioun Of al the hevenes regioun, As fer as that I saw the preve; Therfor I can hem now beleve. Wilt thou lere of sterres aught? Nay, certeinly, quod I, right naught; And wby? for I am now to old. Elles I wolde thee have told, Quod he, the sterres names, lo, And al the hevenes signes to, And which they beenNo fors, quod I Yis, pardee, quod he; wostow why? for whan thou redest poetrye, How goddes gonne stellifye Brid, fish, beste, or him or here, As the Raven, or either Bere, Or Hriones harpe fyn, Castor, Pollux, or Delphyn, Or Htlantes dougbtres sevene, How alle these arn set in hevene; for though thou have hem ofte on honde, Yet nostow not wher that they stonde. O fors, quod I, bit is no nede; I leve as wel, so God me spede, Hem that wryte of this matere, Hs though I knew hir places bere; And eek they shynen here so brighte, Hit shulde shenden al my sigbte, To loke on hem That may wel be, Quod he Hnd so forth bar be me A whyl, and than he gan to crye, That never herde I thing so hye: Now up the heed; for al is wel; Seynt Julyan, lo, bon bostel1 See here the Hous of fame, lo1 Maistow not heren that I do? ert ou Quod he, that rumbleth up and doun In fames Hous, ful of tydínges, Bothe of fair speche and chydinges, And of fals and soth compouned. Herkne wel; hit is not rouned. Herestow not the grete swogh? Yis, pardee, quod I, wel ynogh. Hnd what soun is it lyk? quod he. Peter lyk beting of the see, Quod I, again the roches holowe, Whan tempest doth the shipp swalowe; And lat a man stonde, out of doute, A myle thens, and here hit route; Or elles lyk the last humblinge After the clappe of a thundringe, When Joves hath the air ybete; But hit doth me for fere swete. Nay, dred thee not therof, quod he Hit is nothing wil byten thee; Thou shalt non harm bave, trewely. ND with this word bothe be and I Hs nigh the place arryved AYLW As men may casten with a spere I nistë how, but in a strete He sette me faire on my fete, And seyde: Walke forth a pas, And tak thyn aventure or cas, That thou shalt finde in fames place. O, quod I, whyl we han space To spek, or that I go fro thee, for the love of God, tel me, In sooth, that wil I of thee lere, If this noise that I here Be, as I have herd thee tellen, Of folk that doun in erthe dwellen, And comth here in the same wyse As I thee herde or this devyse; And that ther lyves body nis In al that hous that yonder is, Chat maketh al this loude fare? O, quod he, by Seynte Clare, And also wis God rede mel But o thinge I wil warne thee Of the which thou wolt have wonder. Lo, to the Hous of fame yonder Thou wost how cometh every speche, Hit nedeth noght thee eft to teche. But understond now right wel this; han any speche ycomen is Up to the paleys, anonsright Hit wexeth lyk the same wight, Which that the word in erthe spak, Be hit clothed reed or blak; And hath so verray bís lyknesse That spak the word, that thou wilt gesse That hit the same body be, Man or woman, he or she. And is not this a wonder thing? Yis, quod I tho, by hevene king Hnd with this worde: farwel, quod he, And here I wol abyden thee; And God of hevene sende thee grace, Som good to lernen in this place. And I of him took leve anoon, And gan forth to the paleys goon. Explicit liber secundus. ![](images/111e549bf69ba99346f33a77fdab7d93b9a130c7b9ab7a8a3ea36df880fc2d70.jpg) ![](images/989eafd775735a26422950e764a789b04e91881a0e989ad839897376a2c29696.jpg) GOD OfSCIENCE HND OfLIGNT Apollo, through thy grete might, This litel laste book thou gyel Nat that I wilne, for maistrye, Hereart poetical be shewed; But, for the rym is light and lewed, Yit make hit sumwhat agreable, Chough som vers faile in a sillable; And that I do no diligence To shewe craft, but o sentence. And if, dívyne vertu, thou Wilt helpe me to shewe now That in myn hede ymarked is, Lo, that is for to menen thís, The Hous of fame to descryve, Thou shalt see me go,as blyve, Unto the nexte laure I see, And kisse hit, for hit is thy tree; Now entreth in my breste anoon1... ![](images/5a57453ad37bb5dbc6fefe4aa9f6a0d0079bde5cf053e22e5e7553968018f0d6.jpg) HAN I was fro thís egle goon, I gan beholde upon this place. And certein, or I ferther pace, I wol yow al the shap devyse Of hous and site; and al the How I gan to this place aproche That stood upon so high a roche, Hyer stant ther noon in Spaine. But up I clomb with alle paine, And though to climbe hit greved me, Yit I ententif was to see, Änd for to pouren wonder lowe, If I coude any weyes knowe What maner stoon this roche was; The Hous for hit was lyk a thing of glas, of fame. But that hit shoon ful more clere; That they were molte awey with hete, Of this hille, that northward lay, How hit was writen ful of names Of folk that hadden grete fames Of olde tyme, and yit they were Hs fresshe as men had writen hem there The selve day right, or that houre That I upon hem gan to poure. But wel I wiste what hit made; Hit was conserved with the shade, And stood eek on so cold a place, hO gan I up the hille to goon, And fond upon the coppe a woon, Ne han the cunning to descryve That mighte of beautee be his make, My wit ne may me not suffyse. AT natheles al the substance I bave yit in my remembrance; Bothe castel and the tour, witpouten peces or joininges. But many subtil compassinges, Babewínnes and pinacles, Imageries and tabernacles, I saw; and ful eek of windowes, As flakes falle in grete snowes. And eek in ech of the pinacles Weren sondry habitacles, In whiche stoden, al withoute, ful the castel, al aboute, Of alle maner of minstrales, And gestiours, that tellen tales Bothe of weping and of game, Of al that longeth unto fame. HER herde I pleyen on an barpe That souned bothe wel and sharpe, Orpheus ful craftely, And on his syde, faste by, Sat the harper Orion, And Eacides Chíron, And other harpers many oon, And the Bret Glascurion; And smale harpers with her gleës Seten under hem in seës, And gonne on hem upward to gape, And countrefete hem as an ape, Or as craft countrefeteth kinde. HO saugh I stonden hem behinde Afer fro hem, al by hemselve Tha saugh I Hermes Ballenus, umote. and eek Símon Magus. Thersaugh I, and knew hem by name, Dleve an uncouth thing to telle; HT shuld I make lenger tale Of al the peple that I say, That shoon ful lighter than a glas, HER saugh I than Htiteris, And of Hthenes dan Pseustis, And Marcia that lost ber skin, Bothe in face, body, and chin, for that she wolde envyen, lo1 To pypen bet then Apollo. Ther saugh I famous, olde and yonge, Pypers of the Duche tonge, Colerne loverdaunces, springes, Reyes, and these straunge thinges. HO saugh I in another place Stonden in a large space, Of hem that maken blody soun In trumpe, beme, and clarioun; for in fight and bloodsshedinge Is used gladly clarioninge. HER berde I trumpen Messenus, Of whom that speketh Virgilius. Cher herde I Joab trumpe also, Theodomas, and other mo; And alle that used clarion In Cataloigne and Hragon, That in hir tyme famous were TheHous of fame. LiberIII. Of us1 Thus berde I cryen alle, And faste comen out of halle, And shoken nobles and sterlinges. And somme crouned were as kinges, With crounes wroght ful of losenges And many riban, and many frenges Were on hir clothes trewely. HO atte laste aspyed I Chat pursevauntes and heraudes, That cryen riche folkes laudes, Hit weren alle; and every man Of hem, as I yow tellen can, Had on bim throwen a vesture, Wbich that men clepe a cotesarmure, Enbrowded wonderliche riche, Although they nere nought yliche. But noght nil I, so mote I thryve, Been aboute to discryve Al these armes that ther weren, That they thus on hir cotes beren, for hit to me were impossible; Men mighte make of bem a bible Twenty foot thikke,as I trowe. for certeyn, whoso coude yknowe Mighte ther alle the armes seen Of famous folk that ban ybeen In Huffrike,Europe,and Hsye, Sith first began the chevalrye. O1 how shuide I now telle al this Ne of the balle eek what nede is To tellen yow, that every wal Of hit, and floor, and roof and al Was plated balf a fote thikke Of gold, and that nas nothing wikke, But, for to prove in alle wyse, As fyn as ducat in Venyse, Of whiche to lyte al in my pouche is? And they wer set as thikke of nouchis fulle of the fynest stones faire, That men rede in the Lapidaire, Hs greses growen in a mede; But bit were al to longe to rede The names; and therfore I pace. UT in this riche lusty place, That fames halle called was, ful moche prees of folk ther nas, Ne crouding, for to mochil prees. But al on hye,above a dees, Sitte in a see imperial, That maad was of a rubee al, Wbich that a carbuncle is ycalled, I saugh, perpetually ystalled, A feminyne creature; That never formed by nature Nas swich another thing yseye. for altherfrst, soth for to seye, Me thoughte that she was so lyte That the lengthe of a cubyte Was lenger than she semed be; But thus sone, in a whyle, she Hir tho so wonderliche streighte, That with hir feet she therthe reighte, And with hir heed she touched hevene, ao oiynci otnco oc And therto eek, as to my wit, I saugh a gretter wonder yit, Upon hir eyen to beholde; But certeyn I hem never tolde; foras fele eyen badde she As fetheres upon foules be, Or weren on the bestes foure, That Goddes trone gunne honoure, As John writ in thapocalips. Hir heer, that oundy was and crips, Hs burned gold hit shoon to see. And sooth to tellen, also she Had also fele upstondíng eres And tonges, as on bestes heres; And on bir feet wexen saugh I Partriches winges redely YAT, Lord1 the perrie and the richesse I saugh sitting on this goddessel And, Lord1 the hevenish melodye Of songes, ful of armonye, I herde aboute ber trone ysonge, That al the paleysswalles ronge So song the mighty Muse, she That cleped is Caliopee, And hir eighte sustren eke, Chat in bir face semen meke; And evermo, eternally, They songe of fame,as tho berde I: Heried be thou and thy name, Goddesse of renoun and of famel HO was I war, lo, atte laste, As I myn eyen gan up caste, That this ilke noble quene On bir shuldres gan sustene Bothe tharmes and the name Of tho that hadde large fame; Hlexander,and Dercules That with a sherte his lyf lees Thus fond I sitting this goddesse, In nobley, honour, and richesse; Of which I stinte a whyle now, Other thing to tellen yow. HO saugh I stonde on either syde Streight doun to the dores wyde, fro the dees, many a pileer Of metal, that shoon not ful cleer; But though they nere of no richesse, Yet they were maad for greet noblesse, And in bem greet and by sentence; And folk of digne reverence, Of whiche I wol yow telle fonde, Apon the piler saugh I stonde. Apon a piler stonde on high, Chat was of lede and yren fyn, Hím of secte Saturnyn, The Ebrayk Josephus, the olde, That of Jewes gestes tolde; And bar upon bis shuldres hye Thefame up of the Jewerye. And by him stoden other sevene Wyse and worthy for to nevene, 462 To helpen bim bere up the charge, Hit was so hevy and so large. And for they writen of batailes As wel as other olde mervailes, Therfor was, lo, this pileer, Of which that I yow telle heer, Of lede and yren bothe, ywis. for yren Martes metalis, Which that god is of bataile; And the leed, withouten faile, Is, lo, the metal of Saturne, That hath ful large wheel to turne. Tho stoden forth, on every rowe, Of hem which that I coude knowe, Thogh I hem noght by ordre telle, To make yow to long to dwelle, These, of whiche I ginne rede. HER saugh I stonden, out of dred Apon an yren piler strong, That peynted was, al endelong, With tygres blode in every place, The Tholosan that highte Stace, That bar of Thebes up the fame Apon bís shuldres, and the name Hiso of cruet Hchilles. And by him stood, withouten lees ful wonder bye on a pileer Of yren, he, the gret Omeer; And with him Dares and Tytus Before, and eek he, Lollíus, And Guido eek de Columpnis, And English Gaufride eek, ywis; And ech of these, as have I joye, Was besy for to bere up Troye. So hevy therof was the fame, That for to bere hit was no game. But yit I gan ful wel espye, Betwix hem was a litel envye. Oon seyde, Omere made lyes, feyninge in his poetryes, And was to Grekes favorable; Therfor held be hit but fable. HO saugh I stonde on a pileer, That was of tinned yren cleer, That Latin poete, dan Virgyle, That bore hath up a longe whyle The fame of Píus Eneas. SZ 2 Of coper, Venus clerk, Ovyde, That hath ysowen wonder wyde The grete god of Loves name. And ther be bar up wel his fame, Apon thís piler, also hye As I might see hit with myn yë: forwhy this halle, of whiche I rede Was woxe on highte, lengthe and brede, Wel more, by a thousand del, Than hit was erst, that saugh I wel. HO saugh I, on a piler by, Of yren wroght ful sternely, The grete poete, daun Lucan, And on bhís shuldres bar up than, Hs highe as that I mighte see, The fame of Julíus and Pompee. And by bim stoden alle these clerkes, That writen of Romes mighty werkes, Chat, if I wolde hir names telle, Alto longe moste I dwelle. Dan Claudian, the soth to telle, B Of soulfre, lyk as he were wood Chat bar upal the fame of helle, Of pluto, and of Proserpyne, Chat quene is of the derke pyne. HAT shulde I more telle of this? Of hem that writen olde gestes, As ben on trees rokes nestes; But hit a ful confus matere Wereal the gestes for to bere, That they of write, and how they highte. But whyl that I bebeld this sighte, Iherde anoise aprochen blyve, That ferde as been don in an hyve, Agen ber tyme of outsfleyinge; Right swiche a maner murmuringe, foral the world, hit semed me. HO gan I loke aboute and see, That ther com entring in the halle A right gret company withalle, And that of sondry regiouns, Ofalleskinnes condiciouns, That dwelle in erthe under the mone, Pore and ryche. And also sone As they were come into the halle, They gonne doun on kneës falle Before this ilke noble quene, And seyde: Graunte us, lady shene, Ech of us, of thy grace, a bone1 And somme of hem she graunted sone, And somme she werned wel and faire; And somme she graunted the contraire Of hir axing utterly. Butthus I seye yow trewely, What hir cause was, I niste. for this folk, ful wel I wiste, They hadde good fame ech deserved, Althogh they were diversly served; Right as hir suster, dame fortune, Is wont to serven in comune. OW berkne how she gan to paye Chat gonne hir of hir grace praye; And yit, lo, al thís companye Seyden sooth, and noght a lye. HDAME, seyden they, we be folk that beer besechen thee, Chat thou graunte us now good fame, And lete our werkes han that name; In fulrecompensacioun Of good werk, give us good renoun. WERNÊ yow hit, quod she anoon, Ye gete of me good fame noon, ABy Godl and therfor go your wey. Hlas, quod they, and welaway1 Celle us, what may your cause be? Theous of fame. LiberIII. OR me list hit noght, quod she; No wight shal speke of yow, ywis, Good ne harm, ne that ne this. Hnd with that word she gan to calle Hir messanger, that was in halle, And bad that he shulde faste goon, Up peyne to be blind anoon, for Eolus, the god of winde; In Trace ther ye shul bim finde, And bid hím bringe hís clarioun, That is ful dyvers of his soun, And hit is cleped Clere Laude, With which he wontis to beraude Hem that me list ypreised be: And also bid him how that be Bringe his other clarioun, That highte Sclaundre in every toun, With which he wont is to diffame Hem that me list, and do hem shame. HIS messanger gan faste goon, And found wher, in a cave of stoon, In a contree that highte Trace, This Eolus, with harde grace, Held the windes in distresse, And gan hem under hím to presse, That they gonne as beres rore, He bond and pressed hem so sore. HIS messanger gan faste crye: Rys up, quod he,and faste hye, Til that thou at my lady be; And tak thy clarions eek with thee, And speed thee forth Hnd he anon Took to a man, that hight Triton, His clariouns to bere tho, And leet a certeyn wind to go, That blew so hidously and hye, That hit ne lefte not a skye In al the welken longe and brood. HIS Eolus nowberabood Til he was come at fames feet, And eek the man that Triton beet; And ther he stood, as still as stoon. And herwithal ther com anoon Another huge companye Of gode folk, and gunne crye: Lady, graunte us now good fame And lat our werkes han that name Now, in honour of gentilesse, And also God your soule blesse for we han wel deserved hit, Therfor is right that we ben quit. S thryve I, quod she, ye shal faile, Good werkes shal yow noght availe To have of me good fame as now. But wite ye what? I graunte yow, That ye shal have a shrewed fame And wikked loos, and worse name, Though ye good loos have wel deserved. Now go your wey, for ye be served; And thou, dan Eolus, let see Tak forth thy trumpe anon, quod she, That is ycleped Sclaunder light, And blow hir loos, that every wight ![](images/0b3fef3e8552e118a9e354e57dbb87d2ea3eb283b05c5587fa68ebb75e81a936.jpg) Speke of hem harm and shrewednesse, In stede of good and worthinesse. for thou shalt trumpe al the contraire Of that they han don wel or faire. LAS1 thoughte I, what aventures Han these sory creatures for they, amonges al the pres, Shul thus be shamed gilteles But what1 hit moste nedes be. HHT did this Eolus, but he Tok out bis blakke trumpe of bras That fouler than the devil was, And gan this trumpe for to blowe, As al the world shulde overthrowe; Chat throughout every regioun Wente this foule trumpes soun, As swift as pelet out of gonne, Whan fyr is in the poudre ronne. And swiche a smoke gan outswende Out of hís foule trumpes ende, Blak, blo, grenish, swartish reed, As doth wher that men melte leed, Lo, al on bigh fro the tuell And therto oo thing saugh I wel, Chat, the ferther that hit ran, The gretter wexen hit began, As doth the ríver from a welle, And hit stank as the pit of helle. Allas, thus was hír shame yronge, And giltelees, on every tonge. HO com the thridde companye, And gunne up to the dees to hye, And doun on knees they fille anon, And seyde: We ben everichon folk that han ful trewely Deserved fame rightfully, And praye yow, hit mot be knowe, Right as hit is, and forth yblowe. I graunte, quod she, for me list That now your gode werk be wist; And yit ye shul han better loos, Right in dispyt of alle your foos, Than worthy is; and that anoon: Lat now, quod she, thy trumpe goon, Thou Eolus, that is so blak; And out thyn other trumpe tak That highte Laude, and blow hit so That through the world hir fame go Al esely, and not to faste, That hit be knowen atte laste. UL gladly, lady myn, he seyde; 図 Hnd out bis trumpe of golde he brayde Anon, and sette hit to his mouthe, And blew bit est, and west,and southe, And north, as loude as any thunder, Chat every wight hadde of bit wonder, Sobrode hit ran, or than bit stente. And,certes,al the breeth that wente Outof his trumpes mouthe smelde As men a potful bawme helde Among a basket fulof roses; This favour dide be til ber loses. Ther com the ferthe companye, And gonne stonden in a rewe, And seyden: Certes, lady brighte, We han don wel with al our mighte; But we ne kepen have no fame. hyd our werkes and our name, for Goddes lovel for certes we Hancerteyn doon bit for bountee, And for no maner other thing. I graunte yow al your asking, Quod she: let your werk be deed. And saugh anoon the fifte route And doun on knees anoon to falle; And to hir tho besoughten alle Tobyde bir gode werkes eek, And seyde, they yeven noght a leek for fame, ne for swich renoun; for they, for contemplacioun And Goddes love, hadde ywrought; Neof fame wolde they nought. HAT? quod she, and be ye wood? And wene ye for to do good, have ye dispyt to have my name? Nay, ye shul líven everichoon1 Blow thy trumpe and that anoon, Quod she, thou Eolus, I hote, And ring this folkes werk by note, Thatal the world may of hit here. And he gan blowe hir loos so clere In his golden clarioun, That through the world wente the soun, Sokenely, and eek so softe; But atte laste hit was onslofte. HOO com the sexte companye, And gonne faste on fame crye. Right verraily, in thís manere They seyden: Mercy, lady dere1 Co telle certein, as hit is, We han don neither that ne thís, But ydel al our lyf ybe. But,natheles, yit preye we, That we mowe han so good a fame, And greet renoun and knowen name, As they that han don noble gestes, And acheved alle hir lestes, As wel of love as other thing; Al was us never broche ne ring, Neelles nought, from wimmen sent, Ne ones in hir herte yment Tomake us only frendly chere, But mighte temen us on bere; hh1 The Hous of fame. LiberIII. Yit lat us to the peple seme Swiche as the world may of us deme, That wimmen loven us for wood. Hit shal don us as moche good, And to our herte as moche availe To countrepeise ese and travaile, Hs we had wonne hit with labour; for that is dere boght honour At regard of our grete ese. And yit thou most us more plese; Let us be holden eek, therto, Worthy, wyse, and gode also, And riche, and happy unto love. for Goddes love, that sit above, Chough we may not the body have Of wimmen, yet, so God yow savel Let men glewe on us the name; Suffyceth that we han the fame. GRHANTE, quod she, by my trouth Now,Eolus, withouten slouthe, Tak out thy trumpe of gold, let see, And blow as they han axed me, That every man wene hem at ese, Though they gon in ful badde lese. This Eolus gan hit so blowe, That through the world bhit was yknowe. HO com the seventh route anoon, And fel on kneës everichoon, And seyde: Lady, graunte us sone The same thing, the same bone, That ye this nexte folk ban doon. fy on yow, quod she, everichoon1 Ye masty swyn, ye ydel wrecches, ful of roten slowe tecches1 What? false theves1 wher ye wolde Be famous good, and nothing nolde Deserve why,neneverroughte? Men rather yow tohangen oughtel for ye be lyk the sweynte cat, That wolde have fish; but wostow what? He wolde nothing wete his clowes. Yvel thrift come on your jowes, And eek on myn, if I hit graunte, Or do yow favour, yow to avauntel Thou Eolus, thou king of Tracel Go, blow this folk a sory grace, Quod she,anoon; and wostow how? Hs I shal telle thee right now; Sey:These ben they that wolde honour Have, and do noskinnes labour, Ne do no good, and yít han laude; And that men wende that bele Isaude Ne coude hem noght of love werne; And yit she that grint at a querne Is al to good to ese hir herte. HIS Eolus anon up sterte, And with bis blakke clarioun He gan to blasen out a soun, Hs loude as belweth wind in helle. And eek therwith, the sooth to telle, Chís soun was al so ful of japes, Hs ever mowes were in apes. And that wente al the world aboute, That every wight gan on hem shoute, And for to laughe as they were wode; Such game fonde they in hir hode. HO com another companye, That had ydoon the traiterye, The harm, the gretest wikkednesse That any herte couthe gesse; And preyed hir to han good fame, And that she nolde bem doon no shame, But yeve hem loos and good renoun, And do hit blowe in clarioun. Nay, wis1 quod she, hit were a vyce; Al be ther in me no justyce, Me listeth not to do hit now, Ne this nil I not graunte yow. HO come ther lepinge in a route, And gonne choppen al aboute Every man upon the croune, Chat al the halle gan to soune, And seyden: Lady, lefe and dere, We ben swich folk as ye mowe here. To tellen al the tale aright, We ben shrewes, every wight, And han delyt in wikkednes, Hs gode folk han in goodnes; And joye to be knowen shrewes, And fulle of vyce and wikked thewes; Wherfor we preyen yow, asrowe, That our fame swich be knowe In alle thing right as hit is. GRAANTE hit yow, quod she,ywis. But what art thou that seyst this tale That werest on thy hose a pale, And on thy tipet swiche a belle1 Madame, quod he, sooth to telle, I am that ilke shrewe, ywis, That brende the temple of Isidis In Athenes, lo, that citee. And wherfor didest thou so? quod she. By my thrift, quod he, madame, I wolde fayn han had a fame, Hs other folk hadde in the toun, Althogh they were of greet renoun for hir vertu and for hir thewes; Thoughte I, as greet a fame han shrewes, Thogh hit be but for shrewednesse, Hs gode folk han for goodnesse; And sith I may not have that oon, That other nil I noght forgoon. And for to gette of fames hyre, The temple sette I al afyre. Now do our loos be blowen swythe, As wisly be thou ever blythe. Gladly, quod she; thou Eolus, Herestow not what they preyen us? Madame, yis, ful wel, quod he, And I wil trumpen hit, parde1 And tok his blakke trumpe faste, And gan to puffen and to blaste, Cilbit was at the worldes ende. ICH that I gan aboute wende; for oon that stood right at my bak Me thoughte, goodly to me spak, And seyde: frend, what is thy name? Artow come hider to han fame? Nay, forsothe, frend1 quod I; Icam noght hider, graunt mercy1 forno swich cause, by my heed1 Suffyceth me, as I were deed, That no wight have my name in honde. I woot myself best how I stonde; for wbat I drye or wbat I thinke, I wol myselven al hit drinke, Certeyn, for the more part, As ferforth as I can myn art. But what dost thou bere than? quod he. Quod I: That wol I tellen thee, Thecause wby I stonde here: Som newe tydings for to lere: Som newe thinges, I not what, Cydinges, other this or that, Of love, or swiche thinges glade. for certeynly, he that me made To comen hider, seyde me, I shulde bothe here and see, In this place, wonder thinges; But these be no swiche tydinges Hs I mene of No? quod he. Hnd I answerde: No, pardee1 for wel I wiste, ever yit, Sith that frst I hadde wit, That som folk han desyred fame Dyversly, and loos, and name; But certeynly, Iniste how Ne wher that fame dwelte, er now; Ne eek of hir descripcioun, Ne also hir condicioun, Ne the ordre of hir dome, Anto the tyme I hider come. biche be,lo, these tydinges, That thou now thus hider bringes, That thou hast herd? quod he to me; But now, no fors; for wel I see What thou desyrest for to here. Com forth, and stond no longer here, And I wol thee, withouten drede, In swich another place lede, Ther thou shalt here many oon. The Hous of fame. LiberIII. utortftttogcon Out of the castel, soth to seye. Out or the castel, sotn to seye. Cho saugh I stonde in a valeye, Tho saugh I stonde in a valeye, Under the castel, faste by, An hous, that domus Dedalí, That Laborintus cleped is, Nas maad so wonderliche, ywís, Ne half so queynteliche ywrought. And evermo, so swift as thought, Chis queynte hous aboute wente, That nevermo hit stille stente. And therout com so greet a noise, That, had hit stonden upon Oise, Men mighte hit han berd esely Co Rome, I trowe sikerly. And the noyse which that I herde, for al the world right so hit ferde, As doth the routing of the stoon That from thengyn is leten goon. ND al this hous, of whiche I rede, 0 Was made of twigges, falwe, rede, And grene eek, and som weren whyte, This hous was also ful of gigges, And also ful eek of chirkinges, And of many other werkinges; And eek this hous bath of entrees Hs fele as leves been on trees In somer, whan they grene been; And on the roof men may yit seen A thousand holes, and wel mo, To leten wel the soun out ao. Ben al the dores open wyde, And by night, echoon, unshette Ne porter ther is non to lette No maner tydíngs in to pace; Ne never reste is in that place, That hit nis fild ful of tydinges, Other loude, or of whispringes; And, over alle the houses angles, Is ful of rouninges and of jangles Of werre, of pees,of mariages, Of reste, of labour of víages, Of abood, of deeth, of lyfe, Of love, of hate, acorde, of stryfe, Of loos, of lore, and of winninges, Of hele, of sekenesse, of bildinges, Of faire windes, of tempestes, Of qualme of folk, and eek of bestes; Of dyvers transmutaciouns Of estats, and eek of regiouns; Of trust, of drede, of jelousye, Of wit, of winninge, of folye; Of plentee, and of greet famyne, Of chepe, of derth, and of ruyne; Of good or mis governement, Of fyr, of dyvers accident. 468 ND lo, this hous,of whicheI wryte, Siker be ye, bit nas not lyte; for hit was sixty myle of lengthe; Al was the timber of no strengthe, Yet hit is founded to endure Whyl that hit list to Aventure, That is the moder of tydinges, As the see of welles and springes, And bit was shapen lyk a cage. ERTES, quod I,in almyn age, Ne saugh I swich a hous as this. Hnd as I wondred me, ywis, Apon this hous, tho war was I How that myn egle, faste by, Was perched hye upon a stoon; And I gan streighte to him goon And seyde thus: I preye thee That thou a whyl abyde me for Goddes love, and let me seen What wondres in this place been; for yit, paraventure, I may lere Som good theron, or sumwhat here That leef me were, or that I wente. JETER1 that is myn entente, Quod he to me; therfor I dwelle; But certein, oon thing I thee telle, That, but I bringe thee therinne, Ne shalt thou never cunne ginne To come into hit, out of doute, So faste hit whirleth, lo, aboute. But sith that Joves, of his grace, Hs I have seyd, wol thee solace fynally with swiche thinges, Ancouthe sightes and tydinges, To passe with thyn hevinesse; Suche routhe bath be of thy distresse, That thou suffrest debonairly, And wost thyselven utterly Disesperat of alle blis, Sith that fortune hath maad amis The fruit of al thyn bertes reste Languisshe and eek in point to breste, That he, through his mighty meryte, Wol do thee ese, al be hit lyte, And yaf expres commaundement, To whiche I am obedient, Co furthre thee with al my might, And wisse and teche thee aright Wber thou maist most tydinges here; Shaltow anoon heer many oon lere. ITH this worde he, right anoon Hente me up bitwene his toon, And at a windowe in me broghte, That in this hous was, as me thoghte, And therwithal, me thoghte hit stente, And nothing hit aboute wente, And me sette in the flore adoun. But wbich a congregacioun Of folk, as I saugh rome aboute Some within and some withoute, Nas never seen, ne shal ben eft; That, certes, in the world nis left So many formed by Nature, Ne deed so many a creature; That wel unethe, in that place, Hadde I oon footsbrede of space; And every wight that I saugh there Rouned ech in otheres ere Anewe tyding prevely, Orelles tolde al openly Right thus,and seyde: Nost not thou Chat is betid, lo, late or now? O, quod the other, tel me what; And than he tolde him this and that, And swoor therto that hit was sooth: Thus bath be seyd, and Thus he dooth: Thus shal bit be: Thus herde I seye: That shal be found: That dar I leye: Thatal the folk that is alyve Ne han the cunning to discryve The thinges that I herde there, What aloude, and what in ere. Butal the wondermost was this: Whan oon had herd a thing, ywis, He com forth to another wight, And gan him tellen, anoonsright, The same that to him was told, Or hit a furlong way was old, But gan somwhat for to eche Co this tyding in this speche More than hit ever was. And nat so sone departed nas That he fro him, that he ne mette With the thridde; and, or he lette Any stounde, he tolde him als; Were the tyding sooth or fals, Vit wolde he telle hit nathelees, Ànd evermo with more encrees Than hit was erst. Thus north and southe Went every word fro mouth to mouthe, And that encresing evermo, As fyr is wont to quikke and go from a sparke spronge amis, Tilal a citee brent up is. ND, whan that was ful yspronge, And woxen more on every tonge Chan ever hit was, hit wente anoon Upto a windowe, out to goon; Or, but hit mighte out ther pace, Hit gan out crepe at som crevace, And fleigh forth faste for the nones. ND somtyme saugh I tho, at ones, 2 Hlesing and a sad sothssawe, That gonne of aventure drawe Out at a windowe for to pace; And, when they metten in that place, They were aschekked bothe two, And neither of hem moste out go; forother so they gonne croude, Tileche of hem gan cryen loude: Lat me go firstNay, but lat me1 And here I wol ensuren thee With the nones that thou wolt do so, The Hous offame. LiberIII. That I shal never fro thee go, But be thyn owne sworen brother1 We wil medle us ech with other, That no man, be he never so wrothe, Shal han that oon of two, but bothe Ht ones, al beside his leve, Come we asmorwe or on eve, Be we cryed or stille yrouned. Thus saugh I fals and sooth compouned Cogeder flee for oo tydinge. HUS out at holes gonne wringe Every tyding streight to fame; And she gan yeven eche his name, After hir disposicioun, And yaf hem eek duracioun, Some to wexe and wane sone, As dooth the faire wbyte mone, And leet bem gon. Ther mighte I seen Wenged wondres faste fleen Twenty thousand in a route, Hs Eolus hem blew aboute. ND, Lord1 this hous, in alle tymes, Was ful of shipmen and pilgrymes With scrippes bretful of lesinges, Entremedled with tydinges, And eek alone by bemselve. O, many a thousand tymes twelve Saugh I eek of these pardoneres, Currours, and eek messangeres, With boistes crammed ful of lyes As ever vessel was with lyes. And as I althersfastest wente Aboute, and dide al myn entente Me for to pleye and for to lere, And eek a tyding for to bere, That I had herd of som contree That shal not now be told for me: for hit no nede is, redely; folk can singe bit bet than I; for al mot out, other late or rathe, Alle the sheves in the lathe: I herde a gret noise withalle In a corner of the halle, Ther men of love tydings tolde, And I gan thiderward beholde; for I saugh renninge every wight, Hs faste as that they hadden might; And everich cryed: What thing is that? Hnd som seyde:Inot never what. Hnd whan they were alle on an bepe, Tho bebinde gonne up lepe, And clamben up on othere faste, And up the nose on bye caste, And troden faste on othere heles And stampe, as men don after eles. TTE laste I saugh aman, Wbich that I nevene naught ne can; But he semed for to be Hman of greet auctoritee... Unfinished. ![](images/8ae6d40865e16a57b7a2041185658c1febf71d073291fdf66817972be5fb2a43.jpg) ![](images/82200522403da3e8e9d4e0618aeb52de258a03e12c50559b068743df37aefaa4.jpg) ![](images/19753875ee264b23ab81080154f3f4213874b7c069a5a92decede33a1b90f1ac.jpg) DOUBLE SORWE Of Troilus to tellen, That was the king Priamus sone of Troye, In lovinge, how his aventures fellen fro wo to wele, and after out of joye, My purpos is, er that I parte fro ye. Thesiphone, thou help me for tendyte To thee clepe I, thou goddesse of torment, Thou cruel furie, sorwing ever in peyne; Help me, that am the sorwful instrument That helpeth lovers, as I can, to pleynel for wel sit it, the sothe for to seyne, A woful wight to han a drery fere, And, to a sorwful tale, a sory chere. for I, that god of Loves servaunts serve, Ne dar to Love, for myn unlyklinesse, Preyen for speed, al sholde I therforsterve So fer am I fro hís help in derknesse; But nathelees, if this may doon gladnesse To any lover, and hís cause avayle, Have he my thank, and myn be thís travaylet But ye loveres, that bathen in gladnesse, If any drope of pitee in yow be, Remembreth yow on passed hevinesse That ye han felt, and on the adversitee Of othere folk, and thenketh how that ye Han felt that Love dorste yow displese; Or ye han wonne him with to greet an ese. And preyeth for hem that ben in the cas Of Troilus, as ye may after here, That love hem bringe in hevene to solas, And eek for me preyeth to God so dere, That I have might to shewe, in som manere, Swich peyne and wo as Loves folk endure, In Troilus unsely aventure.
