And biddeth eek for hem that been despeyred In love, that never nil recovered be, And eek for bem that falsly been apeyred Thorugh wikked tonges, be it he or she; Thus biddeth God, for his benignitee, Tograuntehem soneoutof this worldtopace, That been despeyred out of Loves grace. And biddeth eek for hem that been at ese, That God hem graunte ay good perseveraunce, And sende hem might hír ladíes so to plese, That it to Love be worship and plesaunce. for so hope I my soule best avaunce, To preye for hem that Loves servaunts be, And wryte hir wo, and live in charitee. And for to have of hem compassíoun As though I were hir owene brother dere. Now herkeneth with a gode entencioun, for now wol I gon streight to my matere, In whiche ye may the double sorwes here Of Troilus, in loving of Criseyde, And how that she forsook him er she deyde. <table><tr><td></td><td></td><td>Cis wel wist, how that the Grekes stronge In armes with a thousand shippes wente To Troyewardes, and the cite longe Assegeden neigh ten yeer er they stente, And, in diverse wyse and oon entente, The ravisshing to wreken of Eleyne, By Paris doon, they wrought- en al hir peyne. Now fil it so, that in the toun ther was Dwellinge a lordof greetauc- toritee, H gret devyn that cleped was</td></tr></table> ## Calkas, That in science so expert was, that he Knew wel that Troye sholde destroyed be, By answere of hís god, that highte thus, Daun Phebus or Hpollo Delphicus. So whan this Calkas knew by calculinge, And eek by answere of this Hppollo, That Grekes sholden swich a peple bringe, Thorugh which that Troye moste been fordo, He caste anoon out of the toun to go; for wel wiste he, by sort, that Troye sholde Destroyed been, ye, wolde whoso nolde. And in birhoussheabood ot wich eynce As to hir honour nede was to holde; And whyl she was dwellinge in that cite Kepte hir estat, and bothe of yonge an ful wel beloved,and wel men of hir to I rede it nought; therfore I lete it Chis Troilus, as he was wont to gyde le on every syde e ladyes of the toun, Bibo there, for no devocioun hadde he to noon, to reven bim bis reste, That this be sooth, hath preved and doth yet; for this trowe I ye knowen, alle or some, Men reden not that folk han gretter wit Than they that han be most with love ynome; And strengest folk ben therwith overcome, The worthiest and grettest of degree; This was, and is,and yet men shalit see. for which, for to departen softely Took purpos ful this forknowinge wyse, And to the Grekes ost ful prively He stal anoon; and they, in curteys wyse, Him deden bothe worship and servyse, In trust that he hath conning hem to rede In every peril which that is to drede. The thinges fellen, as they doon of werre, Bitwixen bem of Troye and Grekes ofte And eft the Grekes foundenno thing soft The folk of Troye; and thus fortune onlofte And under eft, gan bem to wheelen both After hir cours, ay whyl they were And in bis walk ful fast he gan to wayten If knight or squyer of his companye Gan for to syke, or lete bis eyen bayten On any woman that he coude aspye; he wolde smyle, and holden it folye, And seye him thus: God wot, she slepeth softe for love of thee, whan thou tornest ful oftel And trewelich it sit wel to be so foralderwysest han therwith ben plesed; Hnd tbey that ban ben aldermost in wo With love ban ben conforted most andesed; And ofte it hath the cruel herte apesed, Hnd worthy folk maad worthier of name, And causeth most to dreden vyce and shame. The noyse up roos, whan it was first aspyed, Thorugh al the toun, and generally was spoken Chat Calkas traytor fled was, and allyed With bem of Grece; and casten to ben wroken On bim that falsly badde his feith so broken; And seyden, he and al hís kin at ones Ben worthy for to brennen, fel and bones. But how this toun com to destruccioun Ne falleth nought to purpos me to tell for it were here a long disgressio fro my matere, and yow to longe dwelle But the Troyane gestes, as they felle, In Omer, Or in Dares, or in Dyte Whoso that can, may rede hem as they wryte Ihave herd told, pardieux, of your livinge, Velovers,and your lewede observaunces, labour folk ban in winninge Of love, and, in the keping, which doutaunces; And whan your preye is lost, wo and penaunces; Overrey foles nyce and blinde be ye; Ther nís not oon can war by other be. Now sith it may not goodly be withstonde, And is a thing so vertuous in kinde, Refuseth not to Love for to be bonde, Sin, as himselven list, he may yow binde. The yerde is bet that bowen wole and winde Than that that brest; and therfor I yow rede To folwen him that so wel can yow lede. Now hadde Calkas left, in thís meschaunce, Hlunwist of this false and wikked dede, His doughter, which that was in gret penaunce, for of bir lyf she was ful sore in drede, As she that niste what was best to rede; for bothe a widowe was she, and allone Of any freend, to whom she dorste bir mone. But though that Grekes hem of Troyesheten And hir citee bisegede alaboute, Hir olde usage wolde they As for to honoure hir goddes ful devo But aldermost in bonour, out of dou They hadde a relik bight Palladior That was hir trist aboven everich And with that word he gan caste up the browe, Hscaunc is this nought wysly spoken? od of love gan loken rowe pyt, and shoop for to ben wroken; n his bowe nas nat broken; for sodeynly he hit him at the fulle; And yet as proud a pekok can he pulle. But for to tellen forth in Special Hs of this kinges sone of which I tolde, And leten other thing collateral, Of bim thenke I my tale for to holde, Botbe of bis jove, and of his cares colde; And al his werk, as touching this matere, for I it gan, I wil therto refere. Críseyde was thís lady name aright; As to my dome, in al Troyes citee Nas noon so fair, for passing every wight So aungellyk was hir natyf beautee, That lyk a thing inmortal semed she, Hs doth an hevenish parfit creature, That doun were sent in scorning of nature. And so bifel, whan comen was the tyme Of Aperil, whan clothed is the med With newe grene, of lusty And swote smellen floures whyte In sondry wyses shewed, as Irede The folk of Troye hir observaunces olde, Palladiones feste for to holde. Oblínde world, O blinde entencioun1 How ofte falleth al theffect contraire rye and foul presumpcioun; caught is proud, and caught is debonaire. This Troilus is clomben on the staire, And litel weneth that he moot descenden. But al day fayleth thing that fooles wenden. Withinne the temple he wente him forth pleyinge, This Troilus, of every wight aboute, On this lady and now on that lokinge, berso sbe were of toune, or of withoute: And upon cas bifel, that thorugh a route His eye perced, and so depe it wente, Til on Criseyde it smoot, and ther it stente. This lady, which that alday berde at ere Hir fadres shame, hís falsnesse and tresoun, Wel nigh out of hir wit for sorwe and fere, In widewes habit large of samit broun, On knees she fil biforn Ector adoun; With pitous voys, and tendrely wepinge, His mercy bad, birselven excusinge. And to the temple, in al hir beste wyse, In general, ther wente many a wight, To herknen of Palladion the servyse; And namely, so many a lusty knight, So many a lady fresh and mayden bright, ful wel arayed, bothe moste and leste, Ye, bothe for the seson and the feste. As proude Bayard gínneth for to skippe Out of the wey, so priketh him bis corn, Til hea lash have of the longe whippe, Than thenketh he: Though I praunce al biforn first in the trays, ful fat and newe shorn, Yet am I but an bors, and horses lawe Imoot endure, and with my feres drawe. And sodeynly he wex therwith astoned, And gan bire bet bibolde in thrifty wyse: O mercy, God1 thoughte he, wher hastow woned, That art so fair and goodly to devyse? Therwith his herte gan to sprede and ryse, And softe sighed, lest men mighte him here, And caughte ayein bis firste pleyinge chere. Now was thís Ector pítous of nature, And saw that she was sorwfully bigoon, And that she was so fair a creature; Of his goodnesse he gladed hir anoon, And seyde: Lat your fadres treson goon forth with mischaunce, and ye yourself, in joye, Dwelleth with us, whyl you good list, in Troye. Among thise othere folk was Criseyda, In widewes babite blak; but nathelees, Right as our firste lettre is now an A In beautee first so stood she, makelees; Hir godly looking gladede al the prees. Nas never seyn thing to ben preysed derre. Nor under cloude blak so brighta sterre She nas not with the leste of hir stature, But alle hir limes so wel answeringe Weren to womanbode, that creature Was never lasse mannish in seminge. And eek the pure wyse of here meninge Shewede wel, that men might in hir gesse Honour, estat,and wommanly noblesse. And al thonour that men may doon yow bave, As ferforth as your fader dwelled bere, Ye shul han, and your body shal men save, Hs fer as I may ought enquere or bere. And she him thonked with ful bumble chere, And ofter wolde, and it hadde ben his wille And took hir leve, and hoom, and held hir stille. So ferde it by this fers and proude knight; Though he a worthy kinges sone were, And wendenothing hadde had swiche might Avens bis wil that sholde his herte stere, Yet with a look bis herte wex afere, That he, that now was most in pryde above, Wexsodeynly most subget unto love. Hs was Críseyde, as folk seyde everichoon That hir bibelden in hir blake wede; And yet she stood ful lowe and stillealloon Bibinden othere folk, in litel brede, And neigh the dore, ay under shames drede, Símple of atyr, and debonaire of chere, With ful assured loking and manere. forthy ensample taketh of this man, Vewyse, proude, and worthy folkes alle, To scornen Love, which that so sone can The freedom of your hertes to him thralle; for ever it was, and ever it shal bifalle, That Love is he that alle thing may binde; for may no man fordo the lawe of kindé. To Troilus right wonder wel withalle Gan for to lyke bir mening and bir chere, Wbich somdel deynous was, for she leet falle Hir look a lite aside, in swich manere, Hscaunces: batI may I not stonden bere? And after that hir loking gan she lighte, That never thoughte him seen so good a sighte Troilus and And of hír look in hím ther gan to quíken Criseyde. So greet desir, and swich affeccioun, LiberI. That in bis hertes botme gan to stiken Of hir his fixe and depe impressioun: And though he erst hadde poured up and doun, He was tho glad bis hornes in to shrinke; Annethes wiste he how to loke or winke. Lo, he that leet himselven so konninge, And scorned hem that loves peynes dryen, Was ful unwar that love hadde his dwellinge Withinne the subtile stremes of hir yën That sodeynly him thoughte he felte dyen, Right with hir look, the spirit in his herte; Blessed be love, that thus can folk converte1 She, this in blak, lykinge to Troilus, Over alle thing he stood for to bibolde; Ne his desir, ne wherfor he stood thus, He neither chere made, ne worde tolde; But from afer, hís maner for to holde, On other thing his look somtyme he caste, And eft on hir, whyl that servyse laste. for love bigan bis fetheres so to lyme, That wel unnethe unto bis folk be feyned That othere besye nedes bim destrayned; But bad his folk to goon wher that hem more that I it drinke. foray And whan that he in chaumbre was allone, He doun upon bis beddes feet bim sette, And first he gan to syke and eft to grone, And thoughte ay on bir so, withouten lette That, as he sat and wook, bis spirit mette That he hir saw a temple,andal the wyse Right of hir loke, and gan it newe avyse. And if that at myn owene lust I brenne, fro whennes cometh my wailing and my pleynte? ne why unwery that I feynte. O quike deeth, o swete harm so queynte, How may of thee in me swich quantitee, But if that I consente that it be? And after this, not fulliche al awhaped, Out of the temple al esiliche he wente, Repentinge him that he hadde ever yjaped Of loves folk, lest fully the descente Of scorn fille on himself; but, wbat he mente, Lest it were wist on any maner syde, Hís wo he gan díssimulen and hyde. And if that I consente, I wrongfully Compleyne, ywis; thus possed to and fro Alsterelees withinne a boot am I Amid the see, bytwixen windes two, That in contrarie stonden evermo. Allas what is this wonder maladye? for hete of cold, for cold of hete, I dye. And seyde: Lord, so ye live al in lest, Ye loveres1 for the conningest of yow, That serveth most ententiflich and best, Hím tít as often harm therof as prow; Your hyre is quit ayein, ye, God wot how1 Nought wel for wel, but scorn for good servyse; In feith, your ordre is ruled in good wyse1 Thus gan be make a mirour of bis minde, In which he saugh al hoolly hir figure And that he wel coude in bis herte It was to him a right good avent To love swich oon,and if be dide bis cur To serven hir, yet mighte he falle in grace Or elles, for oon of hir servaur But tak this, that ye loveres ofte eschuwe, Or elles doon of good entencioun, ful ofte thy lady wole it misconstrue, And deme it harm in hir opinioun; And yet if she, for other enchesoun, Be wrooth, than shalt thou han a groyn anoon: Lord1 wel is him that may be of yow oon1 In nounscerteyn ben alle your observaunces, Butit a sely fewe poyntes be; Ne nothing asketh so grete attendaunces Hs doth your lay, and that knowe alle ye; But that is not the worste, as mote I thee; But, tolde I yow the worste poynt, I leve, Al seyde I sooth, ye wolden at me grevel Al feyneth he in lust that he sojorneth; And al his chere and speche also he borneth; And ay, of loves servants every whyle, Hímself to wrye, at hem he gan to smyle. han he was fro the temple thus departed, He streyght anoon unto his paleys torneth, Right with hir look thurghsshoten and thurghdarted, Ye wolden on me rewe er that I deyde1 My dere berte, allas1 myn hele and And lyf is lost, but ye wole on me And to the god of love thus seyde he that oughte youres be. lord, that han me brought to this; goddesse or womman, ywis, She be, I noot, which that ye do me serve; But as hir man I wole ay live and sterve. Imagininge that travaille nor grame Ne mighte, for so goodly oon,be lorn As she, ne hím for his desir ne shame Al were it wist, but in prys and upborn Of alle lovers wel more than biforn; Thus argumented he in his ginninge, ful unavysed of his wo cominge. The sharpe shoures felle of armes preve, That Ector or his othere bretheren diden, Ne made him only therfore ones meve; And yet was he, wherso men wente or riden, founde oon the best, and lengest tyme abiden Ther peril was, and dide eek such travayle In armes, that to thenke it was mervayle. Alle othere dredes weren from bim fledde, Bothe of the assege and his savacioun Ne in bim desyr noon othere fownes bredde But arguments to this conclusioun, That she on him wolde han compassioun, And he to be bir man, wbyl he may dure; Lo, here his lyf, and from the deeth his curel Thus took he purpos loves craft to suwe, And thoughte he wolde werken prively first, to hyden his desir in from every wight yborn, alsoutrely But he mighte ought recovered be therby; Remembring him, that love to wyde yblowe Yelt bittre fruyt, though swete seed be sowe. But for non hate he to the Grekes badde, Ne also for the rescous of the toun Ne made hím thus in armes for to madde, But only, lo, for this conclusioun, To lyken hir the bet for his renoun; fro day to day in armes so he spedde, That alle the Grekes as the deeth him dredde. γe stonden in hire eyen mightily nto your vertu digne; if my servyse or I yow, so beth to me benigne; formyn estat royal bere I resigne Into hir hond, and with ful humble chere Bicome hír man, as to my lady dere. In him ne deyned sparen blood royal The fyrof love, wherfro God me blesse, him forbar in no degree, for al vertu or his excellent prowesse; But held him as his thral lowe in distresse, And brende him so in sondry wyse ay newe, That sixty tyme a day he loste his hewe. And over al this, yet muchel more he thoughte What for to speke, and what to holden inne, And what to arten hir to love he sougt And on a song anoonsright to biginne, And gan loude on his sorwe for to winne; for with good hope he gan fully assente Criseyde for to love, and nought repente. So muche, day by day, hís owene thought, for lust to hír, gan quiken and encrese, That every other charge he sette at nought; forthy ful ofte, his hote fyr to cese, Toseen hir goodly look he gan to prese; for therby to ben esed wel he wende, Anday the ner be was, the more he brende. And fro this forth tho refte him love his sleep, And made bis mete his foo; and eek his sorwe Gan multiplye, that, whoso toke keep, It shewed in his hewe, bothe eve and morwe; Therfor a title he gan him for to borwe Of other syknesse, lest of him men wende That the hote fyr of love him brende. And of his song nought only the sentence, As writ myn autour called Lollius, But pleynly, save our tonges difference, I dar wel sayn, in al that Troilus Seyde in his song; lol every word right thus Hs I shal seyn; and whoso listit here, Lol next thís vers, he may it finden here. And seyde, he hadde a fever and ferde amís; But how it was, certayn, can I not seye, If that his lady understood not this, Or feyned hir she niste, oon of the tweye; But wel I rede that, by no maner weye, Ne semed it as that she of him roughte, Nor of his peyne, or whatsoever he thoughte. But than fel to this Troilus such wo, That he was wel neigh wood; for ay his drede Was this, that she som wight had loved so That never of bim she wolde have taken bede; for whiche him thoughte he felte his herte blede. Ne of his wo ne dorste he not biginne To tellen it, for al this world to winne. ![](images/842735654cfd46b3c9b681fcb53531c777c764410b85d9bb180452df9be6ad59.jpg) Cantus Troílí. ![](images/bcd7b7d7e171b68540d3c5cd79b7a9483d900f9ecf5d977e4e2c9422f2e909bc.jpg) f no love is, O God, what fele I so? And if love is, what thing and whiche is he? If it be wikke, a wonder thinketh me, Whenne every torment and adversitee Eek of the day ther passed nought an houre That to bimself a thousand tyme he seyde: Good goodly, to whom serve I and laboure, As I best can, now wolde God, Criseyde, If love be good, from whennes comth my wo? foray the ner the fyr, the hotter is, This, trowe I, knoweth al this companye. But were he fer or neer, I dar seye this, Bynight or day, for wysdom or folye, His herte, which that is his brestes yë, Was ay on hir, that fairer was to sene Than ever was Eleyne or Polixene. What wole now every lover seyn of thee, If this be wist, but ever in thyn absence Laugben in scorn, and seyn: Lo, ther gooth he, But whanne he hadde a space fro hís care, Thus to himself ful ofte he gan to pleyne; He sayde: O fool, now art thou in the snare, That whilom japedest at loves peyne; Now artow hent, now gnaw thyn owene cheyne; Thou were ay wont eche lovere reprehende Of thing fro which thou canst thee nat defende. Troilus and That is the man of so gret sapience, Criseyde. Chat held us loveres leest in reverence1 LiberI. Now, thonked be God, he may goon in the daunce Of hem that Love list febly for to avauncel But, O thou woful Troilus, God wolde, Sin thow most loven thurgh thy destinee, That thow beset were on swich oon that sholde Knowe al thy wo, al lakkede hir pitee: But al so cold in love, towardes thee, Chy lady is, as frost in winter mone, And thou fordoon,as snow in fyr is sone God wolde I were aryved in the port Of deeth, to which my sorwe wil me lede1 A, Lord, to me it were a greet comfort; Chen were I quit of languisshing in drede. for by myn hidde sorwe yblowe on brede I shal bijaped been a thousand tyme More than that fool of whos folye men ryme. thduhnge Wol thee disese, and I mot nedes deye Therfor go wey, ther is nor But if thou wene I be thus syk for drede It is not so, and therfor scorne nought; Ther is another thing I take of hede But though that I now telle thee it ne leste, Be thou nought wrooth, I hyde it for the bes But now help God, and ye, swete, for whom I pleyne, ycaught, ye, never wight so faste1 O mercy, dere herte, and belp me from The deeth, for I, whyl that my lyf may laste, More than myself wol love yow to my laste. And with som freendly look gladeth me, swete, Though never more thing ye me bibete1 Thise wordes and ful manye another to He spak, and called ever in his compleynte Hir name, for to tellen hir his wo, Til neigh that he in salte teres dreynte. Al was for nought, she herde nought his pleynte; And whan that he bithoughte on that folye, A thousand fold his wo gan multiplye. This Pandare, that neigh malt for wo and routhe, fuloften seyde: Hllas what may this be Now freend, quod he, if ever love or tr Hath been, or is, bitwixen thee an Ne do thou never swiche a cru To hyde fro thy freend so greet a care Wostow nought wel that it am I Biwaylíng in his chambre thus allone, A freend of his, that called was Pandare Com ones in unwar, and herde him grone, And sey bis freend in swich distresse and care: Allas1 quod he, who causeth al this fare? O mercy, God1 what unbap may this mene? Han now thus sone Grekes maad yow lene? Ve, Croilus,now berke, quod Dandare, Though I be nyce; it happeth ofte so, By good counseyl can kepe his freend therfro. Cheras he fel that coude loke wyde; Afool may eek a wys man ofte gyde. Or hastow som remors of conscience, And art now falle in som devocioun, And waylest for thy sinne and thyn offence, And bast for ferde caught attricioun? God save hem that biseged han our toun, And so can leye our jolyte on presse, And bring our lusty folk to holinesse1 Awhetston is no kerving instrument, And yet it maketh sharpe kervingstolis. w woost that I have ought miswent, ewethou that, forswich thing to theescoleis; Chus ofte wyse men ben war by folis. If thou do so, thy wit is wel biwared; By his contrarie is every thing declared. for how might ever sweetnesse have be knowe To him that never tasted bitternesse? ever was in sorwe or som distresse; Eek whyt by blak, by shame eek worthinesse, Ech set by other, more for other semeth; As men may see; and so the wyse it demeth. I wole parten with thee al thy peyne, If it be so I do thee no comfort, Hs it is freendes right,sooth for to seyne, To entreparten wo, as glad desport. I have, and shal, for trewe or fals report In wrong and right yloved thee al my lyve Hyd not thy wo fro me, but telle it The wyse seyth: Wo him that is allone, for, and he falle, he hath noon belp to ryse And sith thou hast a felawe, tel thy mone; for this nis not, certeyn, the nexte wyse To winnen love, as techen us the wyse, To walwe and wepe as Niobe the quene, Whos teres yet in marbel been ysene. And witeth wel, that bothe two ben vyces, Mistrusten alle, or elles alle leve; But wel I woot, the mene of it no vyce is, for for to trusten sum wight is a preve Of trouthe, and fortby wolde I fayn remeve Thy wrong conceyte, & do thee som wight triste, Thy wo to telle; and tel me, if thee liste. These wordes seyde he for the nones alle, That with swich thing he mighte him angry maken, And with an angre don his sorwe falle, Hs for the tyme, and his corage awaken; But wel he wiste, as fer as tonges spaken, Ther nas a man of gretter hardinesse Than he, ne more desired worthinesse. Than gan this sorwful Troilus to syke And seyde him thus: God leveit bemyb To telle it thee; for, sith it may thee lyke Yet wole I telle it, though myn herte breste; And wel wot I thou mayst do me no reste But lest thow deme I truste not to thee Now herkne, freend, for thus it stant with me Sith thus of two contraries is a lore, I, that h Greva in love so ofte assayed ghte conne, and wel the more yllen thee of that thou art amayed. Eek thee ne oughte nat ben yvel apayed, Though I desyre with thee for to bere Thyn bevy charge; it shal the lasse dere. Therfore, as freend fullich in me assure, And tel me plat what is thyn enchesoun, And final cause of wo that ye endure; for douteth nothing, myn entencioun Nis nought to yow of reprehencioun To speke as now, for no wight may bireve A man to love, til that him list to leve. What cas, quod Troilus, or what aventure Lat be thy weping and thy drerinesse, And lat us lissen wo with other speche; So may thy woful tyme seme lesse. Delyte not in wo thy wo to seche, As doon thise foles that hir sorwes eche With sorwe, whan they han misaventure, And listen nought to seche hem other cure. I woot wel that it fareth thus by me ther Parys an herdesse, I was Oënone, pleynt of hir hevinesse: ay the lettre that she wroot, I gesse? Nay,never yet, ywis, quod Troilus. Now, quod Pandare, berkneth; it was thus: Men seyn: To wreccheis consolacioun To have another felawe in his peyne That oughte wel ben our opinioun, for, bothe thou and I, of love we pleyne; So ful of sorwe am I, soth for to seyne, That certeynly no more harde grace May sitte on me, forwhy ther is no space. Love, ayeins the which whoso defendeth Himselven most, him alderlest avayleth, With desespeir so sorwfully me offendeth, That streyght unto the deeth myn berte sayleth. Therto desyr so brenningly me assaylleth, That to ben slayn it were a gretter joye To me than king of Grece been and Troyel Phebus, that first fond art of medicyne, Quod she, and coude in every wightes care Remede and reed, by berbes be knew fyne, Vet to himself his conninge was ful bare; for love hadde him so bounden in a snare, Alfor the doughter of the kinge Admete, That al his craft ne coude his sorwe bete Suffiseth this, my fulle freend Pandare, That I have seyd, for now wostow my wo; And for the love of God, my colde care So hyd it wel, I telle it never to mo; for harmes mighte folwen, mo than two, If it were wist; but be thou in gladnesse, And lat me sterve, unknowe, of my distresse. If God wole thou art not agast of me, Lest I wolde of thy lady thee bigyle, Thow wost thyself whom that I love, pardee, Hs I best can, gon sithen longe whyle. And sith thou wost I do it for no wyle, And sith I am be that thou tristest most, Tel me sumwhat, sin al my wo thou wost. How hastow thus unkindely and longe Híd thís fro me, thou fool? quod Pandarus; Paraunter thou might after swich oon longe, That myn avys anoon may helpen us. This were a wonder thing, quod Troilus, Thou coudest never in love thyselven wisse; How devel maystow bringen me to blisse? Right so fare I, unbappily for me; Ilove oon best, and that me smerteth sore; And yet, paraunter, can I rede thee, And not myself; repreve me no more. Ihaveno cause, I woot wel, for to sore As doth an hauk that listeth for to pleye, But to thyn help yet somwbat can I seye. And of o thing right siker maystow be, Chat certayn, for to deyen in the peyne, That I shal nevermo discoveren thee; Ne,by my trouthe, I kepe nat restreyne Thee fro thy love, thogh that it were Eleyne, That is thy brotheres wyf, if ich it wiste; Be what she be, and love hir as thee liste. Yet Troilus, for al this, no word seyde, But longe be lay as stille as he ded were And after this with sykinge he abreyde, And to Pandarus voys be lente hís ere, And up his eyen caste he, that in fere Was Pandarus, lest that in frenesye He sholde falle, or elles sone dye: And cryde, Awakel ful wonderly and sharpe; What? slombrestow as in a lytargye? Or artow lyk an asse to the harpe, That hereth soun, whan men the strenges plye, But in bis minde of that no melodye May sinken, hím to glade, for that he So dul is of his bestialitee? Troilus and Hnd with that Pandare of his wordes stente; Criseyde. But Troilus yet him no word answerde, LiberI. forwby to telle nas not his entente To never no man, for whom that he so ferde. foritis seyd: Man maketh ofte a yerde With which the makeris himself ybeten In sondry maner as thise wyse treten, But ones niltow, for thy coward berte, And for tbyn ire and folish wilfulnesse, Ne to thyn owene help do bisinesse What womman coude love swich a wre And namely, in his counseyl tellinge That toucheth love that oughte be secree; for of himself it wolde ynough outspringe, But if that it the bet governed be. Eek somtymeit is craft to seme flee fro thing which in effect men hunte faste; Al this gan Troilus in his herte caste. forif hir wheelstinteanything to torne, Than cessed she fortune anoon to be: What wostowif hir mutabilitee Rightas thyselven list, wol doon by thee, Daraunter, thou hast cause for to singel What may she demen other of thy deeth If thou thus deye, and she not why itis, But that for fere is yolden up thy breeth for Grekes han biseged us, ywis? Lord, which a thank than shaltow han of thist Thus wol she seyn, and al the toun at ones: The wrecche is deed, the devel have his But nathelees, whan he had herd bim crye Hwakel he gan to syke wonder sore, And seyde: freend, though that I stille lye, Iam not deef; now pees, and cry no more; for I have herd thy wordes and thy lore; But suffre me my mischef to biwayle, for thy proverbes may me nought avayle. And therfor wostow what I thee beseche? Lat be thy wo and turning to the grounde; for whoso list have helping of his leche, Tohim bihoveth first unwrye his wounde. Co Cerberus in belle ay be I bounde, Wereit for my suster, al thy sorwe, By my wil, she sholde al be thyn tomorwe. Chou maystallonebereweeandcendkele But, love a woman that she woot it nou And she wol quyte that thou shalt not Anknowe, unkist, and lost that is unso Twenty winter that his lady wis That never yet his lady mo Loke up,I seye, and tel me what she is Anoon, that I may goon aboute thy nede; Knowe ich birought? for my love, tel me this; Than wolde I hopen rather for to spede. Tho gan the veyne of Troilus to blede, for he was hit, and wex al reed for shame; Ahal quod Pandare, here biginneth game1 Nor other cure canstow noon for me. Eek I nil not be cured, I wol deye; Wbat knowe I of the quene Niobe? Lat be thyne olde ensaumples, I thee preye. Noquod tho Pandarus, therfore I seye, Swich is delyt of foles to biwepe Hir wo, but seken bote they ne kepe, hat? shulde be therfor fallen in despeyr, Or be recreaunt for bis owene tene, Or sleen himself, al be his lady fayr? Nay, nay, but ever in oon be fresh and grene To serve and love his dere hertes quene, And thenke it is a guerdoun bir to serve A thousandsfold more than be can deserve And with that word he gan bim for to shake, And seyde: Theef, thou shalt hir name telle. y Troilus for to quake en sholde han lad him into helle, And seyde: Allasl of al my wo the welle, Than is my swete fo called Criseyde1 And wel nigh with the word for fere he deyde. Now knowe I that ther reson in thee fayleth. But tel me, if I wiste what she were for whom that thee al this misaunter ayleth Dorstestow that I tolde hir in hir ere Thy wo, sith thou darst not thyself for fere, And hir bisoughte on thee to han som routhe? by, nay, quod he, by God and by my trouthe Hnd of that word took hede Troilus, And thoughte anoon what folye be was inn And how that sooth bim seyde Pandarus That for to sleen himself mighte henot winn But bothe doon unmanbod anda sinne And of his deeth his lady nought to wyte; for of his wo, God woot, she knew fullyte, And whan that Pandare herde hir name nevene, Lord, he was glad, and seyde: freend so dere, Now fare aright, for Joves name in hevene, hath biset thee wel, be of good chere; forof good name and wysdom and manere She hath ynough, and eek of gentilesse; Ifshe be fayr, thow wost thyself, I gesse. bat? not as bísíly, quod Pandarus, Hs though myn owene lyf lay on this nede? No, certes, brother, quod this Troilus. Hnd why?for that thou sholdest never spede. ostow that wel? Ye, that is out of drede, Quod Troilus, for al that ever ye conne, She nil to noon swich wrecche as I be wonne. And with that thought he gan ful sore syke, And seyde: Hllas 1 what is me best to do To whom Pandare answerde thee lyke The best is that thou telle me thy wo; And have my trouthe, but thou it finde so, I be thy bote, or that it be ful longe, To peces do me drawe, and sithen hongel NeInever saw a more bountevous Of hirestat, ne a gladder, ne of speche Afreendlier, ne a more gracious forto do wel, ne lasse hadde nede to seche What for to doon; and al this bet to eche, In bonour, to as fer as she may strecche, Akinges herte semeth by hires a wrecche. Quod Pandarus: Hllas1 what may this be, Chat thou despeyred art thus causelees? What? liveth not thy lady? benedicite How wostow so that thou art gracelees? Swich yvelis not alwey botelees. Why, put not impossible thus thy cure, Sin thing to come is ofte in aventure. I graunte wel that thou endurest wo Hs sharp as doth he, Ticius, in helle, Whos stomak foules tyren evermo Chat highte volturis,as bokes telle. But I may not endure that thou dwelle In so unskilful an opinioun That of thy wo is no curacioun. 478 Ye, so thou seyst, quod Troilus tho,allas But, God wot, it is not the rather so; ful hard were it to belpen in this cas, for wel finde I that fortune is my fo, Ne alle the men that ryden conne or go May of hir cruel wheel the harm withstonde; for, as hir list, she pleyeth with free and bonde. And forthy loke of good comfort thou be; forcerteinly, the firste poynt is this Of noble corage and wel ordeynè, Aman to have pees with himself, ywís; Sooughtest thou, for nought but good it is Coloven wel, and in a worthy place; Theeoughte not to clepe it hap, but grace. Quod Pandarus: Than blamestow fortune for thou art wrooth, ye, now at erst I see; Wostow nat wel that fortune is commune To every maner wight in som degree? And yet thou hast this comfort, lo, pardee That, as hir joyes moten oversgoon, So mote hir sorwes passen everichoon. And also thenk, and therwíth glade thee, Chat sith thy lady vertuous is al, So folweth it that ther is som pitee Amonges alle thise othere in general; And forthy see that thou, in special, Requere nought that is ayein hir name; for vertue streccheth not himself to shame. But wel is me that ever I was born, That thou biset art in so good a place; for by my trouthe, in love I dorste have sworn, Thee sholde never han tid thus fayr a grace And wostow why? for thou were wont to chace At love in scorn, and for despyt him calle Seynt Idiot, lord of thise foles alle How often hastow maad thy nice japes, And seyd, that loves servants everichone Of nycetee ben verray Goddes apes; And some wolde monche hir mete alone, Ligging abedde, and make hem for to grone; And som, thou seydest, hadde a blaunche fevere, And preydest God he sholde never kevere1 And some of hem toke on hem, for the colde, More than ynough, so seydestow ful ofte; And some han feyned ofte tyme, and tolde How that they wake, whan they slepen softe; And thus they wolde han brought hemself alofte, And nathelees were under at the laste; Thus seydestow,and japedest ful faste. Yet seydestow, that, for the more part, These loveres wolden speke in general And thoughten that it was a siker art, for fayling, for to assayen overal Now may I jape of thee, if that I shall But nathelees, though that I sholde deye, That thou art noon of tho, that dorste I seye. Now beet thy brest, and sey to god of love: Thy grace, lord1 for now I me repente If I mis spak, for now myself I love Thus sey with al thyn herte in good entente. Quod Troilus: H1 lord1 I me consente, And pray to thee my japes thou foryive, And I shal nevermore whyl I live. Thow seyst wel, quod Pandare, & now I hope Chat thou the goddes wraththe hast alapesed; And sithen thou hast wepen many a drope, And seyd swich thing wherwith thy god is plesed, Now wolde never god but thou were esed; And think wel, she of whom rist al thy wo Hereafter may thy comfort been also. for thilke ground, that bereth the wedes wikke, Bereth eek thise holsom berbes,as ful ofte Next the foule netle, rough and thikke, The rose waxeth swote and smothe and softe; And next the valey is the hil alofte; And next the derke night the glade morwe; And also joye is next the fyn of sorwe. Now loke that atempre be thy brydel, And, for the beste, ay suffre to the tyde, Or elles al ourlabouris on ydel; He hasteth wel that wysly can abyde; Be diligent, and trewe, and ay wel hyde. Be lusty, free, persevere in thy servyse, And al is wel, if thou werke in this wyse. Troilus and But he that parted is in every place Criseyde. Is nowher hool,as writen clerkes wyse; Liber I. What wonder is, though swich oon have no grace? Eek wostow how it fareth of som servyse? As plaunte a tre or herbe, in sondry wyse, And on the morwe pulle it up as blyve, No wonder is, though it may never thryve. And sith that god of love hath thee bistowed In place digne unto thy worthinesse Stond faste, for to good port hastow rowed; And of thyself, for any hevinesse, Hope alwey wel; for, but if drerinesse Or overshaste our bothe labour shende, I bope of this to maken a good ende. But, dere frend, how shalmyn wo ben lesse Til this be doon? and goode, eek telmett How wiltow seyn of me and my destresse Or nil not here or trowen how it is Al this drede I, and eek for the manere Of thee, hir eem, she nil no swich thing Quod Pandarus: Chou bastaful re are Lest that the cherl may falleout of themonel why, Lord1 I bate of thee thy nyce fare why, entremete of that thou bast to done for Goddes love, I bidde thee a bone, So lat me alone, and it shal be thy beste. Why, freend, quod he, now do right as thee leste. And wostow why I am the lasse afered Of this matere with my nece trete? for this have I herd seyd of wyse ylered: Was never man ne woman yet bigete That was unapt to suffren loves hete Celestial, or elles love of kinde forthy som grace I hope in hir to finde. And for to speke of hír in special, Hir beautee to bithinken and hir youthe, It sit hir nought to be celestial Hs yet, though that hir liste bothe and couthe; But trewely, it sete hir wel right nouthe H worthy knight to loven and cheryce, And but she do, I holde it for a vyce. But herke, Pandare, o word, for I nolde That thou in me wendest so greet folye That to my lady I desiren sh That toucheth harm or any vilenye for dredelees, me were lever dye Than she of me ought elles understode But that, that mighte sounen into gode Wherfore I am, and wol be, ay redy To peyne me to do yow this servyse; for bothe yow to plese thus hope I Herafterward; for ye beth bothe wyse, And conneit counseyl kepe in swich a wyse, That no man shal the wyser of it be; And so we may be gladed alle three. Tho lough this Pandare, & anoon answerde And I thy borw? fyi no wight dooth but so I roughte nought though that she stode & herd How that thou seyst; but farewel,I wol go. Adieul be gladi God spede us bothe twol Yif me this labour and this besinesse, And of my speed be thyn al that swetnesse, And, by my trouthe, I have right now of thee A good conceyt in my wit, as I gesse, And what it is, I wol now that thou see. I thenke, sith that love, of his goodnesse, Hath thee converted out of wikkednesse, That thou shalt be the beste post, I leve, Of al bís lay, and most hís foos togreve. for he bicom the frendlyeste wight, Che thriftieste and oon the beste knight, That in his tyme was, or mighte be. But Troilus lay tho no lenger doun, But up anoon upon his stede bay, Andin the feld he pleyde tho leoun; And in the toun his maner tho forth ay Ensample why, see now these wyse clerkes, That erren aldermost ayein a lawe, And ben converted from bir wikked werkes Thorugh grace of God, that list hem to him drawe, Tho Troilus gan doun on knees to falle And Pandare in bis armes bente faste, And seyde: Now, fy on the Grekes alle Yet, pardee, God shal helpe us at the laste; And dredelees, if that my lyf may laste, And God toforn, lo, som of hem shal smerte; And yet me athinketh that this avaunt meastertel Than arn they folk that han most God in awe, And strengestsfeythed been, I understonde, And conne an errour alderbest withstonde. ban Troilus had herd Pandare assented To been his help in loving of Criseyde, Wex of his wo,as who seyth, untormented, But hotter wex his love, and thus he seyde, With sobre chere, although his herte pleyde: Now blisful Venus helpe, er that I sterve, Of thee, Pandare, I may som thank deserve. 480 And now, Pandare, I can no more seye, But thou wys, thou wost, thou mayst, thouartal My lyf, my deeth, hool in thyn honde I le Help now, quod heYis, by my trouthe,I shal God yelde thee, freend, and this in special Quod Troilus, that thou me recomaunde To hir that to the deeth me may comaunde. Thís Pandarus tho, desirous to serve His fulle freend, than seyde in this manere: farwel, and thenk I wol thy thank deserve; Have here my trouthe, & that thou shalt wel here. And wente his wey, thenking on this matere, And how he best mighte hir beseche of grace, And finde a tyme therto, and a place. for every wight that hath an hous to founde Ne renneth nought the werk for to biginne With rakel hond, but he wol byde a stounde, And sende his hertes lyne out fro withinne Alderfirst his purpos for to winne. Al this Pandare in hís herte thoughte, And caste his werk ful wysly, or he wroughte. Dede were his japes and his crueltee, His beighe port and bís manere estraunge, d ech of tho gan for a vertu chaunge. Now lat us stínte of Troilus a stounde, That fareth lyk a man that hurt is sore, And is somdel of akinge of his wounde Ylissed wel, but heled no del more: And, as an esy pacient, the lore Abit of him that gooth aboute his cure; And thus he dryveth forth his aventure. Explicit Liber Primus. ![](images/136f8f4787a9e8f48e78ba5ae7c813717cabeac132c12ef5e029466c0d8aac4b.jpg) ![](images/59b3b9f0f0955c16a2daa9b2ea50636395ced2111d6ea9397f6af65090adb79b.jpg) CROILUS AND CRISEYDE LIBER SECANDAS. Incipit prohemium Secundi Libri. Of desespeyr that Troilus was inne: ut now of hope the calende ![](images/f244fc35f9fe350f7b76b9dd3dde61a2c315845ea91765dcdfb71c98fb211a93.jpg) Of THESE BLHKE WAES fOR TO sayle, wind, O wind, the weder ginneth clere; for in this see the boot hath swich travayle, Of my conning that unnethe I it stere: This see clepe I the tempestous matere O lady myn, that called art Cleo, Thou be my speed fro this forth, & my mus To ryme wel this book, til I have do; Me nedeth here noon other art to use. forwhy to every lovere I me excuse, That of no sentement I this endyte, But out of Latin in my tonge it wryte. Wherfore I nil have neíther thank ne blame Of al this werk, but pray yow mekely Disblameth me, if any word be lame, for as myn auctor seyde, so seye I Eek though I speke of love unfelingly, No wonder is, for it nothing of newe is; A blind man can nat juggen welin bewis. Ye knowe eek, that in forme of speche is chaunge Withinne a thousand yeer, and wordes tho That hadden prys, now wonder nyceand straunge Us thinketh hem; and yet they spake hem so, And spedde as wel in love as men now do; Eek for to winne love in sondry ages, In sondry londes, sondry ben usages. And forthy if it happe in any wyse, That here be any lovere in this place That herkeneth, as the story wol devyse, how Troilus com to hís lady grace, And thenketh, so nolde I nat love purchace, Or wondreth on hís speche and his doinge, Inoot; but it is me no wonderinge; for every wight which that to Rome went, Halt nat o path, or alwey o manere; Eek in some lond were al the gamen shent, If that they ferde in love as men don here, As thus, in open doíng or in chere, In visitinge, in forme, or seyde hir sawes; forthy men seyn, ech contree hath hís lawes. Eek scarsly been ther in this place three That han in love seyd lyk and doon in al; for to thy purpos this may lyken thee, And thee right nought, yet al is seyd or shal; Eek some men grave in tree, som in stoon wal, As it bitit; but sin I bave begonne, Myn auctor shal I folwen, if I conne. Explicit probemíum Secundi Librí. ![](images/44975987585c74930f0b1ab027d3687e96695d33904a0e443cf441135bff632e.jpg) N May, that moder is o monthes glade, Thatfressbefloures, blewe, and whyte,and rede, Ben quíke agayn, that winter dede made, And ful of bawme is fletinge every mede; ban Phebus doth his brighte bemes sprede Right in the whyte Bole, it so bitidde Hs I shal singe, on Mayes day the thridde, That Pandarus, for al his wyse speche, felte eek his part of loves shottes kene, That, coude he never so wel of loving preche, It made his hewe aday ful ofte grene; So shoop it, that him fil that day a tene In love, for wbich in wo to bedde he wente, And made, erít was day, ful many a wente. The swalwe Proignè, with a sorwful lay, Whan morwe com, gan make hir weymentinge, Wby she forshapen was; and ever lay Pandare abedde, half in a slomeringe Til she so neigh him made hir chiteringe Troilus and How Tereus gan forth hir suster take, Criseyde. That with the noyse of hir he gan awake; LiberII. And gan to calle, and dresse him up to ryse, Remembrínge hím his erand was to done from Troilus, and eek his greet empryse; And caste and knew in good plyt was the mone To doon viage, and took his wey ful sone Anto his neces paleys ther bisyde; Now Janus, god of entree, thou him gyde1 am It is a thir And he welmore vertu bath than might. Ye, boly God1 quod she, what thing is that? What? bet than swiche fyve? ey, na It sholde been; som jape, I tro Hs help me God Ye been the womman in this world livinge, Withoute paramours, to my witinge That I best love, and lothest am to greve, And that ye witen wel yourself, I leve. Whan he was come unto hís neces place, Wber is my lady? to hir folk seyde he; And they bim tolde; and be forth in gan pace, And fond, two othere ladyes sete and she Withinne a paved parlour; and they three Herden a mayden reden bem the geste Of the Sege of Thebes, whyl hem leste. By God, quod she, of Ector that is sooth; us the same thing trowe I OfTro tellen that he dooth And bereth bim bere at boom so gentilly To every wight, that al the prys hath he of hem that me were levest preysed be. Ywís, myn uncle, quod she, grant mercy; Your freendship have I founden ever yit; I am to no man holden trewely So muche as yow, and have so litel quit; And, with the grace of God, emforth my wit, As in my gilt I shal you never offende; And if I have er this, I wol amende. And I your borow, ne never shal, for me, This thing be told to yow, as mote I thry And why so, uncle myn? why so? quode By God, quod be, that wole I telleas b And ye it wiste, in al the toun of Troye; I jape nought, as ever have I joye Ve sey right sooth, ywís, quod Pandarus; for yesterday, whoso badde with bim been, hemight bave wondred upon Troilus; fornever yet so thikke a swarm of been Nefleigh, as Grekes fro him gonne fleen; And thorugh the feld, in every wightes ere, Thernas no cry but: Troilus is therel But, for the love of God, I yow beseche, As ye ben be that I most love and triste, Lat be to me your fremde maner speche, And sey to me, your nece, what yow liste: Hnd with that word bir uncle anoon hir kiste, And seyde: Gladly, leve nece dere, Tak it for good that I shal seye yow here. Quod Pandarus: Ma dame, God yow see, With al your book and al the companyel Ey, uncle myn, welcomé ywis, quod she And up she roos, and by the hond in bye She took him faste, and seyde: This night thrye, To goode mote it turne, of yow I mettel Hnd with that word she doun on bench him sette. Tho gan she wondren more than biforn A thousand fold, and doun bir eyen cas for never, sith the tyme that she was To knowe thing desired she so fa And with a syk she seyde bim at the laste Now, uncle myn, I nil yow nought displese, Nor axen more, that may do yow d Ye, nece, ye shal fare wel the bet, If God wole, al this yeer, quod Pandarus; But I am sory that I have yow let To berknen of your book ye preysen thus; for Goddes love, what seith it? tel it us. Is it of love? O, som good ye me lere1 Uncle, quod she, your maistresse is not here Now here, now there, be hunted hem so faste, Ther nas but Grekes blood; and Troilus, m be hurte, and hem alle doun he caste; bere he wente it was arayed thus: hewas bir deeth, and sheld and lyf for us; That as that day ther dorste noon withstonde, Whyl that he held his blody swerd in honde. With that she gan hir eyen doun to caste, And Pandarus to coghe gan a lyte, And seyde: Nece, alwey, lol to the laste, Howso it be that som men hem delyte With subtil art hir tales for to endyte, Yet for al that, in hir entencioun, Hír tale is al for som conclusioun. ## With that they gonnen laughe, and tho she seyde: So after this, with many wordes glade, And freendly tales, and with mery chere, Of this and that they pleyde, and gunnen wad In many an unkouth glad and deep matere, As freendes doon, whan they ben met yfere; Til she gan axen him bow Ector ferde, That was the tounes wal and Grekes yerde. Therto be is the freendlieste man Of grete estat, that ever I saw my lyve; ist, best felawsbipe can as him thinketh able for to thryve. I with that word tho Pandarus, as biyve, he took his leve, and seyde: I wol go henne: Nay, blame have I, myn uncle, quodshe thenne. And sithen thende is every tales strengthe, And thís matere is so bihovely, Wbat sholde I peynte or drawen it on lengthe To yow, that been my freend so feithfully Hnd with that word he gan right inwardly Bibolden hir, and loken on bir face, And seyde: On suche a mírour goode grace1 This romaunce is of Thebes, that we rede; And we han herd how that king Laius deyde Thurgh Edippus his sone,and al that dede; And here we stenten at these lettres rede, How the bisshop,as the book can telle, Amphiorax, fil thurgh the ground to helle. ful wel, I thanke it God, quod Pandarus Save in his arm he hath a litel wor And eek bis fresshe brother The wyse worthy Ector the secound In whom that every vertu list abounde As alle trouthe and alle gentillesse, Wysdom, honour, fredom, and worthinesse. What eyleth yow to be thus wery sone, of wommen? wol ye so? Nav, sitteth down; by God, I have to done h yow, to speke of wisdom er ye go. And every wight that was aboute hem tho, That herde that, gan fer awey to stonde, Whyl they two hadde al that hem liste in honde. Than thoughte he thus: If I my tale endyte Ought hard, or make a proces any whyle, She shal no savour han therin but lyte, And trowe I wolde hir in my wil bigyle. for tendre wittes wenen al be wyle Cheras they can nat pleynly understonde; forthy hir wit to serven wol I fonde: Quod Pandarus: Al this knowe I myselve, And al the assege of Thebes and the care; for herof been ther maked bokes twelve: But lat be this, and tel me how ye fare Do wey your barbe, and shew your face bare; Do wey your book, rys up, and lat us daunce, And lat us don to May som observaunce. In good feith, eem, quod she, that lyketh me; They faren wel, God save hem bothe two for trewely I holde it greet de A kinges sone in armes wel to do And been of good condiciouns therto; for greet power and moral vertu bere Is selde yseye in o persone yfere. @han that hír tale al brought was to an ende Of hire estat and of hir governaunce, Quod Pandarus: Now is it tyme I wende; But yet, I seye,aryseth, lat us daunce, And cast your widwes habit to mischaunce: Ahat list yow thus yourself to disfigure, Sith yow is tid thus fair an aventure? And loked on hir in a besy wyse, And she was war that he byheld hir so, And seyde: Lord1 so faste ye me avysel Sey yeme never er now? wbat sey ye,no? Yes, yes, quod he, and bet wole er I go; But, by my trouthe, I thoughte now if ye Be fortunat, for now men shal it see. H1 God forbedel quod she, be ye mad? Is that a widewes lyf, so God you save? By God, ye maken me right sore adrad, Ye ben so wilde, it semeth as ye ravel It sete me wel bet ay in a cave To bidde, and rede on holy seyntes lyves: Lat maydens gon to daunce, and yonge wyves. Hs ever thryve I, quod thís Pandarus, Yet coude I telle a thing to doon you pleye. Now uncle dere, quod she, telit us In good feith, that is sooth, quod Pandarus; But, by my trouthe, the king hath sones tweye, That is to mene, Ector and Troilus, That certainly, though that I sholde deye, Chey been as voyde of vyces, dar I seye, Hs any men that liveth under the sonne, Hir might is wyde yknowe, and what they conne. for to every wight som goodly aventure Som tyme is shape, if he it can receyven; And if that he wol take of it no cure Whan that it cometh, but wilfully it weyven, Lo, neither cas nor fortune him deceyven, But right his verray slouthe and wrecchednesse; And swich a wight is for to blame, I gesse. Of Ector nedeth it nought for to telle; In al this world ther nis a bettre knight Than he, that is of worthinesse welle; Ai wel bithought1 for love of God, quod she, Shal I not witen what ye mene of this? No, this thing axeth layser, tho quod he, And eek me wolde muche greve, ywis, If Iit tolde, and ye it toke amis Yet were it bet my tonge for to stille Than seye a sooth that were ayeins your wille. for,nece, by the goddesse Minerve, And Juppiter, that maketh the thonder ringe, And by the blisful Venus that I serve, Good aventure, O bele nece, have ye ful lightly founden, and ye conne it take; And, for the love of God, and eek of me, 485 Troilus and Caccheit anoon, lest aventure slake. Críseyde. Wbat sholde I lenger proces ofit make? LiberII. Yif me your hond, for in this world is noon, If that you list, a wight so wel begoon. And ye, that been of beautee crop and ro Than is it harm ye liven, by my And sith I speke of good entencioun, Hs I to yow have told wel herebiforn, And love as wel your honour and renoun Hs creature in al this world yborn By alle the othes that I have yow sworn, And ye be wrooth therfore, or wene I lye, Ne shal I never seen yow eft with yë. So longe mote ye live, and alle proude, In whiche ye may see your face amorwel Nece, I bidde wisshe yow no more sorwe. And also thenk wel, that this is no gaude; for me were lever, thou and I and he were banged, than I sbolde been bis baude, As heyghe, as men mighte on us alle ysee: I am thyn eem, the shame were to me As wel as thee, if that I sholde assente, Thorugh myn abet, that be thyn honour sher Hs men ben cruel in bemself and wikke; And if this man slee here bimself, allas In my presence, it wol be no solas Wbat men wolde of hit deme I can nat seye; It nedeth me ful sleyly for to pleye. With this he stente, and caste adoun the beed, And she bigan to breste awepe anoon. Allas, for wol why nere I deed? this world the feith is al agoon1 Allas what sholden straunge to me doon, When be, that for my beste freend I wende, Ret me to love, and sholde it me defende? Hnd with a sorwful syk she seyde thrye: H1 Lord1 what me is tid a sory chauncel for myn estat now lyth in jupartye, And eek myn emes lyf lyth in balaunce; But nathelees, with Goddes governaunce, I shal so doon, myn honour shal I kepe, And eek his lyf and stinte for to wepe. Beth nought agast, ne quaketh nat; wherto? Ne chaungeth nat for fere so your bewe; for hardely, the werste of this is do; And though my tale as now be to yow newe, Yet trist alwey, ye shal me finde trewe; And were it thing that me thoughte unsittinge, To yow nolde I no swiche tales bringe. Now understond, for I yow nought requere To binde yow to him thorugh no bebe But only that ye make him bett Than ye han doon er this, and So that his lyf be saved, at the leste: This al and som, and playnly our entente; God helpe me so, I never other Allas1 I wolde han trusted, doutelees, Chatif that I, thurgh my disaventure, had loved other bim or Achilles, rany mannes creature, yenolde han bad no mercy ne mesure On me, but alwey had me in repreve; This false world, allas1 who may it leve? Of harmes two, the lesseis for to chese; Yet have I lever maken bim good chere In honour, than myn emes lyf to lese; Ye seyn, ye nothing elles me requere? No, wis, quod be, myn owene nece dere. Now wel, quod she, and I wol doon my peyne; I shal myn berte ayeins my lust constreyne, Now, my good eem, for Goddes love, I preye, Quod she, com of, and tel me wbat it is; for bothe Iam agast what ye wol seye, And eek me longeth it to wite, ywis. for whether it be wel or be amis, Sey on, lat me not in this fere dwelle: So wolI doon,now herkneth,I shal telle: Lo, this request is not but skile, ywis, Ne doute of reson, pardee, is ther noon I sette the worste that ye dredden this, Men wolden wondren seen bim come or goon And ther ayeins answere I thus anoon That every wight, but he be fool of kinde Wol deme it love of freendship in bis minde. What? is this al the joye and al the feste? Is this your reed, is this my blisful cas? the verray mede of your beheste? this peynted proces seyd,allas Right for this fyn? O lady myn, Pallas1 Thou in this dredful cas for me purveye; for so astonied am I that I deyel But that I nil not holden bim in honde, Ne love a man, ne can I not, ne may Aveins my wil; but elles wol I fonde, Myn bonour sauf, plese bím fro day to day; Therto nolde Inought ones have seyd nay, But that I dredde, as in my fantasye; But cesse cause, ay cesseth maladye. Now, nece myn, the kinges dere sone, The goode, wyse, worthy, fresshe, and free, Which alwey for to do wel is his wone, The noble Troilus, so loveth thee, That, bot ye helpe, it wol his bane be. Lo, hereis al, what sholde I more seye? Doth what yow list, to make him líve or deye. What? who wol deme, though be see a man To temple go, that he the images eteth Thenk eek how wel and wysly that he can Governe bimself, that be nothing foryeteth, That, wher he cometh, he prys and thank him geteth; And eek therto, he shal come bere so selde, What fors were it though al the toun be ebelde? With that she gan ful sorwfully to syke; Almay it be no bet? quod Pandarus; l no more com here this wyke, od toforn, that am mistrusted thus; see ful wel that ye sette lyte of us, Orofour deeth1 Allas1 I woful wrecche1 Mighte he yet live, of me is nought to recche. And bere I make a protestacioun, That in this proces if ye depper go, That certaynly, for no savacioun Of yow, though that ye sterve bothe two, Though al the world on o day be my fo, Ne shal I never on bim ban other routhe. I graunte wel, quod Pandare, by my trouthe. But if ye lete him deye, I wol sterve; Have her my trouthe, nece, I nil not lyen; Al sholde I with this knyf my throte kerve: Wlith that the teres braste out of his yën, And seyde: If that ye doon us bothe dyen, Thus giltelees, than have ye fisshed faire; What mende ye, though that we bothe apeyre? Swich love of freendes regneth al this toun; And wrye yow in that mantel evermo; And, God so wis be my sa Hs I have seyd, your beste is to do so. But alwey, goode nece, to stinte bis wo, So lat your daunger sucred ben a lyte That of his deeth ye be nought for to wyte. O cruel God, O díspitouse Marte, O furies three of helle, on yow I crye1 me never out of this hous departe, that I mente barm or vilanye But sith I see my lord mot nedes dye, And I with him, here I me shryve, and seye That wikkedly ye doon us bothe deye. But may I truste wel therto, quod he, That, of this thing that ye han hight me here, Ye wolit holden trewly unto me? ye, doutelees, quod she,myn uncle dere. Ne that I shal han cause in this matere, Quod he, to pleyne, or after yow to preche? by,no, pardee; what nedeth more speche? Allas1 he whích that is my lord so dere, Chat trewe man, that noble gentil knight, That nought desireth but your freendly chere, I see him deye, ther he goth upright, And hasteth him, with al his fulle might, for to be slayn, if fortune wol assente; Allas1 that God yow swich a beautee sentel Criseyde, which that herde him in this wyse, Thoughte: I shal fele wbat he meneth,ywis. Now, eem, quod she, what wolde ye devyse What is your reed I sholde doon of this? Thatis wel seyd, quod he, certayn, bestis That ye him love ayein for his lovinge, As love for love is skilful guerdoninge. But sith it lyketh yow that I be deed, By Neptunus, that god is of the see, fro this forth shal I never eten breed CilImyn owene herte blood may see; for certayn, I wole deye as sone as be. And up be sterte, and on bis wey he raughte, Tilshe agayn him by the lappe caughte. Tho fillen they in othere tales glade, Til at the laste: O good eem, quod she tho, for love of God, which that us bothe made, Tel me how first ye wisten of his wo Wot noon of hit but ye?He seyde,No. Can he wel speke of love? quod she, I preye, Tel me, for I the bet me shal purveye. If it be so that ye so cruel be, That of his deeth yow liste nought to recche, That is so trewe and worthy, as ye see, No more than of a japere or a wrecche, If ye be swich, your beautee may not strecche To make amendes of so cruel a dede; Avysement is good bifore the nede. Wo worth the faire gemme vertulees1 Wo worth that herbealso that dooth no botel Wo worth that beautee that is routhelees1 486 Thenk eek, how elde wasteth every houre In eche of yow a party of beautee; And therfore, er that age thee devoure, Go love, for, olde, ther wol no wight of thee. Lat thís proverbe a lore unto yow be; To late ywar, quod Beautee, whan it paste; And elde daunteth daunger at the laste. Criseyde, which that wel neigh starf for fere, Soas she was the ferfulleste wight That mighte be, and bherde eek with hir ere, And saw the sorwful ernest of the knight, Andin bis preyere eek saw noon unright, And for the harm that mighte eek fallen more, She gan to rewe, and dradde hir wonder sore; Tho Pandarus a litel gan to smyle, Hnd seyde: By my trouthe, I shal yow telle. This other day, nought gon ful longe whyle, Inwith the paleyssgardyn, by a welle, Gan be and I wel half a day to dwelle, Right for to speken of an ordenaunce, How we the Grekes mighte disavaunce. The kinges fool is woned to cryen loude, Whan that him thinketh a womman bereth hirhyë, And thoughte thus: Anbappes fallen thikke Alday for love, and in swich maner cas, Sone after that bigonne we to lepe, And casten with our dartes to and fro, Troílus and Til at the laste he seyde, he wolde slepe, Criseyde. And on the gres adoun he leyde bim tho; LiberII. And I after gan rome to and fro Til that I herde, as that I welk allone, How he bigan ful wofully to grone. Ne bid me not, but ye wol se Tho gan I stalke him softely bibinde, And sikerly, the sothe for to seyne, As I can clepe ayein now to my minde, Right thus to Love he gan bim for to pleyne; He seyde: Lord1 have routhe upon my peyne, Al have I been rebel in myn entente; Now, mea culpa, lord1 I me repente. And wounded was bis hors, and gan to blede, m, was nought, withouten faile, ars, that god is of batayle. Colokeon But for to save his lyf, and elles nought, And to non harm of yow, thus am I driven, Now have I plat to yow myn berte schriven; And sin ye woot that myn entente is clene, Tak bede therof, for I non Disposed wel, and with aspectes payed, To helpen sely Troilus of his wo. And, sooth to seyn, she nas nat al a fo Troilus in his nativitee; God woot that wel the soner spedde he. So lyk a man of armes and a knight he was to seen, fulfild of beigh prowesse; Co doon that thing, as wel as hardinesse; And eek to seen bim in his gere him dresse, So fresh, so yong, so weldy semed he, It was an heven upon him for to see. Now lat us stinte of Troilus a throwe, That rydeth forth, and lat us tourne faste Unto Criseyde, that heng bir heed ful lowe, Theras she sat allone, and gan to caste Wheron she wolde apoynte hir at the laste, If it so were hir eem ne wolde cesse, for Troilus, upon hir for to presse. O god, that at thy disposicioun Ledest the fyn, by juste purveyaunce, Of every wight, my lowe confessioun Accepte in gree, and send me swich penaunce As lyketh thee, but from desesperaunce, That may my goost departe awey fro thee, Thou be my sheld, for thy benignitee. And right good thrift,I pray to God,haveye, That han swich oon ycaught withoutenet; And be ye wys, as ye ben fair to see Wel in the ring than is the ruby set Ther were never two so wel ymet Whan ye ben bis al hool, as he is youre: Ther mighty God yet graunte us see that houret his helm tobewen was in twenty places, That by a tissew beng, bis bak bibinde, his sheld todasshed was with swerdes & maces, which men mighte many an arwe finde That thirled hadde born and nerf and rinde; Anday the peple cryde: Here cometh our joye, And,next his brother, boldere up of Troyet And, Lord1 so she gan in bir thought argue In this matere of which I have yow told And what to doon best were, and what eschue, That plyted she ful ofte in many fold. Now was hir herte warm, now was it cold, And what she thoughte somwhat shal I wryte, As to myn auctor listeth for to endyte. for certes, lord, so sore hath she me wounded That stod in blak, with loking of hir yën, Chat to myn bertes botme it is ysounded, Thorugh wbich I woot that I mot nedes dyen; This is the worste, I dar me not biwryen; And wel the hotter been the gledes rede, That men hem wryen with asshen pale & dede Nay, therof spak I not, a, bal quod she, Hs helpe me God, ye shenden every O mercy, dere nece, anoon qu Whatso I spak, I mentenought buty Now beth nought wrooth,my blood,myneer. Now wel, quod she, foryeven b for which he wex a litel reed for shame, ban the peple upon him herde cryen, was a noble game breliche he caste doun his yën. Cryseyda gan al bis chere aspyen, And leet so softe it in bir berte sinke, That to hirself she seyde: Wbo yaf me drinke? With that he smoot his heed adoun anoon, And gan to motre, Inoot what, trewely. And I with that gan stille awey to goon, And leet therof as nothing wist hadde I, And come ayein anoon and stood him by, And seyde: Hwake, ye slepen al to longe; It semeth nat that love dooth yow longe, With this he took bis leve, and hoom e wente And Lord, how he was glad and wel big Criseyde aroos, no lenger shene ste But straught into hir closet wentea And sette here doun as stille as any stoor And every word gan up and doun to winde, That he hadde seyd, as it com She thoughte wel, that Troilus persone She knew by sighte and eek his gentillesse, And thus she seyde: Al were it nought to done, To graunte him love, yet, for his worthinesse, It were honour, with pley and with gladnesse, In honestee, with swich a lord to dele, for myn estat, and also for his hele. forof hir owene thought she wex al reed, Remembringe hir right thus: Lo, this is he myn uncle swereth he moot be deed, im have mercy and pitee; And with that thought, for pure ashamed, she Gan in hir beed to pulle, and that as faste, Ahyl he and al the peple forby paste, That slepen so that no man may yow wake. Who sey ever or this so dul a man? Ye, freend, quod he, do ye your hedes ake for love, and lat me liven as I can But though that he for wo was pale and wan, Yet made he tho as fresh a contenaunce, Hs though he shulde have led the newe daunce. And wex somdel astonied in bir thought, Right for the newe cas; but whan that she Was ful avysed, tho fond she right nought Of peril, why she oughte afered be. for man may love, of possibilitee, A womman so, his herte may tobreste, And she nought love ayein, but if hir leste. Eek, wel wot I my kinges sone is he; And sith he hath to see me swich delyt, If I wolde utterly his sighte flee, Paraunter he mighte have me in dispyt, Thurgh which I mighte stonde in worse plyt; Now were I wys, me hate to purchace, Withouten nede, ther I may stonde in grace? And gan to caste and rollen up and doun Withinne hir thought his excellent prowesse, And hís estat, and also bis renoun, his wit, his shap, and eek hís gentillesse; Butmost hir favour was, for his distresse Was al for hir, and thoughte it was a routhe To sleen swich oon, if that he mente trouthe. In every thing, I woot, ther lyth mesure. for though a man forbede dronkenesse, He nought forbet that every creature Be drinkelees for alwey, as I gesse; Eek sith I woot for me is his distresse, I ne oughte not for that thing him despyse, Sith it is so, he meneth in good wyse. Chis passed forth, til now, this other day, It fel that I com roming al allone Into his chaumbre, and fond how that he lay Apon his bed; but man so sore grone Ne herde I never, and what that was his mone, Ne wiste I nought; for, as I was cominge, Al sodeynly he lefte his compleyninge. But as she sat allone and thoughte thus, Chascry aroos at skarmish al withoute, And men cryde in the strete: See,Troilus Hath right now put to flight the Grekes route With that gan al hir meynee for to shoute: Hl go we see, caste up the latis wyde; for thurgh this strete he moot to palays ryde; Now mighte som envyous jangle thus: This was a sodeyn love, how mighte it be That she so lightly lovede Troilus Right for the frste sighte; ye, pardee? Now whoso seyth so,mote he never theel forevery thing, a ginning hath it nede Eralbe wrought, withouten any drede. And eek I knowe, of longe tyme agoon, His thewes goode, and that he is not nyce. Ne avauntour, seyth men, certein, is be noon; To wys is he to do so gret a vyce; Ne als I nel him never so cheryce, That he may make avaunt, by juste cause; He shal me never binde in swiche a clause. Of which I took somwhat suspecioun, And neer I com, and fond he wepte sore; And God so wís be my savacioun As never of thing hadde I no routhe more. for neither with engyn, ne with no lore, Anethes mighte I fro the deeth him kepe; That yet fele I myn herte for him wepe. for other wey is fro the yate noon Of Dardanus, ther open is the cheyne. With that com he and al his folk anoon An esy pas rydinge, in routes tweyne, Right as hís happy day was, sooth to seyne, for which, men say, may nought disturbed be That shal bityden of necessitee. forI sey nought that she so sodeynly Yaf him hir love, but that she gan enclyne Co lyke him first, and I have told yow why And after that, hís manhod and hís pyne Made love withinne hir herte for to myne, for which, by proces and by good servyse, He gat hir love, and in no sodeyn wyse. And God wot, never, sith that I was born, Was I so bísy no man for to preche, Now set a cas, the hardest ís, ywis, Men mighten deme that he loveth me: What dishonour were it unto me, this? May I him lette of that? why nay, pardee1 I knowe also, and alday here and see, Men loven wommen al this toun aboute; Be they the wers? why, nay, withouten doute. Thís Troilus sat on hís baye stede, Al armed, save his heed, ful richely, And also blísful Venus, wel arayed, Sat in hir seventhe house of hevene tho, I thenk eek how he able is for to have Of al this noble toun the thriftieste, To been bis love, so she hir honour save; for out and out he is the worthieste, Save only Ector, which that is the beste. And yet hís lyf al lyth now in my cure But swich is love, and eek myn aventure. To what fyn is swich love, I can nat see Or wher bicomth it, whan it is And thanked be ye, lord, for that I lovel Chat day by day I in my wil amende. Ne me to love, a wonder is it nought; for wel wot I myself, so God me spede, Al wolde I that noon wistë of this thought, I am oon the fayreste, out of drede, And goodlieste, whoso taketh hede; And so men seyn in al the toun of Troye. What wonder is it though he of me have joye? How bisy, if I love, eek moste I be And coye hem, that they sey non harm of me for though ther be no cause, yet hem semen And who may stoppen every wikked tonge, Or soun of belles whyl that they ber And whoso seyth that for to love is vyce, Or thraldom, though be fele in it distresse, ous, orrightnyce Co loven; for swich maner folk, I gesse, Defamen love, as nothing of him knowe; They speken, but they bente never his bowe. I am myn owene woman, wel at ese, I thank it God, as after myn estat; Right yong, and stonde unteyd in lusty lese, Withouten jalousye or swich debat; Shal noon housbonde seyn to me, Chekmat1 for either they ben ful of jalousye, Or maisterful, or loven novelrye. And after that, bir thought bigan toclere, Nothing ne acheveth, be bim looth or dere. Than slepeth bope, and after dreed awaketh; She rist hir up, and went hir for to pley What is the sonne wers, of kinde righte, Chough that a man, for feblesse of his yën, May nought endure on it to see for brighte? Or love the wers, though wrecches on it cryeni No wele is worth, that may no sorwe dryen. And forthy, who that hath an beed of verre, fro cast of stones war him in the werre1 Adoun the steyre anoonsright tho she wente Into the gardin, with bir neces three, And up and doun ther made many a wente, flexippe, she, Tharbe, and Antig To pleyen, that it joye was to And othere of bir wommen,a gret route, Hir folwede in the gardin alaboute A nightíngale, upon a cedre grene, Under the chambrerwal ther as she lay, ful loude sang ayein the mone shene, Paraunter, in bís briddes wyse, a lay Of love, that made hir herte fresh and gay. That herkned she so longe in good entente, Til at the laste the dede sleep hir hente. What shal I doon? to what fyn live I thus? Shal I nat loven, in cas if that me leste? What, par dieux1 I am nought religious And though that I myn herte sette at reste Upon this knight, that is the worthieste, And kepe alwey myn honour and my name, By alle right, it may do me no shame. But I with al myn herte and al my might, AsI have seyd, wol love, unto my laste, My dere herte,and al myn owene knight, In which myn herte growen is so faste, And his in me, that it shal ever laste. Aldredde I first to love him to biginne, Now woot I wel, ther is no peril inne. But right as whan the sonne shyneth brighte, In March, that chaungeth ofte tyme his face, And that a cloud is put with wind to flighte Which oversprat the sonne as for a space, A cloudy thought gan thorugh bir soule pace, That overspradde hir brighte thoughtes alle, So that for fere almost she gan to falle. Chís yerd was large,and rayled allethealeyes And shadwed wel with blosmy bowes gre And benched newe, and sonded alle th In which she walketh arm in ar Til at the laste Antigone the s Gan on a Troían song to singe clere, That it an heven was hir voys to here. And, as she sleep, anoonsright tho hir mette, How that an egle, fethered whyt as boon, Under bir brest bis longe clawes sette, And out hir herte he rente, and that anoon, And dide his berte into hir brest to goon, Of which she nought agroos ne nothing smerte, And forth he fleigh, with herte left for berte. And of hir song right with that word she therwithal: Now, nece, quod Criseyde, Whomade this song with so good entente? Antigoneanswerde anoon,and seyde: dame, ywis, the goodlieste mayde Of greet estat in al the toun of Troye; And let hir lyf in most honour and joye. That thought was thís: Allas1 sin I am free, Sholde I now love, and putte in jupartye My sikernesse,and tbrallen libertee? Allas 1 how dorste I thenken that folye? May Inought wel in other folk aspye Hir dredful joye, hir constreynt, and hir peyne? Ther loveth noon, that she nath why to pleyne. She seyde: O love, to whom I have and shal Ben humble subgit, trewe in myn entente Hs I best can, to yow, lord, yeve ich a for evermore, myn hertes lust to rente. for never yet thy grace no wight sente So blisful cause as me, my lyf to lede In alle joye and seurtee, out of drede. Now lat hir slepe, and we our tales holde Of Troilus, that is to paleys riden, fro the scarmuch, of the wbiche I tolde, And in bis chambre sit, and hath abiden Til two or three of his messages yeden for Pandarus, and soughten bim ful faste, Til they him founde, & brougbte him at the laste. forsothe, so it semeth by hir song, Quod tho Criseyde, and gan therwith to syke, And seyde: Lord, is there swich blisse among se lovers, as they conne faire endyte? Ve, wis, quod fresh Antigone the whyte, foralle the folk that han or been on lyve Neconne wel the blisse of love discryve. for love is yet the moste stormy lyf, Right of himself, that ever was bigonne; for ever som mistrust, or nyce stryf, Ther is in love, som cloud is over the sonne: Therto we wrecched wommen nothing conne, Whan us is wo, but wepe and sitte and thinke; Our wreche is this, our owene wo to drinke. Ye, blisful god, han me so wel beset In love, ywis, that al that bereth lyf Imaginen ne cowde how to ben bet; for, lord, withouten jalousye or stryf, I love oon which that is most ententyf To serven wel, unwery or unfeyned, That ever was, and leest with harm distreyned. Thís Pandarus com leping in at ones Hnd seide tbus: Wbo bath ben wel ybete Today with swerdes,and with slingesstones, But Troilus, that bath caught him an bete? Hnd gan to jape, and seyde: Lord, so ye swete But rys, and lat us soupe and go to reste; Hnd he answerde him: Do we as thee leste. But wene ye that every wrecche woot The parfit blisse of love? why, nay, ywis; Chey wenen al be love, if oon be hoot; Dowey, do wey, they woot nothing of this1 Men mosten axe at seyntes if it is Aught fair in hevene; wby? for they conne telle; And axen fendes, is it foul in belle. The nightes fo, al this clepe I the sonne, Gan westren faste, and dounward for to wrye, Hs he that hadde hís dayes cours yronne; And wbyte thinges wexen dimme and donne for lak of light, and sterres for to appere, Chat she and al hir folk in wente yfere. Also these wikked tonges been so prest To speke us harm, eek men be so untrewe, That, right anoon as cessed is hir lest, So cesseth love, and forth to love a newe: But harm ydoon, is doon, whoso it rewe. for though these men for love hem first torende, ful sharp biginning breketh ofte at ende. So whan it lyked hir to goon to reste, And voyded weren they that voyden oughte, She seyde, that to slepe wel hir leste. Hir wommen sone til hir bed bir broughte. han al was hust, than lay she stille, & thoughte Of al this thing the manere and the wyse. Reherce it nedeth nought, for ye ben wyse. With al the haste goodly that they mighte, They spedde hem fro the souper unto bedde And every wight out at the dore bim dighte, And wher him list upon his wey he spedde; But Troilus, that thoughte bis berte bledde for wo, til that he herde som tydinge, He seyde: freend, shal Inow wepe or singe? As he that is the welle of worthinesse, Of trouthe ground, mirour of goodlibeed Of wit Hppollo, stoon of sikernesse, Of vertu rote, of lust findere and beed, Thurgh which is alle sorwe fro me deed, Ywis, I love him best, so doth he me; Now good thrift have he, wherso that he bel How ofte tyme hath it yknowen be, The treson, that to womman bath be do? Criseyde unto that purpos nought answerde, But seyde: Ywis, it wol be night as faste. But every word which that she of hir herde, She gan to prenten in bir herte faste; And ay gan love bir lasse for to agaste Chan it dide erst, and sinken in bir herte, That she wex somwhat able to converte. Whom sholde I thanke but yow, god of love, Of al this blisse, in which to bathe I ginne? The dayes honour, and the hevenes yë, Quod Pandarus: Ly stille, and lat me slepe, And don thyn hood, thy nedes spedde be; And chese,if thou wolt singe or daunce or lepe; At shorte wordes, thow shalt trowe me. Sire, my nece wol do wel by thee, And love thee best, by God and by my trouthe, But lak of pursuit make it in thy slouthe. for thus ferforth I have thy work bigonne, 491 Troilus and fro day to day, tíl thís day, by the morwe, Críseyde. Hir love of freendship have I to thee wonne, LiberII. And also hath she leyd hir feyth to borwe. Hlgate a footis hameled of thy sorwe. Wbat sholde I lenger sermon ofit bolde? Hs ye han berd bifore, al be him tolde.
