And upon me make thy contenaunce; But, by thy lyf, be war and faste eschu And we shal speke of thee somwhat,I tr Whan thou art goon, to do th But right as floures, thorugh the colde of night Yclosed, stoupen on hir stalkes lowe, Redressen hem ayein the sonne bright, And spreden on hir kinde cours by rowe; Right so gan tho his eyen up to throwe This Troílus,and seyde: O Venus dere, Thy might, thy grace, yheried be it here1 Couching thy lettre, thou art wys ynough I woot thow nilt it digneliche endyte As make it with thise argumentes tough; Ne scrivenish or craftily thou it wryte Beblotte it with thy teres eek a lyte And if thou wryte a goodly word al softe Though it be good, reberce it not to of Andafter this, fullowly be bir prayde Cobenought wrooth, though be, of his folye, And pitously gan mercy for to crye; And to Pandare he held up bothe his hondes, Hnd seyde: Lord, al thyn be that I have; for Iam hool, al brosten been my bondes; A thousand Troíans who so that me yave, Eche after other, God so wis me save, Ne mighte me so gladen; lo, myn herte, It spredeth so forjoye, it wol tosterte1 acused for though the beste harpour upon lyve Wolde on the beste souned joly har Touche ay o streng, or ay o werbul ba Were his nayles poynted never so sha It shulde maken every wight to d To here his glee, and of his stro But Lord, how shal I doon, how shal I liven? Wban shal I next my dere herte see? How shal this longe tyme awey be dríven, Til that thou be ayein at hir fro me? Chou mayst answere,Hbyd,abyd, but he That hangeth by the nekke, sooth to seyne, In grete disese abydeth for the peyne. And with bis salte teres gan he bathe Theruby in bis signet, and it sette Apon the wex deliverliche and rathe; Therwith a thousand tymes, er he lette, hekiste tho the lettre that he shette, Andseyde: Lettre,a blisful destenee Thee shapen is, my lady shal thee see. Ne jompre eek no discordaunt thing yfere, Hs thus, to usen termes of phisy In loves termes, hold of th The forme alwey, and do that it b for if a peyntour wolde peynte a pyl With asses feet, and hede it as ana It cordeth nought; so nere it but Hl esily, now, for the love of Marte, Quod Pandarus, for every thing hath tyme; So longe abyd til that the night departe; for al so siker as thow lyst here by me, And God toforn, I wol be there at pryme, And for thy werk somwbat as I shal seye, Or on som other wight this charge leye. This counseyl lyked wel to Troilus; But, as a dreedful lover, he seyde this: Allas, my dere brother Pandarus, I am ashamed for to wryte, ywis, Lest of myn innocence I seyde amis, Or that she nolde it for despyt receyve; Thanne were I deed, ther mighte it nothing weyve. This Pandare took the lettre, and that by tyme ís neces paleys sterte, , that it was passed pryme, and seyde: Ywis,myn herte, So fresh it is, a although it sore smerte, Imay not slepe never a Mayes morwe; Ihaveajoly wo, a lusty sorwe. Criseyde, whan that she hir uncle herde, With dreedful herte, and desirous to here Thecause his cominge, thus answerde: Now by your feyth, myn uncle, quod she, dere, What maner windes gydeth yow now here? Celus your joly wo and your penaunce, how ferforth be ye put in loves daunce. for pardee, God wot, I have ever yit Ben redy thee to serve, and to this night Have I nought fayned, but emforth my wit Don al thy lust, and shal with al my might. Do now as I shal seye, and fare aright; And if thou nilt, wyte al thyself thy care, On me is nought along thyn yvel fare. To that Pandare answerde: If thee lest, Do that I seye, and lat me therwith goon for by that Lord that formed est and west I hope of it to bringe answere ano Right of hir hond, and if that thou nilt noon, Lat be; and sory mote he been his lyve, Ayeins thy lust that helpeth thee to thryve. I woot wel that thow wyser art than I H thousand fold, but if I were as thou, God helpe me so, as I wolde outrely Right of myn owene hond, wryte hir right now A lettre, in which I wolde hir tellen how I ferde amis, and hir beseche of routhe; Now help thyself, and leve it not for slouthe. But thus ye faren, wel neigh alle and some, That he that most desireth yow to serve, Of him ye recche leest wber he bicome, And wbether that he live or elles sterve. But for al that that ever I may deserve, Refuse it nought, quod he, and bente hir faste, And in hir bosom the lettre doun he thraste, And I myself shal therwith to hir goon; And whan thou wost that I am with hir there, Worth thou upon a courser right anoon, Ye, hardily, right in thy beste gere, And ryd forth by the place, as nought ne were, And thou shalt finde us, if I may, sittinge At som windowe, into the strete lokinge. By God, quod he, I hoppe alwey bibinde1 And she to laughe,it thoughte hir berte breste. Quod Pandarus: Loke alwey that ye finde Gamein myn hood, but herkneth, if yow leste; Theris right now come into toune a geste, AGreek espye, and telleth newe thinges, for which come I to telle yow tydinges. And seyde hir: Now cast it away anoon, That folk may seen and gauren on us tweye Quod she: I can abyde til they be goon. And gan to smyle, and seyde hím: Eem, I preye, Swich answere as yow list yourself purveye, for trewely I nil no lettre wryte. No? than wol I, quod he, so ye endyte. Quod Troilus: Depardieux, I assente; Sin that thee list, I will aryse and wryte; And blisful God preye ich, with good entente The vyage, and the lettre I shal endyte, So spede it; and thou, Minerva, the whyte, Yif thou me wit my lettre to devyse: And sette him doun, and wroot rightin this wyse. And if thee líst, than maystow us saluwe, 492 fírst he gan hir his righte lady calle, His hertes lyf, hís lust, hís sorwes leche, His blisse, and eek this othere termes alle, That in swich cas these loveres alle seche; And in ful bumble wyse, as in his speche, He gan bím recomaunde unto hir grace; To telle al how, it axeth muchel space. This Pandarus gan on hir for to stare, And seyde: Now is this the grettest wonder That ever I sey1 lat be this nyce fare1 To deethe mote I smiten be with thonder If, for the citee which that stondeth yonder, Wolde I a lettre unto yow bringe or take To harm of yow; wbat list yow thus it make? And loketh now if this be resonable, And letteth nought, for favour ne for slouthe, To seyn a sooth; now were it covenable Co myn estat, by God, and by your trouthe, To taken it, or to han of him routhe, In harming of myself orin repreve? Ber it ayein, for him that ye on leve1 ful dredfully tho gan she stonde stille, And took it nougbt, but al hir bumble chere Gan for to chaunge, and seyde: Scrit ne bille, for love of God, that toucheth swich matere, Ne bríng me noon; and also, uncle dere, To myn estat have more reward, I preye, Chan to his lust; what sholde I more seye? Into the gardin go we, and we shal here, Alprevely, of this a long sermoun. With that they wenten arm in arm yfere Into the gardin from the chaumbre doun. And whan that he so fer was that the soun Of that he speke, no man here mighte, heseyde hir thus, and out the lettre plighte: Lo,he that is al hoolly youres free Him recomaundeth lowly to your grace, And sent to you this lettre here by me; Avyseth you on it, whan ye han space, And of som goodly answere yow purchace; Or, helpe me God, so pleynly for to seyne, Hemay not longe liven for his peyne. Therwith she lough, and seyde: Go we dyne. Hnd he gan at himself to jape faste, Hnd seyde: Nece, I have so greet a pyne for love, that every other day I faste Hnd gan his beste japes forth to caste; And made hir so to laughe at his folye, That she for laughter wende for to dye. And whan that she was comen into halle: Now, eem, quod she, we wol go dyne anoon; And gan some of hir women to hir calle, And streyght into hir chaumbre gan she goon; But of hir besinesses, this was oon Amonges othere thinges, out of drede, ful prively this lettre for to rede; Avysed word by word in every lyne, And fond no lak, she thoughte he coude good; And up it putte, and went hir in to dyne. And Pandarus, that in a study stood, Er he was war, she took hím by the hood, And seyde: Ye were caught er that ye wiste; I vouche sauf, quod he, do what yow liste. 493 Tho wesshen they, and sette hem doun & ete; And after noon ful sleyly Pandarus Gan drawe him to the window next the strete, Hnd seyde: Nece, who bath arayed thus The yonder hous, that stant aforyeyn us? hich hous? quod sbe, and gan for to bibolde, And knew it wel, and whos it was him tolde, And fillen forth in speche of thinges smale, And seten in the window bothe tweye. Whan Pandarus saw tyme unto hís tale, And saw wel that hir folk were alle aweye: Now, nece myn, tel on, quod he, I seye, How lyketh yow the lettre that ye woot? Can be theron? for, by my trouthe, I noot. But ye ban pleyed tyrauntneigh to longe, And bard was it your berte for to grave; Al wolde ye the forme of daunger save. But hasteth yow to doon bim joye have; Causeth despyt ful often,for distress And right as they declamed this matere Lo, Troilus, right at the stretes ende, Com ryding with bis tenthe some yfere, Al softely, and thiderward gan bende Theras they sete, as was his wey to wende To paleys ward; and Pandare him aspyde, And seyde: Nece, ysee who cometh bere rydel But Pandarus thoughte: It shal not be so, I may; this nyce opinioun If that hemoste assente on that conclusioun And al was wel, he roos and took his leve. Therwith al rosy hewed tho wex she, And gan to humme, and seyde: So I trowe Hquyte him wel, for Goddes love, quod be; Myself to medes wol the lettre sowe, And held his hondes up, and sat on knowe, Now, goodenece, beit never so lyte, Yif me the labour, it to sowe and plyte. ye he felte his herte daunce; alone abedde ay as dooth these loveres, in a traunce, Bitwixen bope and derk desesperaunce. But Pandarus, right at bis incominge, hesong, as who seyth: Lol sumwhat I bringe. O flee not in, he seeth us, I suppose; Lest he may thinke that ye him eschuwe Nay, nay, quod sbe,and wex as reed as rose, With that he gan bir humbly to saluwe, With dreedfulcbere,andoftebis hewesmuwe And up his look debonairly be caste And bekked on Pandare, and forth be paste Ye, for I can so wryte, quod she tho; And eek Inoot wbat I sholde to him seye. Nay, nece, quod Pandare, sey not so; Yet at the leste thanketh him, I preye, Of his good wil, and doth hím not to deye. Now for the love of me, my nece dere, Refuseth not at this tyme my preyere. God woot if he sat on bis hors aright, Or goodly was beseyn, that ilke day God woot wher he was lyk a manly knig Wbat sholde I drecche, or telle of his ar Criseyde, which that alle these thinges say To telle in short, hir lyked al yfer Hís persone, hís aray, his look, bis chere, Deparsdieux, quod she, God leve al be well God helpe me so, this is the firste lettre That ever I wroot, ye, al or any del And into a closet, for to avyse hir bettre, She wente allone, and gan hir herte unfettre Out of dísdaynes príson but a lyte; And sette hir doun, and gan a lettre wryte, His goodly manere and his gentillese, So wel, that never, sith that she was bor Ne badde she swich routhe of bis distre And howso she hath hard ben herbifor To God hope I, she hath now caughta thor She shal not pulle it out this nexte wyke; God sende mo swich thornes on to pyke Of which to telle in short is myn entente Theffect, as fer as I can understonde: She thonked him of al that he wel mente Towardes hir, but holden bim in honde She nolde nought, ne make hirselven bonde In love, but as hís suster, hím to plese, She wolde fayn, to doon hís herte an ese. And seyde: Who is in his bed so sone Yburied thus?It am I, freend, quod be. Who, Troilus? nay helpe me so the mone, Quod Pandarus, thou shalt aryse and see Acharme that was sent right now to thee, The which can belen thee of thyn accesse, If thou do forthwith al thy besinesse. She shette it, and to Pandarus gan goon, There as he sat and loked into strete, And doun she sette hir by him on a stoon Of jaspre, upon a quisshin gold ybete, And seyde: Hs wisly helpe me God the grete, I never dide a thing with more peyne Than wryte this, to which ye me constreyne; And took it him: he thonked hir and seyde: God woot, of thing ful ofte looth bigonne Cometh ende good; and nece myn, Criseyde That ye to him of hard now ben ywonne Oughte he be glad, by God and yonder sonne1 forwhy men seyth: Impressiounes lighte ful lightly been ay redy to the flighte Pandare, which that stood bir faste by, felte iren hoot, and he bigan to smyte, And seyde: Nece, I pray yow hertely, Tel me that I shal axen yow a lyte. A womman, that were of his deeth to wyte, Withouten his gilt, but for hir lakked routhe, Were it wel doon?Quod she: Nay, by my trouthel Ye, through the might of Godi quod Troilus. Hnd Jandarus gan him the lettre take, ee, God hath holpen us; t, and loke on al this blake. But ofte gan the herte glade and quake Of Troilus, whyl that he gan it rede, So as the wordes yave him hope or drede. But fynally, he took al for the beste him wroot, for sumwhat he biheld .him thoughte,hemightehishertereste, vered she the wordes under sheld. Thus to the more worthy part he held, That, what for hope and Pandarus biheste, his grete wo foryede he at the leste. God helpe me so, quod he, ye sey me sooth. Ye felen wel yourself that I not lye; Lo, yond he rit 1Quod she: Ye, so be dooth Wel, quod Pandare, as I have told yow thrye, Lat be your nyce shame and your folye And spek with bim in esing of his herte; Lat nycetee not do yow bothe smerte. But theron was to heven and to done; Considered al thing, it may not be; And why, for shame; and it were eek to sone To graunten him so greet a libertee. for playnly hír entente, as seyde she, Was for to love him unwist, if she mighte, And guerdon him with nothing but with sighte. But as we may alday ourselven see, Through more wode or col, the more fyr; t so encrees of hope, of what it be, rwith ful ofte encreseth eek desyr; Or, as an ook cometh of a lítel spyr, So through this lettre, which that she him sente, Encresen gan desyr, of which he brente. Wherfore I seye alwey, that day and night This Troilus gan to desíren more Chan he dide erst, thurgh hope, & dide his might Co pressen on, as by Pandarus lore, And wryten to hir of his sorwes sore fro day to day; he leet it not refreyde, That by Pandare he wroot somwhat or seyde; And dide also hís othere observaunces That to a lovere longeth in this cas; And, after that these dees turnede on chaunces, So was he outher glad or seyde Hllas1 And held after hís gestes ay hís pas; And aftír swiche answeres as he hadde, So were his dayes sory outher gladde. But to Pandare alwey was hís recours, And pitously gan ay til hím to pleyne, And him bisoughte of rede and som socours; And Pandarus, that sey his wode peyne, Wex wel neigh deed for routhe, sooth to seyne, And bisily with al his herte caste Som of his wo to sleen, and that as faste; And seyde: Lord, and freend, and brother dere, God woot that thy disese dooth me wo. But woltow stinten al this woful chere, And, by my trouthe, orit be dayes two, But God toforn, yet shal I shape it so, That thou shalt come into a certayn place, Theras thou mayst thyself hir preye of grace. And certainly, I noot if thou it wost, But tho that been expert in love it seye, It is oon of the thinges that furthereth most, A man to have a leyser for to preye, And siker place his wo for to biwreye; for in good herte it moot som routhe impresse, To here and see the giltles in distresse. Paraunter thenkestow: though it be so That kinde wolde doon hir to biginne To han a maner routhe upon my wo, Seyth Daunger: Nay, thou shalt me never winne; So reuleth hir hir hertes goost withinne, That, though she bende, yet she stant on rote; What in effect is this unto my bote? Thenk hereayeins, whan that the sturdy ook, On which men hakketh ofte, for the nones, Receyved hath the happy falling strook, The grete sweigh doth it come al at ones, As doon these rokkes or these milnesstones. for swifter cours cometh thing that is of wighte, Whan it descendeth, than don thinges lighte. And reed that boweth doun for every blast, ful lightly, cesse wind, it wol aryse; But so nil not an ook wban it is cast; It nedeth me nought thee longe to forbyse. Men shal rejoysen of a greet empryse Acheved wel, and stant withouten doute, Al han men been the lenger theraboute. But, Troilus, yet tel me, if thee lest, A thing now which that I shal axen thee; Which is thy brother that thou lovest best As in thy verray hertes privetee? Ywis, my brother Deiphebus, quod he. Now, quod Pandare, er houres twyes twelve, He shal thee ese, unwist of it himselve. Now lat me allone, and werken as I may, Quod he; and to Deipbebus wente he tho hich hadde his lord and grete freend ben ay; Save Troilus, no man he lovede so. To telle in short, withouten wordes mo, Quod Pandarus: I pray yow that ye be freend to a cause which that toucheth me. Yis, pardee, quod Deiphebus, wel thow wost, In al that ever I may, and God tofore, nere it but for man I love most, My brother Troilus; but sey wherfore It is; for sith that day that I was bore, I nas, ne nevermo to been I thinke, Ayeins a thing that mighte thee forthinke. Pandare gan him thonke, and to hím seyde: Lo, síre, I have a lady in this toun, Chat is my nece, and called is Criseyde Which som men wolden doon oppressioun, And wrongfully have hir possessioun: Wherfor I of your lordship yow biseche To been our freend, withoute more speche. He seyde: O veray God, so have Ironnel Lo,nece myn, see yenought how I swete? Inoot whether ye the more thank me co Is now aboute eftsones for to plet And bringe on yow advocacyes newe? I? no, quod she, and chaunged alh What is be more aboute, me to drecche And doon me wrong? what shal I do,allas? Yet of bimself notbing ne wolde Irecche, Nere it for Antenor and Eneas, That been bis freendes in swich maner cas; But, for the love of God, myn uncle dere, No fors of that, lat him have al yfere Sey that thy fever is wont thee for to take The same tyme, and lasten til amorwe; And lat see now bow wel thou canst it make, for, pardee, syk is he that is in sorwe. Gonow, farewell and, Venus here to borwe, I hope, and thou this purpos holde ferme, Thy grace she shal fully ther conferme. Quod Troilus: Vwis, thou nedelees ylest me, that sykliche I me feyne1 ernest, doutelees, I sterve for the peyne. arus: Thou shalt the bettre pleyne, And hast the lasse nede to countrefete; for him men demen hoot that men seen swete. Deiphebus him answerde: O, is not this, That thow spekest of to me thus straungely Crisëyda, my freend?He seyde: Yis Than nedeth, quod Deiphebus hardely, Namore to speke, for trusteth wel, that I Wol be hir champioun with spore and yerde; I roughte nought though alle hir foos it herde. Withouten that, I have ynough for us. Nay, quod Pandare, it shal nothing beso for I bave been right now at Deiphebus, And Ector, and myne othere lordes mo, And shortly maked eche of bem bis fo; That, by my thrift, heshalit never winne for ought he can, whan that so he biginne, But tel me, thou that woost al this matere, How I might best avaylen? now lat see. Quod Pandarus: If ye, my lord so dere, Wolden as now don this honour to me, To prayen hir tomorwe, lo, that she Com unto yow hír pleyntes to devyse, Hir adversaries wolde of hit agryse. nd as they casten what was best to done, Deiphebus, of his owene curtasye, Com hir to preye, in hís propre persone, To holde him on the morwe comp At diner, which she nolde not deny But goodly gan to his preyere ob He thonked hir, and wente upon his weye Lo,holde thee at thy triste cloos, and I Shal wel el the deer unto thy bowe dryve. ne took his leve al softely, And Troilus to paleys wente blyve. So glad ne was he never in al bis lyve; And to Pandarus reed gan al assente, And to Deiphebus hous at night he wente. What nedeth yow to tellen al the chere Chat D íphebus unto his brother made, r bis syklych manere, gan him with clothes for to lade, Whan he was leyd, and how men wolde him glade? Butal for nought, he held forth ay the wyse That ye han herd Pandare er this devyse. Herde al this thing Criseyde wel ynough, And every word gan for to notifye; for which with sobre chere hir herte lough; for who is that ne wolde hir glorifye, To mowen swich a knight don live or dye? But al passe I, lest ye to longe dwelle; for for o fyn is al that ever I telle. And if I more dorste preye as now, And chargen yow to have so greet travayle, To han som of your bretheren here with yow That mighten to hir cause bet avayle, Than, woot I wel, she mighte never fayle for to be holpen, what at your instaunce, What with hir othere freendes governaunce. After compleynt, him gonnen they to preyse, Hs folk don yet, whan som wight hath bigonne To preyse a man, and up with prys him reyse H thousand fold yet byer than the sonne: Heis, he can, that fewe lordes conne Hnd Pandarus, of that they wolde afferme, He not forgat hir preysing to conferme. hanne this was doon, this Pandare upanoon To telle in short, and forth gan for t Co Troilus, as stille as an And al this thing he tolde him, word and end And how that be Deiphebus gan to blende; To bere thee wel tomorwe, The tyme com, fro díner for to ryse, And,as hem oughte, arisen everychoon, And gonne a wbile of this and that devyse. But Pandarus brak al thís speche anoon, And seyde to Deiphebus: Wole ye goon, If yourë wille be, as I yow preyde, To spekebere of the nedes of Criseyde? Compleyned eek Eleyne of his syknesse So feithfully, that pitee was to here, Hnd every wight gan waxen for accesse H leche anoon,and seyde:In this manere Men curen folk; this charme I wol yow lere. But there sat oon, al list hir nought to teche, That thoughte, best coude I yet been his leche. Spek thou thyself also to Troilus On my bibalve, and pray hím wíth us dyne. Sire,al this shal be doon, quod Pandarus; And took his leve, and never gan to fyne, But to hís neces hous, as streght as lyne, He com; and fond bir fro the mete aryse; And sette him doun, and spak right in this wyse. But certeyn is, er Troilus him leyde, Deiphebus had him prayed, over night, Cobeena freend and helping to Criseyde. God woot, that he it grauntede anonsright, Co been hir fulle freend with al his might. But swich a nede was to preye him thenne, As for to bidde a wood man for to renne. Deiphebus, which that comen was, of kinde, To al honour and bountee to consente, Hnswerde: It shal be doon; and I can finde Yet gretter help to this in myn entente. What wolt thow seyn, if I for Eleyne sente To speke of this? I trowe it be the beste; for she may leden París as hír leste. Eleyne, which that by the hond hir held, Took first the tale, and seyde: Go we blyve; And goodly on Criseyde she bibeld, And seyde:Joves lat him never thryve, That dooth yow harm, & bringe him sone of lyve And yeve me sorwe, but he shalit rewe, If that I may, and alle folk be trewe. Of Ector, which that is my lord, my brother, It nedeth nought to preye him freend to be; for I have herd him, o tyme and eek other, Speke of Criseyde swich honour, that he May seyn no bet, swich bap to him hath she It nedeth nought his helpes for to crave; He shal be swich, right as we wole him bave. Now spek, now prey, now pitously compleyne; Lat not for nyce shame, or drede, or slouthe Somtyme a man mot telle his owene peyne; Bileve it, and she shal han on thee routhe; Thou shalt be saved by thy feyth, in trouthe. But wel wot I, thou art now in a drede; And what it is, I leye, I can arede. Che morwen com, and neighen gan the tyme Of meel tyd, that the faire quene Eleyne Shoop hir to been, an houre after the pryme, With Deiphebus, to whom she nolde feyne; But as hís suster, hoomly, sooth to seyne, She com to díner in hir playn entente. But God and Pandare wiste al what this mente. Thow thinkest now: How sholde I doonalthis? for by my cheres mosten folk aspye, Chat for hir love is that I fare ami Yet hadde I lever unwist for sorwe dye Now thenk not so, for thou dost greet folye. for right now have I founden o manere Of sleighte, for to coveren al thy chere. Thow shalt gon over night, and that as blyve, Anto Deíphebus hous, as thee to pleye, Thy maladye awey the bet to dryve, forwhy thou semest syk, soth for to seye Sone after that, doun in thy bed thee leye, And sey, thow mayst no lenger up endure, And lye right there, and byde thyn aventure. Tel thou thy neces cas, quod Deiphebus To Pandarus, for thou canst best it telle. @My lordes and my ladyes, it stant thus; Wbat sholde I lenger, quod he, do yow dwelle? He rong hem out a proces lyk a belle, Apon bir fo, that highte Poliphete, So bëynous, that men mighte on it spete. Come eek Criseyde, al innocent of this, Antigone, hir sister Tarbe also; Butflee we now prolixitee best is, for love of God, and lat us faste go Right to the effect, withoute tales mo, Why al this folk assembled in this place; And lat us of hír saluinges pace. Gret honour dide hem Deiphebus, certeyn, And fedde hem wel with al that mighte lyke. But evermore: Hllas1 was his refreyn, My goode brother Troilus, the syke, Lyth yet And therwithal he gan to syke; And after that, he peyned hím to glade Hem as he mighte, and chere good he made. kk1 Answerde of this ech worse of hem than other, And Poliphete they gonnen thus to warien: Ansbonged be swich oon, were be my brother; And so he shal, for it ne may not varien. Wbat sbolde I lenger in this tale tarien? Pleynly, at ones, alle they hir highten, To been hir helpe in al that ever they mighten. Spak than Eleyne,and seyde: Pandarus, Woot ought my lord, my brother, this matere, I mene, Ector? or wootit Troilus? He seyde:Ye, but wole ye now me here? Me thinketh this, sith Troilus is bere, It were good, if that ye wolde assente, She tolde hirself him al this, er she wente. 407 <table><tr><td>oílus and for he wole have the more hir grief at herte, iseyde. By cause, lo, that shea lady is; berII. And, by your leve, I wol but right in sterte, And do yow wite, and that anoon, ywis, If that he slepe, or wole ought here of this. And in he lepte, and seyde him in his ere: God have thy soule, ybrought have I thy bere1 To smylen of this gan tho Troilus, And Pandarus, withoute rekeninge, Out wente anoon to Eleyne and Deiphebus, And seyde hem: So there be no taryinge, Ne more pres, he wol wel that ye bringe Crísëyda, my lady, that is here; And as he may enduren, he wole here. But wel ye woot, the chaumbre is but lyte,</td><td>Or elles God forbede, tho quod he, If that she youche sauf for to do so. To yow have I to speke of o matere, Tobeen avysed by yourreed the bettre: The copie of a tretis and a lettre, woot I nougbt who; but inagrisly wyse He preyede hem anoon on it avyse.</td><td>Chefolk devneat wagginge of a stree; Com of therfore,and bringeth him to bele. But now to yow, ye lovers that ben here,</td><td>Was Troilus nought in a cankedort, Troílusand That lay,and mighte whispringe of hem Criseyde. here, LiberII. And thoughte: O Lord, right now renneth my sort fully to dye, or han anoon comfort And was the firste tyme be shulde hir preye Of love; O mighty God, what shal he seye? Explicit Secundus Liber.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Now loketh ye, for I wol have no wyte, To bringe in prees that mighte doon bim barm Or him disesen, for my bettre arm, Wher it be bet she byde til eftsones; Now loketh ye, that knowen what to doon is. I sey for me, best is, as I can knowe,</td><td>And fewe folk may lightly make it warm;</td><td>Deiphebus gan this lettre to unfolde Inernest greet; so dide Eleynethe quene And rominge outward, fast it gan bibolde, Downward a steyre, into an berber grene. This ike thing they redden bem bitwene; And largely, the mountaunce of an houre, They gonne on it to reden and to poure. Now lat hem rede, and turne we anoon To Pandarus, that gan ful faste prye</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>But it were I, for I can, in a throwe, Reherce hír cas, unlyk that she can seye; And after this, she may him ones preye To ben good lord, in short, and take hir leve; This may not muchel of his ese him reve. And eek, for she is straunge, he wol forbere</td><td></td><td>That al was wel, and out he gan to goon Into the grete chambre, and that in bye, And seyde: God saveal this companyer Com, nece myn; my lady quene Eleyne Abydeth yow, and eek my lordes tweyne. Rys, take with yow your nece Antigone, Or whom yow list, or no fors, hardily;</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>His ese, which that him thar nought for yow; Eek other thing, that toucheth not to bere, He wol me telle, I woot it wel right now, That secret is, and for the tounes prow. And they, that nothing knewe of this entente, Withoute more, to Troilus in they wente. Eleyne in al hir goodly softe wyse,</td><td></td><td>The lasse prees, the bet; com fort withme, And loke that ye thonke humblely Hem alle three, and, whan ye may goodly Your tyme ysee, taketh of hem your leve, Lest we to longe his restes him bireve. Alinnocent of Pandarus entente, Quod tho Criseyde: Go we, uncle dere;</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>And seyde: Ywis,ye moste alweyes aryse Now fayre brother, beth al hool, I preyel And gan bir arm right over his sholder leye, And him with al hir wit to recomforte; As she best coude, she gan him to disporte. So after this quod she: We yow biseke, My dere brother, Deiphebus, and I,</td><td></td><td>And arm in arm inward with him she wente, Avysed wel hir wordes and hir chere; And Pandarus, in ernestful manere, Seyde: Alle folk, for Goddes love, I preye, Stinteth right here,and softely yow pleye Aviseth yow what folk ben here withinne, And in what plyt oon is, God bim amendel</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>for love of God, and so doth Pandare eke, To been good lord and freend, right bertely, Unto Criseyde, which that certeinly Receyveth wrong, as woot wel here Pandare, That can bir cas wel bet than I declare. Chis Pandarus gan newe his tunge affyle,</td><td></td><td>And inward thus ful softely biginne; Nece, I conjure and heighly yow defende, On bis half, which that sowle us alle sende, And in the vertue of corounes tweyne, Slee nought this man,that hath for yow this peynel fy on the devel1 thenk which oon he is,</td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>And al hir cas reberce, and that anoon; Whan it was seyd, sone after, in a whyle, Quod Troilus: Hs sone as I may goon, I wol right fayn with al my might ben oon, Have God my trouthe, hir cause to sustene. Good thrift have ye, quod Eleyne the quene. 498</td><td></td><td>And in what plyt he lyth; com of anoon; Thenk al swich taried tyd, but lost it nist That wol ye bothe seyn, whan ye ben oon. Secoundelich, ther yet devyneth noon Apon yow two; com of now, if ye conne; Whyl folk is blent, lo,al the tyme is wonnel</td><td>kk2</td><td>499</td></tr></table> ![](images/18e8d14269addc1934ead1cace1a748894204ae6a81896c4e4e862f7261b574b.jpg) TROILIS AND CRISEYDE LIBER CERCIAS. Incipit Prohemium Tercií Librí Yheried be thy m ![](images/127f2e0e197ff9e29f5ed4ddad60d7d5cfaebf41225f1b3093adbf99c16e7685.jpg) BLISfALLIGHT,OfADICHECHE bemes clere Adorneth al the thridde hevene faíre1 O sonnes leef, O Joves doughter dere, Plesaunce of love, O goodly debonaire, In gentil hertes ay redy to repaire1 O verray cause of hele and of gladnesse, In hevene and helle, in erthe and salte see Is felt thy might, if that I wel descerne Hs man, bríd, best, fish, herbe and grene tre Thee fele in tymes with vapour eterne. God loveth, and to love wol nought werne; And in thís world no lyves creature, Withouten love, is worth, or may endure. Ye Joves first to thilke effectes glade, Thorugh which that thinges liven alle and be Comeveden, and amorous hím made On mortal thíng, and as yow list, ay ye Yeve him in love ese or adversitee; And in a thousand formes doun hím sente for love in erthe, and whom yow liste, be hente. Ye fierse Mars apeysen of his ire, And, as yow líst, ye maken hertes digne; Algates, hem that ye wol sette afyre They dreden shame, and vices they resigne; Ye do hem corteys be, fressheand benigne, And hye or lowe, after a wight entendeth; The joyes that he hath, your might him sendeth. Ye holden regne and hous in unitee; Ýesoothfast cause of frendship been Ye knoweal thilke covered qualitee Öf thinges which that folk on wondren so, Whan they can not construe how it may jo, She loveth him, or why he loveth here; As wby this fish, and nought that, cometh to were. Ve folk a lawe han set in universe, And this knowe I by hem that loveres be, That whoso stryveth with yow hath the werse: Now, lady bright, for thy benignitee, Atreverence of hem that serven thee, Whos clerk I am, so techeth me devyse Som joye of that is felt in thy servyse. Yein my naked berte sentement Inbelde,and do me shewe of thy swetnesse. Caliope, thy vois be now present, fornow is nede; sestow not my destresse, How I mot telle anonsright the gladnesse Of Troilus, to Venus heryinge? To which gladnes, who nede hath, God him bringe1 Explicit probemium Tercii Libri. ralthis mene whyle ![](images/b06425b6f72034020f42f52d8e9c672f9b07e3f7e2a810283856d33f8a63f011.jpg) Recordinge his lessoun in thís manere: Ma fey1 thought he, thus wole I seye and thus; Chus wole I pleyne unto my lady dere; Chat word is good, and this shal be my chere; This nil I not foryeten in no wyse. God leve him werken as he gan devyse. And Lord, so that his herte gan to quappe, Heringe hir come, and shorte for to sykel And Pandarus, that ladde hír by the lappe, Com ner, and gan in at the curtin pyke, And seyde: God do bote on alle sykel See, who is here yow comen to visyte; Lo, here is she that is your deeth to wyte. Therwith it semed as he wepte almost: H ha, quod Troilus so rewfully, her me be wo, O mighty God, thou wost1 Who is al there? I see nought trewely Sire, quod Criseyde, it is Pandare and I. Troílus and Criseyde. LiberIII. Ye, swete herte? allas, I may nought ryse To knele, and do yow honour in som wyse. And dressede him upward, and she right tho Gan bothe here hondes softe upon bim leye: O, for the love of God, do ye not so To me, quod she, eyl what is this to seye? Sire, come am I to yow for causes tweye; first, yow to thonke, and of your lordshipe eke Continuance I wolde yow biseke. for Goddes love, and doth bim noughttode Now thanne thus, quod she, preye To telle me the fyn of his entent Yet wiste I never wel what that Beth glad, and draweth yow to lustinesse, shal trewely, with al my might, ornen al into swetnesse; f I be she that may yow do gladnesse, for every wo ye shalrecoverea blisse; And hím in armes took, and gan him kisse. Wbat that I mene, O swete berte dere? Quod Troilus, O goodly fresshe freel That, with the stremes of your eyen clere, Ye wolde somtyme freendly on me see And thanne agreen that I may ben be, Withoute braunche of vyce in any wyse, In trouthe alwey to doon yow my servy This Troilus, that herde his lady preye Of lordship bim, wex neither quik ne deed, Ne mighte a word for shame to it seye Although men sholde smyten of bis heed. But Lord, so he wex sodeinliche reed And sire, bis lesson, that he wende conne, To preyen bir,is thurgh his wit yronne. fil Pandarus on knees, and up bís yën Tohevene threw, and beld his hondes hye: I mene, of this mayst glorifye; And Venus, thou mayst make melodye; Withouten bond, me semeth that in towne, for this merveyle, I here ech belle sowne. Whan every wight was voided but they two, And alle the dores were faste yshette, To telle in short, withoute wordes mo, This Pandarus, withouten any lette, Up roos, and on hís beddes syde hím sette, And gan to speken in a sobre wyse To Troilus, as I shal yow devyse. Myn alderlevest lord, and brother dere, God woot, and thou, that it sat me so sore, Wben I thee saw so languisshing toyere, for love, of which thy wo wex alwey more; That I, with al my might and al my lore, Have ever sithen doon my bisinesse To bringe thee to joye out of distresse; Cryseyde al this aspyede wel ynough, for she was wys, and lovede him neverthelasse, Al nere he malapert, or madeit tough, Or was to bold, to singe a fool a masse. But whan bis shame gan somwhat to passe, His resons, as I may my rymes bolde, I yow wol telle,as techen bokes olde. As to my lady right and chief resort With al my wit and al my diligence, And I to ban, right as yow list, comfort Under your yerde, egal to myr And that ye deigne me so muche honoure Me to comaunden ought in But hol no more as now of this matere, this folk wol comen up anoon, tre red; lo, I hem bere. njure thee, Criseyde, and oon, And two, thou Troilus, whan thow mayst goon, That at myn bous ye been at my warninge, forI ful wel shal shape your cominge; And have it brought to swich plyt as thou wost, So that, thorugh me, thow stondest now in weye To fare wel, I seyeit for no bost, And wostow why? for shame it is to seye, for thee have I bigonne a gamen pleye Which that I never doon shal eft for other, Although he were a thousand fold my brother. And I to ben your verray humble trewe, Secret, and in my paynes pacient, And evermo desire freshly newe, To serven, and been ylyke ay diligent, And, with good herte, al holly your talent Receyven wel, how sore that me smerte, Lo, this mene I, myn owene swete berte In chaunged vois, right for his verrey drede, Wbich vois eek quook, and therto bis manere Goodly abayst, and now his hewes rede, Now pale, unto Criseyde, his lady dere, With look doun cast and humble yolden chere, Lo, the alderfirste word that him asterte Was,twyes: Mercy, mercy, swete herte1 And eseth ther your bertes right ynough; And lat see wbich of yow shal bere the belle ove aright1therwith he lough e ye a layser for to telle. Quod Troilus: How longe shal I dwelle Er this be doon? Quod he: han thou mayst And stinte a whyl, and whan he mighte outbringe, Quod Pandarus: Lo, bere an bard request And resonable, a lady for to werne Now, nece myn, by natal Joves Were I a god, ye sholde sterveas That heren wel, this man wolnothing yerne But your honour, and seen bimalmost sterve And been so looth to suffren him yow serve That is to seye, for thee am I bicomen, Bitwixen game and ernest, swich a mene Hs maken wommen unto men to comen; Hl sey I nought, thou wost wel what I mene. for thee have I my nece, of vyces clene, So fully maad thy gentilesse triste, That al shal been right as thyselve liste. This thing shal be right as I yow devyse. The nexte word was: God wot, for I have, Hs feythfully as I have had konninge, Ben youres, also God my sowle save; And shal, til that I, woful wight, be grave. And though I dar ne can unto yow pleyne, Ywis, I suffre nought the lasse peyne. With that Eleyne and also Deiphebus Tho comen upward, right at the steyres ende; than gan grone Troilus, nd bis suster for to blende. Quod Pandarus: It tyme is that we wende; Cak,nece myn, your leve at alle three, And lat hem speke, and cometh forth with me. But God, that al wot, take I to witnesse, That never I this for coveityse wroughte, But only for to abregge that distresse, for which wel nygh thou deydest, as me thoughte. But gode brother, do now as thee oughte, for Goddes love, and keep hir out of blame, Sin thou art wys, and save alwey hir name. Thus muche asnow, O wommanliche wyf, I may outbringe, and if this yow displese, That shal I wreke upon myn owne lyf Right sone, I trowe, and doon your herte an ese, If with my deeth your herte I may apese. But sin that ye han herd me somwhat seye, Now recche I never how sone that I deye. With that she gan bir eyen on him caste ful esily, and ful debonairly Hvysing hir, and byed nott With never a word, but seyde him softely Myn honour sauf, I wol wel trewely And in swich forme as he can now devyse, Receyven hím fully to my servyse, Therwith his manly sorwe to biholde, It mighte han maad an berte of stoon to rewe; And Pandare weep as he to watre wolde And poked ever his nece newe and newe, And seyde: Wo bigon ben bertes trewe for love of God, make of this thing an ende, Or slee us bothe at ones, er that ye wende. I? what? quod she, by God and by my trouthe, I noot nought what ye wilne that I seye. I? what? quod he, that ye han on him routhe, 502 for wel thou wost, the name as yet of here Among the peple, as who seyth, halwedis; for that man is unbore, I dar wel swere, That ever wiste that she dide amis But wo is me, that I, that cause al this, May thenken that she is my nece dere, And I hir eem, and traytor eek yferel Biseching him, for Goddes love, that he Wolde, in honour of trouthe and gentilesse Hs I wel mene, eek mene wel to And myn honour, with wit and besinesse, Hy kepe; and if I may don bim gladnesse, from hennesforth,ywis, I nil not feyne: Now bèeth al hool, no lenger ye ne pleyne. She took hir leve at hem ful thriftily, As she wel coude, and they hir reverence Into the fulle diden hardely, And speken wonder wel, in hir absence, Of hir, in preysing of hir excellence, Hir governaunce, hír wit; and hir manere Commendeden, it joye was to here. But nathelees, this warne I yow, quod she, A kinges sone although ye be, ywis, Ye shul namore have soverainetee Of me in love, than right in that cas is; Ne I nil forbere, if that ye doon amis, To wrathen yow; and whyl that ye me serve, Cherycen yow right after ye deserve. And shortly, derë herte and al my knight, Now lat hír wende unto hir owne place, And torne we to Troilus ayein, That gan ful lightly of the lettre passe, That Deiphebus hadde in the gardin seyn. And of Eleyne and him be wolde fayn Delivered been, and seyde, that him leste Toslepe,and after tales have reste. Eleyne him kíste, and took hir leve blyve, Deiphebus eek,and hoom wente every wight; And Pandarus, as faste as be may dryve, To Troilus tho com, as lyne ríght; And on a paillet, al that glade night, By Troilus he lay, with mery chere, Co tale; and wel was hem they were yfere. kk4 And were it wist that I, through myn engyn, Hadde in my nece yput this fantasye, To do thy lust, and hoolly to be thyn, Why, al the world upon it wolde crye, And seye, that I the worste trecherye Dide in this cas, that ever was bigonne, And she forlost, and thou right nought ywonne. Wherfore, er I wol ferther goon a pas, Yet eft I thee biseche and fully seye, That privetee go with us in thís cas, That is to seye, that thou us never wreye; And be nought wrooth, though I thee ofte preye To holden secree swich an beigh matere; for skilful is, thow wost wel, my preyere. Troilus and And thenk what wo ther hath bitid er this, Criseyde. for makinge of avauntes, as men rede; LiberMI. And what mischaunce in this world yet ther is, fro day to day, right for that wikked dede; for which these wyse clerkes that ben dede Han ever yet proverbed to us yonge, That firste vertu is to kepe tonge. ho mighte telle alf the joye or feste Which that the sowle of Troilus tho felte, Heringe theffect of Pandarus bibeste? His olde wo, that made bis herte swelte, Gan tho for joye wasten and tomelte, And al the richesse of bis sykes sore At ones fledde, he felte of hem no mor And, nere it that I wilne as now tabregge Diffusioun of speche, I coude almost A thousand olde stories thee alegge Of wommen lost, thorugh fals and foles bost; Proverbes canst thyself ynowe, and wost, Hyeíns that vyce, for to been a labbe, Ai seyde men sooth as often as they gabbe. But right so as these holtes and these ayes That han in winter dede been and dreye Revesten bem in grene, whan that May is, Whan every lusty lyketh best to pleye: Right in that selve wyse, sooth to seye, Wex sodeynliche bis berte ful of joye That gladder was ther never man in Tr And, that thou knowe I thenke nought ne wene Chat this servyse a shame be or jape, Cassandre, Eleyne, or any of the frape; Be she never so faire or wel yshape, Telme, which thou wilt of everichone, Co han for thyn, and lat me thanne allone. But bhe that goth, for gold or for richesse, On swich message, calle him what thee list; And this that thou dost, calle it gentilesse, Compassioun, and felawship, and trist; Departe it so, for wyderwhere is wist how that there is dyversitee requered Bitwixen thinges lyke, as I have lered. O tonge, allas1 so often herebíforn Hastow mad many a lady bright of hewe Seyd: Welawey1 the day that I was born1 And many a maydes sorwes for to newe; And, for the more part, al is untrewe That men of yelpe, and it were brought to preve; Of kinde non avauntour is to leve. And gan bís look on Pandarus up caste ful sobrely,and frendly for to see, And seyde: freend, in Aprille the laste As wel thou wost, if it remembre thee, How neigh the deeth for wo thou foundeme And how thou didest al thy bisinesse To knowe of me the cause of my distresse. But thilke litel that they speke or wroughte, His wyse goost took ay of al swich hede, It semed hir, he wiste what she thoughte Withouten word, so that it was no nede To bidde him ought to done, or ought forbede; for which she thoughte that love, al comeit late, Of alle joye hadde opned hir the yate. Avauntour and a lyere, al ís on; Hs thus: I pose, a womman graunte me Hir love, and seyth that other wol she non, And I am sworn to holden it secree, And after I go telle it two or three; Ywis, I am avauntour at the leste, And lyere, for I breke my bibeste. But sin that thou bast don me this servyse, My lyf to save, and for noon hope of mede, of God, this grete empryse ne it out; for now is moste nede. for high and low, withouten any drede, I wolalwey thyne hestes alle kepe; ħave now good night, and lat us bothe slepe. And shortly of this proces for to pace, So wel his werk and wordes he bisette, That he so ful stood in his lady grace, That twenty thousand tymes, or she lette, She thonked God she ever with him mette; So coude he him governe in swich servyse, That al the world ne mighte it bet devyse. But it was spoken in so short a wyse, In swich awayt alwey, and in swich fere, Lest any wyght divynen or devyse Wolde of hem two, or to it leye an ere, That al this world so leef to hem ne were Hs that Cupido wolde hem grace sende To maken of hir speche aright an ende. Thou wost how longe lit forbar to seye To thee, that art the man that I best triste; And peril was it noon to thee bywreye, That wiste I wel; but tel me, if thee liste, Sith I so looth was that thyself it wiste, How dorste I mo tellen of this matere, That quake now, and no wight may us here? Now loke thanne, if they be nought to blame, Swich maner folk; what shal I clepe hem, what, That hem avaunte of wommen, and by name, That never yet bihighte hem this ne that, Ne knewe hem more than myn olde hat? No wonder is, so God me sende hele, Though wommen drede with us men to dele. Thus held him ech with other wel apayed, That al the world ne mighteit bet amende; And, on the morwe, whan they were arayed, to his owene nedes gan entende. But Troilus, though as the fyr he brende for sharp desyr of hope and of plesaunce, henot forgat his gode governaunce. But natheles, by that God I thee swere That, as hím list, may al this world And, if I lye, Hchilles with Myn herte cleve, al were my As I am mortal, if I late or yerne Wolde it biwreye, or dorste, or sholde conne, for al the good that God made under sonne: forwby she fond him so discreet in al, So secret, and of swich obëisaunce, That wel she felte he was to hir a wal Of steel, and sheld from every displesaunce; That, to ben in his gode governaunce, So wys he was,she was no more afered, I mene, as fer as oughte ben requered. I sey not this for no mistrust of yow, Ne for no wys man, but for foles nyce, And for the harm that in the world is now, Hs wel for foly ofte as for malyce; for wel wot I, in wyse folk, that vyce No womman drat, if she be wel avysed; for wyse ben by foles harm chastysed. But now to purpos; leve brother dere, Have al this thing that I have seyd in minde, And keep thee clos, and be now of good chere, for at thy day thou shalt me trewe finde. I shal thy proces sette in swich a kinde, And God toforn, that it shall thee suffyse, for it shal been right as thou wolt devyse. for wel I woot, thou menest wel, parde; Therfore I dar this fully undertake. Thou wost eek what thy lady graunted thee, And day is set, the chartres up to make. Have now good night, I may no lenger wake; And bid for me, sin thou art now in blisse, That God me sende deeth or sone lisse. But in himself with manbod gan restreyne Ech rakel dede and ech unbrydled chere, That alle tho that liven, sooth to seyne, Ne sholde han wist, by word or by manere, What that he mente, as touching this matere. from every wight as fer as is the cloude he was, so wel dissímulen he coude. And Pandarus, to quike alwey the fyr, Was ever ylyke prest and diligent; To ese his frend was set al hís desyr. He shof ay on, be to and fro was sent; He lettres bar whan Troilus was absent. TChat never man, as in his freendes nede, Ne bar him bet than he, withouten drede. That rather deye I wolde, and determyne, As thinketh me, now stokked in presoun In wrecchednesse, in filthe, and in vermyne Caytif to cruel king Agamenoun And this, in alle the temples of this toun, Upon the goddes alle, I wol thee swere, Tomorwe day, if that thee lyketh here. And al the whyl which that I yow devyse, This was his lyf; with al his fulle might, By day he was in Martes hígh servyse, Chis is to seyn, in armes as a knight; And for the more part, the longe night belay, and thoughte how that he mighte serve His lady best, hir thank for to deserve. And that thou bast so muche ydoon for me, That I ne may it nevermore deserve, This knowe I wel, al mighte I now for thee A thousand tymes on a morwen sterve I can no more, but that I wol thee serve Right as thy sclave, whiderso thou wende, for evermore, unto my lyves endel But here, with al myn herte, I thee biseche, That never in me thou deme swich folye Hs I shal seyn; me thoughte, by thy speche, That this, which thou me dost for companye, I sholde wene it were a bauderye; I am nought wood, alif I lewed be; It is not so, that woot I wel, pardee. But now, paraunter, som man wayten wolde That every word, or sonde, or look, or chere Of Troilus that I rehersen sholde, In al this wbyle, unto hís lady dere; I trowe it were a long thing for to here; Or of what wight that stant in swich disjoynte, Hís wordes alle, or every look, to poynte. Nil I nought swerë, although he lay softe, Thatin his thought he nas sumwhat disesed, Ne that he tornede on his pilwes ofte, And wolde of that him missed han ben sesed; But in swich cas man is nought alwey plesed, for ought I wot, no more than was he; That can I deme of possibilitee. But certeyn is, to purpos for to go, That in this whyle, as writen is in geste, Hesay his lady somtyme; and also She with him spak, whan that she dorste or leste, And by hir bothe avys, as was the beste, Apoynteden ful warly in this nede, So as they dorste, how they wolde procede. for sothe, I have not herd it doon er this, In storye noon, ne no man here, I wene; And though I wolde I coude not, ywis; for ther was som epistel hem bitwene, That wolde, as seyth myn auctor, wel contene Neigh half this book, of which him list not wryte; How sholde I thanne a lyne of it endyte? But to the grete effect: than sey I thus, That stonding in concord and in quiete Chise ilke two, Criseyde and Troilus, As I have told, and in this tyme swete, Save only often mighte they not mete, Ne layser have bir speches to fulfelle, That it befel right as I shal yow telle, Troílus and That Pandarus, that ever díde his might Críseyde. Right for the fyn that I shal speke of here, LiberMI. As for to bringe to his hous som night His faire nece, and Troilus yfere, Wheras at leyser al this heigh matere, Touching hir love, were at the fulle upbounde, Hadde out of doute a tyme to it founde. Sone after this, to bim she gan torowne, And asked bim if Troilus were there And seyde: Nece, I pose that be were, Yow thurfte never have the more fere. Me were lever a thousandsfold to dy for he with greet deliberacioun Hadde every thing that herto mighte avayle forncast, and put in execucioun, And neither laft for cost ne for travayle; Come if hem lest, hem sholde nothing fayle; And for to been in ought espyed there, That, wiste he wel, an inpossible were. Nought list myn auctor fully to declare What that she thougbte whan be seydeso That Troilus was out of town yfare, As if he seyde therof sooth or no; And, as hís nece, obeyed as bír ought Che bente mone with hir hornes pale, That swich a rayn from hevene gan avale, That every maner womman that was there Hadde of that smoky reyn a verray fere; At which Pandare tho lough, and seyde thenne: And dredelees, it cleer was in the wind Of every pye and every lettesgame; Now al is wel, for al the world is blind In this matere, bothe fremed and tame. This tímber is al redy up to frame; Us lakketh nought but that we witen wolde A certein houre, in whiche she comen sholde. But goode nece, if I mighte ever plese yow anything, than prey I yow, quod he, Co doon myn berte as now so greet an ese As for to dwelle bere al this night with me, forwhy this is your owene bous, pardee. for, by my trouthe, I sey it nought agame, To wende as now, it were to me a shame. But nathelees, yet gan she bim biseche, Although with him to goon it was no fere for to be war of goosish peples sped That dremen thinges whiche that ner Loke al be wel, and do now as y Criseyde, whiche that coude as muche good Hs bal , as good chep may I dwellen here, And graunte it gladly with a freendes chere, And bave a thank, as grucche and thanne abyde; for hoom to goon it may nought wel bityde. And Troilus, that al thís purveyaunce Knew at the fulle, and waytede on it ay, Hadde bereupon eek maad gret ordenaunce And founde hís cause, and therto hís aray, If that he were missed, night or day, Cherwhyle he was aboutethis servyse, That he was goon to doon his sacrifyse, But Pandarus, that wel coude eche a del The olde daunce, and every poynt therinne, Whan that he sey that alle thing was wel, He thoughte he wolde upon his werk biginne, And ganne the stewesdore al softe unpinne, And stille as stoon, withouten lenger lette, By Troilus adoun right he him sette. He swor bir: Vis, by stokkes and by stones, And by the goddes that in hevene dwell Or elles were him lever, soule and b With Pluto king as depe been Hs Tantalus bat sholde I more telle Whan al was wel, he roos and took his leve And she to souper com, whan it was And moste at swich a temple alone wake, Answered of Appollo for to be; And first, to seen the holy laurer quake, Er that Apollo spak out of the tree, To telle him next wban Grekes sholden flee, And forthy lette him no man, God forbede, But preye Apollo helpen in this nede. I wol, quod she, myn uncle leef and dere, st, it skile is to be so; yow to dwellen here; agame, I wolde go ywis, graunt mercy, necel quod be tho; Wereit a game or no, soth for to telle, Now am I glad, sin that yow list to dwelle. With a certayn of hir owene men, And with bir faire nece Antigone, And othere of hir wommen nyne or ten; But who was glad now, who, as trowe ye, But Troilus, that stood and mighte it see Thurghout a litel windowe in a stewe Ther he bishet, sin midnight, was in mewe, And, shortly to the poynt right for to gon, Of al this werk he tolde him word and ende, Hnd seyde: Make thee redy rigbtanon, for thou shalt into hevene blisse wende. Now blisful Venus, thou me grace sende, Quod Troilus, for never yet no nede Hadde I er now, ne halvendel the drede. So whan that she was in the closet leyd, And alle hir wommen forth by ordenaunce Hbedde weren, ther as I have seyd, There was no more to skippen nor to traunce, But boden go to bedde, with mischaunce, If any wight was steringe anywhere, And late hem slepe that abedde were. But Pandare up, and shortly for to seyne, Right sone upon the chaunging of the mone, Uhan lightles is the world a night or tweyne, And that the welken shoop him for to reyne, He streight amorwe unto his nece wente; Ye han wel herd the fyn of his entente. Whan he was come, he gan anoon to pleye Hs he was wont, and of himself to jape; And fynally, he swor and gan hir seye By thís and that, she sholde him not escape, Ne lenger doon hím after bir to gape; But certeynly she moste, by hir leve, Come soupen in his hous with him at eve. At wbiche she lough, and gan hír faste excuse, And seyde: It rayneth; lo, how sholde I goon? Lat be, quod he, ne stond not thus to muse; This moot be doon, ye shal be ther anoon. So at the laste herof they felle at oon, Or elles, softe he swor hir in hir ere, He nolde never come ther she were. 506 Thus al is wel; but tho bigan aright Thenewe joye, and al the feste agayn; But Pandarus,if goodiy hadde he might, he wolde han hyed bir to bedde fayn, And seyde: Lord, this is an huge rayn1 This were a weder for to slepen inne; And that I rede us sone to biginne. Quod Pandarus: Ne drede thee never a del, forit shal been right as thou wilt desyre; So thryve I, this night shal I make it wel, Or casten al the gruwel in the fyre. Yit blisful Venus, this night thou me enspyre, Quod Troilus, as wis as I thee serve, And ever bet and bet shal, til I sterve. Unwist of every wight but of Pandare? But to the poynt; now whan she was ycome With alle joye, and alle frendes far Hir eem anoon in armes hath hir nome, And after to the souper, alle and some, Whan tyme was, ful softe they hem sette; God wot, ther was no deyntee for to fette. Tho Pandarus, hír eem, right as hím oughte, With women swiche as were hir most aboute, ful glad unto hir beddes syde bir broughte, And toke bis leve, and gan ful lowe loute And seyde: Here at this closetsdore withoute, Right oversthwart, your wommen liggen alle, That, whom yow liste of hem, ye may here calle. And nece, woot ye wher I wol yow leye, for that we shul not liggen fer asonder, And for ye neither shullen, dar I seye, heren noise of reynes nor of thonder? By God, right in my lyte closet yonder. And I wol in that outer hous allone Be wardeyn of your wommen everichone. And after souper gonnen they to ryse, At ese wel, with hertes fresshe and glade, And wel was him that coude best devyse To lyken bir, or that hir laughen made. He song; she pleyde; he tolde tale of Wade. But at the laste, as every thing hath ende, She took hir leve, and nedes wolde wende But O, fortune, executrice of wierdes, O influences of thise hevenes hye Soth is, that, under God, ye ben our hierdes Though to us bestes been the causes wrye This mene I now, for she gan hoomward bye, But execut was al bisyde hir leve, At the goddes wil; for which she moste bleve. And if I badde, O Venus ful of mirthe, Hspectes badde of Mars or of Saturne Or thou combust or let were in my birthe, Chy fader prey al thilke harm disturne Of grace, and that I glad ayein may turne, for love of him thou lovedest in the shawe, I mene Adoon, that with the boor was slawe. And in this middel chaumbre that ye see Shul youre wommen slepen wel and softe; And ther I seyde shal yourselve be; And if ye liggen wel tonight, com ofte, And careth not what weder is onslofte. The wyn anon, and whan so that yow leste, So go we slepe, I trowe it be the beste. Ther nis no more, but hereafter sone, Thevoydë dronke, and travers drawe anon, Gan every wight, that hadde nought to done More in that place, out of the chaumber gon. And evermo so sternelich it ron, And blew therwith so wonderliche loude, That wel neigh no man heren other coude. O Jove eek, for the love of faire Europe, The whiche in forme of bole away thou fette; Now help, O Mars, thou with thy blody cope, for love of Cipris, thou me nought ne lette; O Phebus, thenk whan Dane birselven shette Ander the bark, and laurer wex for drede, Yet for hir love, O help now at this nede1 Mercurie, for the love of Hiersë eke, for which Pallas was with Aglauros wrooth Now help, and eek Diane, I thee biseke, That this viage be not to thee looth. O fatal sustren, which, er any clooth Me shapen was, my destenë me sponne, So helpeth to this werk that is bigonnel Quod Pandarus:Thouwrecchedmouses herte Art thou agast so that she wol thee byte? Wby, don this furred cloke upon thy sherte, And folowe me, for I wol han the wyte; But byd, and lat me go bifore a lyte. And with that word he gan undo a trappe, And Troilus he broughte in by the lappe. That, but he be al fully wood by this, he sodeynly mot falle into wodnesse, But if God belpe; and cause why this is be seyth bim told is, of a freend of his, for sorwe of which this night shalt b The sterne wind so loude gan to route That no wight other noyse mighte here; And they that layen at the dore withoute, ful sykerly they slepten alle yfere; And Pandarus, with a fut sobre chere, Goth to the dore anon withouten lette, Theras they laye, and softely it shette. So shalI do tomorwe, ywis, quod she, And God toforn, so that it shal suffyse. Tomorwe allas, that werea fayr, quod be, Nay, nay,it may not stonden in this wyse; for, nece myn, thus wryten clerkes wyse, That perilis with drecching in ydrawe; Nay,swich abodes been nought worth an hawe. Gan sodeynly aboute bir berte colde Hllas1 I wende, whoso tales to Nece,alle thing bath tyme, I dar avowe; for whan a chaumber afyr is, or an balle, Welmore nedeis, it sodeynly rescowe Than to dispute, and axe amonges alle howis this candele in the straw yfalle? Al benedicitel for al among that fare The harm is doon, and farewel feldefare1 And as he com ayeinward prívely, His nece awook, and asked: ho goth there? My dere nece, quod be, it am I Ne wondreth not, ne have of it no fere; And ner he com, and seyde hir in bir ere: No word, for love of God I yow biseche; Lat no wight ryse and heren of our speche. Horastet allas1 and falsen Troilus? I knowe him not, God belpe me so, quod she Allas1 what wikked spirit tolde bim thus? Now certes, eem, tomorwe,and I bim see, I shal therof as ful excusen me As ever dide womman, if hím lyke; And with that word she gan ful sore syke What1 which wey be ye comen, benedicite? Quod she, and how thus unwist of hem alle? Here at thís secre trappesdore, quod be. Quod tho Criseyde:Lat me som wight calle. Ey1 God forbede that it sholde falle, Quod Pandarus, that ye swich foly wroughte1 They mighte deme thing they never er thoughte1 And, nece myn, ne take it not agreef, If that ye suffre him al night in this wo, God help me so, ye hadde him never leef, Chat dar I seyn, now there is but we two; But wel I woot, that ye wol not do so; Ye been to wys to do so gret folye, To putte his lyf al night in jupartye. God1 quod she, so worldly selinesse, Which clerkes callen fals felicitee Ymedled is with many a ful anguisshous than is, God woot,quod she Condicioun of veyn prosperitee for either joyes comen nought yfere, Or elles no wight hath hem alwey here It is nought good a sleping hound to wake, Ne yeve a wight a cause to devyne; Your wommen slepen alle, I undertake, So that, for hem, the hous men mighte myne; And slepen wolen til the sonne shyne. And whan my tale al brought is to an ende, Anwist, right as I com, so wol I wende. Hadde I bim never leef? By God, I wene Ve badde never thing so leef, quod she. thrift, quod he, that shal be sene; ake this ensample of me, If I al night wolde him in sorwe see foral the tresour in the toun of Troye, I bidde God, I never mote have joyel O brotel wele of mannes joye unstablel With what wight so thou be, or how thou pley Either he woot that thou, joye,art mual Or woot it not, it moot ben oon of t Now if he woot it not, how may he seye That he hath verray joye and selinesse That is of ignoraunce ay in derknesse? Now loke thanne, if ye, that been his love, Shul putte al night his lyf in jupartye forthing of nought1 Now, by that God above, ought only this delay comth of folye, Butof malyce, if that I shal nought lye. What, platly, and ye suffre him in distresse, Yeneither bountee doon ne gentilesse1 Now nece myn, ye shul wel understonde, Quod be, so as ye wommen demen alle, That for to holde in love a man in honde, And bim bir Leef and Dere herte calle, And maken hím an howve above a calle, I mene, as love an other in this whyle, She doth hírself a shame, and hím a gyle. Now if he woot that joye is transitorie, As every joye of worldly thing mot fle Than every tyme he that hath in r The drede of lesing maketh bim that he May in no parfit selinesse be And if to lese his joye be set a myte, Than semeth it that joye is worth ful lyte. Now wherby that I telle yow al this? Ye woot yourself, as wel as any wight, How that your love al fully graunted is To Troilus, the worthieste knight, Oon of this world, and therto trouthe plyght, That, but it were on him along, ye nolde Him never falsen, whyl ye liven sholde. Quod tho Criseyde: Wole ye doon o thing, And ye therwith shal stinte al his disese; bave here, and bereth him this blewe ring, for ther is nothing mighte him bettre plese, Save Imyself, ne more his herte apese; And sey my dere herte, that his sorwe Is causeles, that shal be seen tomorwe. Wherfore I wol deffyne in this matere, That trewely, for ought I can espye, Ther is no verray wele in this world here But O, thou wikked serpent jalousye, Thou misbeleved and envious folye, Why hastow Troilus me mad untriste, That never yet agilte him, that I wiste? Now stant it thus, that sith I fro yow wente, This Troilus, right platly for to seyn, Is thurgh a goter, by a privë wente, Into my chaumbre come in al this reyn, Unwist of every maner wight, certeyn, Save of myself, as wisly have I joye, And by that feith I shal Pryam of Troyel 508 Quod Pandarus: Thus fallen is this cas. by, uncle myn, quod she, who tolde him this) Wby doth my dere herte thus,allas? Ye woot, ye nece myn, quod be, whatis; I hope al shal be wel that is amis for ye may quenche al this, if that yow leste, And doth right so, for I holde it the beste. Hring?quod he, ye, haselswodes shaken1 Ve,nece myn, that ring moste han a stoon Chatmighte dede men alyve maken; And swich a ring, trowe I that ye have noon. Discrecioun out of your beed is goon; Chat fele I now, quod he, and that is routhe; Otyme ylost, welmaystow cursen slouthel Wot ye not wel that noble and heigh corage Nesorweth not, ne stinteth eek for lyte? But if a fool were in a jalous rage, I nolde setten at his sorwe a myte, But feffe him with a fewe wordes wbyte Another day, whan that I mighte bim finde: But this thing stont al in another kinde. This is so gentil and so tendre of herte, That with his deeth he wol his sorwes wreke; for trusteth wel, how sore that him smerte, He wol to yow no jalouse wordes speke. And forthy, nece, er that his herte breke, So spek yourself to him of this matere; for with o word ye may his berte stere. Now have I told what peril he is inne, And his coming unwist to every wight; Ne, pardee, harm may ther be noon ne sinne; I wol myself be with yow al this night. Ye knowe eek how itis your owne knight, And that, by right, ye moste upon him triste, And I al prest to fecche him whan yow liste. This accident so pitous was to here, And eek so lyk a sooth, at pryme face, And Troilus hir knight to hir so dere, His privë coming, and the siker place, That, though that she dide him as thanne a grace, Considered alle thinges as they stode, No wonder is, sin she dide al for gode. Cryseyde answerde: Hs wisly God at reste My sowle bringe, as me is for him wol And eem, ywis, fayn wolde I doon the beste, If that I hadde grace to do so. But whether that ye dwelle or for him go, I am, til God me bettre minde sende, At dulcarnon, right at my wittes ende. Quod Pandarus: Ye, nece, wol ye here? Dulcarnon called is fleminge of wrecches; It semeth hard, for wrecches wol not lere for verray slouthe or othere wilful tecches; This seyd by hem that be not worth two fecches. But ye ben wys, and that we han on honde Nis neither hard, ne skilful to withstonde. Thanne, eem, quod she, doth herof as yow list; But er he come I wil up frst aryse; And, for the love of God, sin al my trist Is on yow two, and ye ben bothe wyse, So wircheth now in so discreet a wyse, That I honour may bave, and he plesaunce; for I am here al in your governaunce. That is wel seyd, quod be, my nece dere, Ther good thrift on that wyse gentil hertel But liggeth stille, and taketh him right bere, It nedeth not no ferther for him sterte; And ech of yow ese otheres sorwes smerte, for love of God; and, Venus, I thee herie; for sone hope I we shulle ben alle merie. This Troilus ful sone on knees him sette ful sobrely, right by hir beddes heed, And in his beste wyse his lady grette; But Lord, so she wex sodeynliche reed1 Ne, though men sholden smyten of bir heed, She coude nought a word aright outbringe So sodeynly, for his sodeyn cominge. But Pandarus, that so wel coude fele In every thing, to pleye anoon bigan, And seyde: Nece, see how this lord can knele1 Now, for your trouthe, seeth this gentil man1 And with that word he for a quisshen ran, And seyde: Kneleth now, whyl that yow leste, Ther God your hertes bringe sone at reste1 But O, thou Jove, O auctor of nature, Is this an honour to thy deitee Chat folk ungiltif suffren bere injure And who that giltif is,al quit gotht O were it leful for to pleyne on thee That undeserved suffrest jalousye And that I wolde upon thee pleyn Can I not seyn, for she bad him not ryse, If sorwe it putte out of hir remembraunce, Or elles if she toke it in the wyse Of duëtee, as for his observaunce; But wel finde I she dide him this plesaunce, That she bim kiste, although she syked sore; And bad him sitte adoun withouten more. And in his minde he gan the tyme acurse That he cam there, and that he was born; for now is wikke yturned into worse, And al that labour be bath doon biforn, he wende it lost, be thoughte he nas but lorn. O Pandarus, thougbte he, allast thy wyle Serveth of nought, so weylawey the whylel Eek al my wo is this, that folk now usen To seyn right thus: Ve, jalousyeis lovel And wolde a bussbel venim al excusen for that o greyn of love is on it shovel But that wot heighe God that sit above, Ifit be lyker love, or bate, or grame; And after that, it oughte bere his name. And therwithal he heng adoun the beed, And fil on knees, and sorwfully be sighte; What mighte he seyn? he felte be nas but deed, for wrooth was she that shulde bis sorwes lighte. But nathelees, whan that he speken mighte, Than seyde be thus: God woot, thatof this game, Whan al is wist, than am I not to blamel Quod Pandarus: for ought I can espyen, This light nor I ne serven bere of nought; Lightis not good for syke folkes yën. But for the love of God, sin ye be brought In thus good plyt, lat now non hevy thought Ben hanginge in the hertes of yow tweye: And bar the candele to the chimeneye. And therwithal hir arm over bim she leyde, And al foryaf, and ofte tyme him keste. He thonked hir, and to hir spak, and seyde As fil to purpos for his herte reste. And she to that answerde him as hir leste; And with hir goodly wordes him disporte She gan, and ofte his sorwes to comforte. Quod Pandarus: Now wol ye wel biginne; Now doth him sitte, gode nece dere, Apon your beddes syde al there withinne, That ech of yow the bet may other here. And with that word he drow him to the fere, And took a light, and fond his contenaunce As for to loke upon an old romaunce. But certeyn is, some maner jalousye Is excusable more than som, ywis. Hs whan cause is, and som swich fantasye With pietee so welrepressed is That it unnethe dooth or seyth amis, But goodty drinketh upal bis distresse; And that excuse I, for the gen Cherwith the sorwe so bis berte shette, Chat from his eyen fil ther not a tere, they astoned and oppressed were. The feling of his sorwe, or of his fere, Orof ought elles, fled was out of towne; And doun he fel al sodeynly aswowne. Sone after this, though it no nede were, Whan she swich othes as hir list devyse Hadde of him take, hir thoughte tho no fere, Ne cause eek non, to bidde him thennes ryse. Yet lesse thing than othes may suffyse In many a cas; for every wight, I gesse, That loveth wel meneth but gentilesse. Criseyde, that was Troilus lady right, And cleer stood on a ground of sikernesse, Al thoughte she, bir servaunt and bir knight Ne sholde of right non untrouthe in bir gesse, Yet nathelees, considered his distresse, And that love is in cause of swich folye, Thus to hím spak she of hís jelousye: And som so ful of furie is and despyt, That it sourmounteth his repres But berte myn, ye be not in that That thanke I God, for whiche your passioun I wol not calle it but illusi Of habundaunce of love and bisy cure That dooth your herte this disese end This was no litel sorwe for to see; Butal was hust, and Pandare up as faste: ees, or we be lost, quod he, nought agast but certeyn, at the laste, for this or that, he into bedde him caste, And seyde: O theef, is this a mannes herte? And of herente al to his bare sherte; But in effect she wolde wite anoon Of what man, and eek where, and also why He jelous was, sin ther was cause noon; And eek the signe, that he took it by, She bad him that to telle hir bisily, Or elles, certeyn, she bar bim on honde, That this was doon of malis, hír to fonde. Lo, herte myn, as wolde the excellence Of love, ayeins the which that no man may, Ne oughte eek goodly maken resistence; And eek bycause I felte wel and say Your grete trouthe,and servyse every day; And that your herte al myn was, sooth to seyne, This droof me for to rewe upon your peyne. Of which I am right sory, but not wrooth; But, for my devoir and your hertes rest Wherso yow list, by ordal By sort, or in what wyse so for love of God, lat preve it for the bestel And if that I be giltif, do me deye Allas1 what mighte I more doon or seye? And seyde: Nece, but ye helpe us now, our owne Troilus is lorn1 o wolde I, and I wiste how, ful fayn, quod she; allas1 that I was born1 Ye, nece, wol ye pullen out the thorn That stiketh in his herte? quod Pandare; Sey Al foryeve, and stint is al thís fare1 Withouten more, shortly for to seyne, He moste obeye unto bis lady heste; And for the lasse harm, he moste feyne. He seyde hir, whan she was at swiche a feste She mighte on bim ban loked at the leste; Not I not wbat, al dere ynough arisshe, Hs he that nedes moste a cause fisshe. And your goodnesse have I founde alwey yit, Of whiche, my dere herte and al my knight I thonke it yow, as fer as I have wit Al can I nought as muche as it were right And I, emforth my conninge and my might, Have and ay shal, how sore that me smerte, Ben to yow trewe and hool, with al myn herte; With that a fewe brighte teres newe Out of hir eyen fille, and thus she seyde: Now God, thou wost, in thought ne dede untrewe To Troilus was never yet Criseyde. With that hir heed doun in the bed she leyde And with the shete it wreigh, and syghed sore, And held hir pees; not o word spak shemore. Ye, that to me, quod she, ful lever were Than al the good the sonne aboute gooth; And therwithal she swoor him in his ere: Ywis, my dere herte, I am nought wrooth, ave here my trouthe and many another ooth; Now speek to me, forit am I, Cryseyde1 But al for nought; yet mighte he not abreyde. And she answerde: Swete, al were it so, Wbat barm was that, sin I non yvel mene? for, by that God that boughte us bothe two, In alle thinge is myn entente clene. Swich arguments ne been not worth a bene; Wol ye the childish jalous contrefete? Now were it worthy that ye were ybete. And dredelees, that shal be founde at preve. But, herte myn, wbat al this is to seyne Shal wel be told, so that ye noght yow greve, Though I to yow right on yourself compleyne. for therwith mene I fynally the peyne, That halt your herte and myn in hevinesse, fully to sleen, and every wrong redresse. But now help God to quenchen al this sorwe, So hope I that he shal, for he best may; for I have seyn, of a ful misty morwe folwen ful ofte a mery someres day; And after winter folweth grene May. Men seen alday, and reden eek in stories, That after sharpe shoures been victories. My goode, myn, not I forwby ne how Chat jalousye, allas1 that wikked wivere Thus causelees is cropen into yow The harm of which I wolde fayn delivere1 Allas1 that he, al hool, or of him slivere, Shuld have his refut in so dígne a place, Ther Jove him sone out of your herte aracel 510 Thís Troílus, whan he hir wordes herde, Have ye no care, him liste not to slepe; for it thoughte him no strokes of a yerde To here or seen Criseyde his lady wepe; But wel he felte aboute his herte crepe, for every teer which that Criseyde asterte, The crampe of deeth, to streyne him by the berte. Therwith hís pous and pawmes of hís hondes They gan to frote, and wete his temples tweyne, And, to delíveren bím from bittre bondes, She ofte him kiste; and, shortly for to seyne, Him to revoken she dide al hir peyne. And at the laste, he gan his breeth to drawe, And of his swough sone after that adawe, Tho Troilus gan sorwfully to syke, Lest she bewrooth, him thoughtehis hertedeyde; And seyde: Hllasl upon my sorwes syke Have mercy,swete hertemyn,Cryseydel And if that, in tho wordes that I seyde, Be any wrong, I wolno more trespace; Do what yow list, I am al in your grace. And gan bet mínde and reson to hím take, But wonder sore he was abayst, ywis. And with a syk, whan he gan bet awake, Heseyde: O mercy, God, what thing is this? Ahy do ye with yourselven thus amis? Quod tho Criseyde; is this a mannes game? What, Troilus1 wol ye do thus, for shame? And she answerde: Of gilt misericordel That is to seyne, that I foryeve al this; And evermore on this night yow recorde, And beth wel war ye do no more amis. Nay, dere herte myn, quod he, ywis. Hnd now, quod she, that I have do yow smerte, foryeveit me, myn owene swete herte. Troílus and This Troilus, with blisse of that supprysed, Criseyde. Put al in Goddes hond, as he that mente LiberIII. Nothing but wel; and, sodeynly avysed, He hir in armes faste to bim hente. And Pandarus, with a ful good entente, Leyde him to slepe, and seyde: If ye ben wyse, Swowneth not now, lest more folk aryse. And sodeynly rescous doth him escaper Was Troilus, and bath bis lady swete; With worse hap God lat us never met Wbat mighte or may the sely larke seye, Whan that the sparbauk bath it in bhis foot? I can no more, but of thise ilke tweye, To whom this tale sucre be or soot, Chough that I tarie a yeer, somtyme I moot, After myn auctor, tellen bir gladnesse, As wel as I have told hir hevinesse. Hir armes smale, birstreyghte bakand softe, Hir sydes longe, fleshly, smothe, and whyte Thus in this hevene he gan bim to delyte, And therwithal a thousand tyme bir kiste; That, what to done, for joye unnethe forcertes, fresshe wommanliche wyf, This dar I seye, that trouthe and diligence, Nel wolnot, certeyn, breken your defence; do, present or in absence, forlove of God, lat slee me with the dede, If that it lyke unto your womanbede. ywis, quod she, myn owne bertes list, Graunt mercy, for on that is al my trist; But late us falle awey fro this matere; forit suffyseth, this that seyd is here. And at o word, withouten repentaunce, Welcome, my knight, my pees, my suffisaunce1 Criseyde, which that felte hir thus ytake, Hs writen clerkes in bir bokes olde, Right as an aspes leef she gan to quake, Whan she him felte hir in his armes folde. But Troilus, al hool of cares colde, Gan thanken tho the blisful goddes sevene; Thus sondry peynes bringen folk to hevene. Than seyde he thus: O,Love, O, Charitee, Thy moder eek, Citherea the swete After thyself next heried be sh Venus mene I, the welwilly planete And next that, Imeneus, I thee grete for never man was to yow goddes holde Hs I, which ye han brought fro cares colde. Chis Troilus in armes gan hír streyne, And seyde: O swete, as ever mote I goon, Now be ye caught, now is ther but we tweyne; Now yeldeth yow, for other boot is noon. To that Criseyde answerde thus anoon: Ne hadde I er now, my swete herte dere, Ben yolde, ywis, I were now not here1 Of hir delyt, or joyes oon the leste ye that han ben at the feste gladnesse, if that hem liste pleye1 Ican no more, but thus thise ilke tweye That night, betwixen dreed and sikernesse, felten in love the grete worthinesse.
