Benigne Love, thou holy bond of thinges, Lo, his desyr wol flee withouten wi for, noldestow of bountee her Yet were al lost, that dar I wel seyn,certes, But if thy grace passed our des O1 sooth is seyd, that beled for to be Hs of a fevre or othere greet syknesse, Men moste drinke, as men may often see, ful bittre drink; and for to han gladnesse, Men drinken often peyne and greet distresse; I mene it here, as for this aventure That thourgh a peyne hath founden al hís cure. Oblisfulnight, of hem so longe ysought, bow bl the unto hem bothe two thou werel hadde I swich on with my soule ybought, be leeste joye that was there? Awey, thou foule daunger and thou fere, And lat hem in this hevene blisse dwelle, That is so heygh, that al ne can I telle1 And for thou me, that coude leest deserve Of hem that nombred been unto thy grace Hast holpen, ther I lykly was to sterv And me bistowed in so heygh a place That thilke boundes may no blisse pace, I can no more, but laude and reverence Be to thy bounte and thyn excellencel And now swetnesse semeth more swete, That bitternesse assayed was biforn; for out of wo in blisse now they flete. Non swich they felten, sith they were born; Now is this bet, than bothe two be lorn1 for love of God, take every womman hede To werken thus, if it comth to the nede. Criseyde, al quit from every drede and tene, Hs she that juste cause hadde him to triste, Made him swich feste, it joye was to sene, Whan she his trouthe and clene entente wiste. And as aboute a tree, with many a twiste, Bitrent and wryth the sote wodesbinde, Gan eche of hem in armes other winde. Lord1 trowe ye, a coveitous, a wrecche, That blameth love and holt of it despyt, That, of tho pens that he can mokre and kecche, Was ever yet yyeve him swich delyt, As is in love, in oo poynt, in som plyt? Nay, doutelees, for also God me save, So parfit joye may no nigard havel But sooth is, though I can not tellen al, myn auctor, of his excellence, and, God toforn, I sbal al hoolly his sentence. Andif that I, at loves reverence, have any word in eched for the beste, Doth therwithal right as yourselven leste. And as the newe abaysshed nightingale, That stinteth frst whan she biginneth singe, Whan that she hereth any herde tale, Or in the hegges any wight steringe, And after siker dooth hir voys outringe; Right so Criseyde, whan hir drede stente, Opned hir herte, and tolde him hir entente. 512 Hnd therwithal Criseyde anoon be kiste, Of which, certeyn, she felte no disese And thus seyde he: Now wolde God I wiste Myn herte swete, how I yow mighte plese What man, quod be, was ever thus at ese As I, on whiche the faireste and the beste That ever I say, deyneth hir herte reste. They wol sey Yis, but Lord1 so that they lye, Tho bisy wrecches, ful of wo and dredel They callen love a woodnesse or folye, But it shal falle hem as I shal yow rede; They shul forgo the whyte and eek the rede, And live in wo, ther God yeve hem mischaunce, And every lover in his trouthe avauncel Sone after thís they speke of sondry thinges, As fil to purpos of this aventure, And pleyinge entrechaungeden hir ringes, Of which I can nought tellen no scripture; But wel I woot a broche, gold and asure, In whiche a ruby set was lyk an herte, Criseyde him yaf, and stak it on his sherte. Here may men seen that mercy passeth right; The experience of that is felt in me, That am unworthy to so swete a wight But herte myn, of your benignitee, So thenketh, though that I unworthy be Yet mot I nede amenden in som wyse, Right thourgh the vertu of your heyghe servyse. formyne wordes, here and every part, Ispeke hem alle under correccioun Of yow, that feling han in loves art, And putte it al in your discrecioun To encrese or maken diminucioun Of my langage, and that I yow biseche; But now to purpos of my rather speche. Therwith he gan hir faste in armes take, And wel an bundred tymes gan be syke, Nought swiche sorwful sykes as men make for wo, or elles whan that folk ben syke, But esy sykes, swiche as been to lyke, That shewed his affeccioun withinne; Of swiche sykes coude he nought bilinne. This Troilus ful ofte hir eyen two Gan for to kisse, and seyde: O eyen clere, It were ye that wroughte me swich wo, Ye humble nettes of my lady dere1 Though ther be mercy writen in your chere, God wot, the text ful hard is, sooth, to finde, How coude ye withouten bond me binde? Thiseilke two, that ben in armes laft, So looth to hem asonder goon it were, That ech from other wende been biraft, Or elles, lo, this was hir moste fere, That al this thing but nyce dremes were; for which ful ofte ech of bem seyde: O swete, Clippe ich yow thus, or elles I it mete? And for the love of God, my lady dere, Sin God hath wrought me for I shal yow serve, As thus I mene, that ye wol be my stere, To do me líve, if that yow liste, or sterve, So techeth me how that I may deserve Your thank, so that I, thurgh myn ignoraunce, Ne do nothing that yow be displesaunce. And, Lord1 so he gan goodly on hir see, That never his look ne bleynte from hir face, And seyde: O dere herte, may it be That it be sooth, that ye ben in this place? Ye, berte myn, God thank I of his grace1 Quod tho Criseyde, and therwithal him kiste, That where his spirit was, for joye he niste. 111 As wolde God, tho wrecches, that dispyse Servyse of love, hadde eres also longe Hs hadde Myda, ful of coveityse; And therto dronken hadde as hoot and stronge As Crassus díde for hís affectís wronge, To techen hem that they ben in the vyce, And loveres nought,although they holde hem nyce1 Thise ilke two, of whom that I yow seye, Whan that hir hertes wel assured were, Tho gonne they to speken and to pleye, And eek rehercen how, and whanne, and where, They knewe hem first, and every wo and fere That passed was; but al swich hevinesse, I thanke it God, was tourned to gladnesse. And evermo, whan that hem fel to speke Of any thing of swich a tyme agoon, With kissing al that tale sholde breke, And fallen in a newe joye anoon, And diden al hir might, sin they were oon, for to recoveren blisse and been at ese, And passed wo with joye countrepeyse. Troílus and Reson wil not that I now speke of sleep, Criseyde. for it accordeth nought to my matere LiberIII. God woot, they toke of that ful litel keep, But lest this night, that was to hem so dere, Ne sholde in veyn escape in no manere, It was biset in joye and bisinesse Of al that souneth into gentilnesse. But whan the cok, comune astrologer, Gan on his brest to bete, and after crowe, And Lucifer, the dayes messager, Gan for to ryse, and out hir bemes throwe; And estward roos, to him that coude it knowe, fortuna major, than anoon Críseyde, With herte sore, to Troilus thus seyde: Hnd eek the sonne Tytan gan he chyde, That bast the Dawing al night by thy s And suffrest bir so sone up fro the for to disesen loveres in this wy I bidde God, so yeve yow bothe so My lady right, and of my wele or wo The welle and rote, O goodly myn,Criseyde, And shal I ryse, allas l and shal I go? Now fele I that myn herte mootatwol for how sholde I my lyf an houre save, Sin that with yow is al the lyf I have? Myn bertes lyf, my trist and my plesaunce, Chat I was born, allas1 what me is wo, Chat day of us mot make desseverauncel for tyme it is to ryse, and hennes go, Or elles I am lost for evermol O night, allas1 why niltow over us hove, As longe as whanne Hlmena lay by Jove? What shal I doon, for certes, Inot how, Ne whanne, allas1 I shal the tyme se That in this plyt I may be eft with yow; And of my lyf, God woot, how that shalbe, Sin that desyr right now so byteth me That I am deed anoon, but I retour How sholde I longe, allas fro O blake night, as folk in bokes rede, That shapen art by God this world to hyde At certeyn tymes with thy derke wede That under that men migbte in reste abyde, Wel oughte bestes pleyne, and folk thee chyde, That thereas day with labour wolde us breste, That thou thus fleest, & deynest usnoughtreste1 Chou dost, allas1 to shortly thyn offyce, Thou rakel night, ther God, makere of kinde, Thee, for thyn bast and thyn unkinde vyce, So faste ay to our hemispere binde, That nevermore under the ground thou winde1 for now, for thou so hyest out of Troye, Have I forgon thus hastily my joyel But nathelees, myn owene lady bright, Yit were it so that I wiste outrely That I, your bumble servauntand your knight Were in your herte set so fermely As ye in myn, the which thing, trewely Me lever were than thise worldes tweyne, Yet sholde I bet enduren al my This Troilus, that with tho wordes felte, As thoughte him tho, for pietous distresse, The blody teres from his herte melte, As he that never yet swich hevinesse Assayed hadde, out of so greet gladnesse, Gan therwithal Criseyde his lady dere In armes streyne, and seyde in thís manere: To that Criseyde answerderight anoon, And with a syk she seyde: O herte der The game, ywis, so ferforth now is The first shal Phebus falle fro And every egle been the dowves fer And every roche out of his place sterte, Er Troilus out ofCriseydes herte1 O cruel day, accusour of the joye That night and love han stole and faste ywryen, Acursed be thy coming into Troye, for every bore hath oon of thy bright yën1 Envyous day, what list thee so to spyen? What hastow lost, why sekestow this place, Ther God thy lyght so quenche, for his grace? Allas1 what han thise loveres thee agilt Dispitous day? thyn be the pyne of helle1 for many a lovere hastow shent, and wilt; Thy pouring in wol nowher lete hem dwelle. What proferestow thy light here for to selle? Go selle it hem that smale seles graven, We wol thee nought, us nedeth no day haven. 514 Retorned to his real palaís, sone he softe into bis bed gan for to slinke, Butal for nought; he may wel ligge and winke, But sleep ne may ther in bis berte sinke; Thenkinge how she, for whom desyr him brende, A thousand fold was worth more than he wende. To which no word for sorwe she answerde, So sore gan bis parting hir destreyne; nto hís palays ferde bigon as she was, sooth to seyne; So hard him wrong of sharp desyr the peyne for to ben eft there be was in plesaunce, Agayns his wil,sin it mot nedes be, This Croilus up roos, and faste him cledde, Ther God us graunte sounde and sone to mete1 And in his thought gan up and doun to winde Hir wordes alle, and every contenaunce, impressen in his minde poynt that to him was plesaunce; And verrayliche, of thilke remembraunce, Desyral newe him brende, and lust to brede Gan more than erst,and yet took he non bede. Ye be so depe inwith myn herte grave That, though I wolde it turne out of my thought As wisly verray God my sou To dyen in the peyne, I coude nought! And, for the love of God that us hath wrought, Lat in your brayn non other fantasye So crepe, that it cause me to dyel Criseyde also, right in the same wyse, Of Troilus gan in hir herte shette thinesse, his lust, his dedes wyse, entilesse, and how she with him mette, Thonkinge love he so wel hir bisette; Desyring eft to have hir herte dere In swich a plyt, she dorste make him chere. And that ye me wolde han as faste in minde As I have yow, that wolde I yow biseche; And, if I wiste soothly that to finde, God mighte not a poynt my joyes eche But, herte myn, withoute more speche, Beth to me trewe, or elles were it routhe; for I am thyn, by God and by my trouthel He seyde: O frend,of frendes the alderbeste That ever was, the sothe for to telle, Thou hast in hevene ybrought my soule at reste fro flegiton, the fery flood of helle; That, though I mighte a thousand tymes selle, Upon a day, my lyf in thy servyse, It mighte nought a mote in that suffyse. This Troilus, with al the affeccioun Of frendes love that herte may devyse, To Pandarus on kneës fil adoun, And er that he wolde of the place aryse, He gan bim thonken in his beste wyse; A hondred sythe he gan the tyme blesse, That he was born to bringe him fro distresse. The sonne, which that al the world may see, Saw never yet, my lyf, that dar I leye, So inly fair and goodly as is she, Whos I am al, and shal, til that I deye; And, that I thus am bíres, dar I seye, That thanked be the heighe worthinesse Of love, and eek thy kinde bisinesse. Beth glad forthy, and live in sikernesse; Thus seyde I never er this, ne shal to mo; And if to yow it were a gret gladnesse To turne ayein, soone after that ye go, Hs fayn wolde I as ye, it were so As wisly God myn herte bringe at restel Hnd bim in armes took, and ofte keste. Pandare, amorwe whích that comen was Anto bis nece, and gan hir fayre grete, Seyde: Hl this night so reyned it, allas Chat al my drede is that ye, nece swete, Han litel layser had to slepe and mete; Alnight, quod he, hath reyn so do me wake, Chat som of us, I trowe, hir hedes ake. Now torne we ayein to Troilus, Chat resteles ful longe abedde lay, And prevely sente after Pandarus, To him to come in al the haste he may. He com anoon, nought ones seyde he Nay, And Troilus ful sobrely he grette, And doun upon hís beddes syde hím sette. I passe al that which chargeth nought to seye, What1 God foryaf his deeth, and she also foryaf, and with hir uncle gan to pleye, for other cause was ther noon than so. But of this thing right to the effect to go, Whan tyme was, hom til hir hous she wente, And Pandarus hath fully hís entente. And ner he com, and seyde: How stont it now This mery morwe, nece, how can ye fare? Criseyde answerde: Never the bet for yow, fox that ye been, God yeve your herte carel God helpe me so, ye caused al this fare, Trow I,quod she, for alle your wordes wbyte; Ol whoso seeth yow knoweth yow ful lytel Thus hastow me no litel thing ysyive, fo which to thee obliged be for ay My lyf, and why? for thorugh thyn help I live; for elles deed badde I be many a day And with that word doun in bis bed he lay, Hnd Pandarus ful sobrely him berde Til al was seyd, and thanne he him answerde: With that she gan hir face for to wrye With the shete, and wex for shame al reed; And Pandarus gan under for to prye, And seyde: Nece, if that I shal ben deed, Have here a swerd, and smyteth of myn beed. Wlith that his arm al sodeynly he thriste Ander bir nekke, and at the laste bir kiste. 112 My dere frend, if I have doon for thee In any cas, God wot, it is me leef; And am as glad as man may of it be God help me so; but tak now not agreef That I shal seyn, be war of this myscheef That, thereas thou now brought art into blisse, That thou thyself ne cause it nought to misse. for of fortunes sharp adversitee The worst kinde of infortune is this, A man to have ben in prosperitee, And it remembren, whan it passed is. Thou art wys ynough, forthy do nought amis; Be not to rakel, though thou sitte warme, for if thou be, certeyn, it wol thee harme. Thou art at ese, and holde thee wel therinne. for also seur as reed is every fyr, Hs greet a craft is kepe wel as winne; Brydle alwey wel thy speche and thy desyr. for worldly joye halt not but by a wyr; That preveth wel, it brest alday so ofte; forthy nede is to werke with it softe. Quod Troilus: I hope, and God toforn, My dere frend, that I shal so me bere, That in my gilt ther shal no thing be lorn, Ne I nil not rakle as for to greven bere; It nedeth not this matere ofte tere; for wistestow myn herte wel, Pandare, God woot, of this thou woldest litel care. This is no litel thing of for to seye, This passeth every wit for to devyse; This passeth al that herte may bithink Tho gan he telle him of his glade night. Hnd wberof first his herte dredde, and how, And seyde: freend,as Iam trewe knight And by that feyth I shal to God and yow, I hadde it never half so hote as now; And ay the more that desyr me byteth To love hir best, the more it me delyteth. I noot myself not wisly wbat it is; But now I fele a newe qualitee, Ye, al another than I dide er thís. Pandare answerde, and seyde thus, that he That ones may in hevene blisse be, He feleth other weyes, dar I leye, Than thilke tyme he frst herde of it seye. This is o word for al; this Troilus Was never ful, to speke of this matere, And for to preysen unto Pandarus The bountee of his righte lady dere, And Pandarus to thanke and maken chere. This tale ay was spannewe to biginne Til that the night departed hem atwinne. But cruel day, so welawey the stounde So wo was hem, that changen gan hir hewe And day they gonnen to dispyse alnewe, Calling it traytour, envyous, and worse And bitterly the dayes light they curse Sone after this, for that fortune it wolde, Icomen was the blisful tyme swete, That Troilus was warned that he sholde, Ther he was erst, Criseyde his lady mete; for which he felte his herte in joye flete; And feythfully gan alle the goddes berie; And lat see now if that he can be merie. And holden was the forme and al the wyse, Of hír cominge, and eek of hís also, As it was erst, which nedeth nought devyse. But playnly to the effect right for to go, In joye and seurte Pandarus bem two Abedde broughte, whan hem bothe leste, And thus they ben in quiete and in reste. Nought nedeth it to yow, sin they ben met, To aske at me if that they blythe were; for if it erst was wel, tho was it bet A thousandsfold, this nedeth not enquere. Agon was every sorwe and every fere; And bothe, ywís, they hadde, and so they wende, As muche joye as herte may comprende. 516 Quod Troilus: Allas1 now am I war That Pirous and tho swifte stedes thre Wbiche that drawen forth the sonnes ch Han goon som bypath in despy That maketh it so sone day to be Ne shal I never doon him sacri But nedes day departe moste hem sone, And whanne bir speche doon was and hirche And setten tyme of meting eft And thus fortune a tyme ladde in joye Criseyde, and eek this kinges sone of Troye. In suffisaunce, in blisse, and in singinges, This Troilus gan al his lyf to lede; He spendeth,justeth, maketh festeyinges; He yeveth frely ofte, and chaungeth wede And beld aboute him alwey, out of drede, A world of folk, as cam bim wel of kinde, The fressheste and the beste he coude fynde That swich a voys was of hym and a stevene Thorughout the world, of honour and largesse, Chat it up rong unto the yate of And, as in love, he was in swich gladnesse That in his herte he demede, as I gesse, That there nis lovere in this world at ese So wel as he, and thus gan love him plese. The godlibede or beautee which that kinde In any other lady hadde yset Can not the mountaunce of a knot unbinde, Aboute his herte, of al Criseydes net. He was so narwe ymasked and yknet, That it undon on any manere syde, That nil not been, for ought that may betyde. And by the bond ful ofte he wolde take Thís Pandarus, and into gardín lede, And swich a feste and swich a proces make Hím of Criseyde, and of hir womanbede And of hir beautee, that, withouten drede, It was an bevene hís wordes for to here; And thanne he wolde singe in this manere. Love,that of erthe and see bath governaunce, Bind this acord, that I bave told and telle; That that the world with feyth, which that is Dyverseth so his stoundes concordinge, That elements that been so discordable Holden a bond perpetuely duringe, That Phebus mote bis rosy day forth bringe, And that the mone hath lordship over the Althis doth Love; ay heried be his mightes1 That that the see, that gredy is to flowen, Constreyneth to a certeyn ende so hat so fersly they ne growen nchen erthe and al for evermo; dif that Love ought lete his brydel go, Althat now loveth asonder sholde lepe, And lost were al, that Love halt now tohepe. So wolde God, that auctor is of kinde, That, with his bond, Love of his vertu liste Tocerclen bertes alle, and faste binde, That from his bond no wight the wey out wiste. And hertes colde, hem wolde I that he twiste Tomake hem love, and that hem leste ay rewe On hertes sore, and kepe hem that ben trewe. In alle nedes, for the tounes werre, he was, and ay the firste in armes dight; And certeynly, but if that bokes erre, Save Ector, most ydrad of any wight; And this encrees of hardinesse and might Cam bím of love, hís ladies thank to winne, That altered his spirit so withinne. In tyme of trewe, on haukinge wolde he ryde, elles hunten boor, bere, or lyoun The smale bestes leet he gon bisyde. 113 And whan that he com rydinge into toun, ful ofte his lady, from hir window doun, As fresh as faucon comen out of muwe, ful redy was, him goodly to saluwe. And most of love and vertu was his speche, And in despyt hadde alle wrecchednesse; And doutelees,no nede was him biseche To honouren hem that hadde worthinesse, And esen bem that weren in distresse. And glad was he if any wight wel ferde, That lover was, whan he it wiste or herde. for sooth to seyn, he lost held every wight But if he were in loves heigh servyse, I mene folk that oughte it been of right. And over al this, so wel coude he devyse Of sentement, and in so unkouth wyse Al his array, that every lover thoughte, That al was wel, whatso he seyde or wroughte. And though that he be come of blood royal, Him liste of pryde at no wight for to chase; Benigne he was to ech in general, for wbich he gat him thank in every place. Thus wolde Love, yheried be his grace, That Pryde, Envye, Ire, and Avaryce He gan to flee, and every other vyce. Thou lady bright, the doughter to Dione, Thy blinde and winged sone eek, daun Cupyde; Ve sustren nyne eek, that by Elicone In bil Parnaso listen for to abyde, That ye thus fer han deyned me to gyde, I can no more, but sin that ye wol wende, Ye heried been foray, withouten endel Thourgh yow have I seyd fully in my song Theffect and joye of Troilus servyse, Al be that ther was som disese among, As to myn auctor listeth to devyse. My thridde book now ende ich in this wyse; And Troilus in luste and in quiete Is with Criseyde, his owne herte swete. Explicit Liber Tercius ![](images/9648f4ffa5f771d32f0cfa5e346eb55ed77db3d231803bd417b795eb412756eb.jpg) CROILAS HND CRISEYDE LIBER QAARTAS. Prohemium Libri Quartí. Chan laugheth she, & ma ![](images/2b1900d2919b2eb3b018dcbdd0bd631fad17452962dbad5379d3b5d7fc6b1ebe.jpg) ALTOLITEL,WEYLAWEYtheWhyle, Lasteth swich joye, ythonked be fortune That semeth trewest, whan she wol bygyle, And can to foles so hir song entune, That she hem bent & blent, traytour comune And whan a wight is from hir wheel ythrowe, from Troilus she gan hir brighte face Awey to wrythe, and took of him non bed But caste him clene oute of his lady gra And on hir wheel she sette up Diom for which right now myn herte ginneth bled And now my penne, allas1 with which I wryte Quaketh for drede of that I moot endyte. for how Criseyde Troilus forsook, Or at the leste, how that she was unkinde, Mot hennesforth ben matere of my book, As wryten folk thorugh which itis in mind Allas1 that they shulde ever cause finde To speke hir harm; and if they on hir lye, Ywis, hemself sholde han the vilanye. O ye Herines, Nightes doughtren three, That endelees compleynen ever in pyne, Megera,Hlete,and eek Thesiphone; Thou cruel Mars eek, fader to Quíryne, This ilke ferthe book me helpeth fyne, So that the los of lyf and love yfere Of Troilus be fully shewed here. Explicit Prohemium. ![](images/f64dfe0ee9a0c73910533610b089b0242a938d36caa4fa3489b77e86e0ecec28.jpg) IGGINGE in ost, as I have seyd er thís, The Grekes stronge, aboute Troye toun, Bifel that, whan that Phebus shyningis Apon the brest of Hercules Lyoun, That Ector, with ful many a bold baroun, Srekes for to fighte, Not I how longe or short it was bitwene This purpos and that day they fighte mente; But on a day wel armed, bright and shene, Ector, and many a worthy wight out wente, With spere in hond and bigge bowes bente; And in the berd, withoute lenger lette, Hir fomen in the feld anoon bem mette. The longe day, wíth speres sharpe ygrounde, With arwes, dartes, swerdes, maces felle, Chey fighte and bringen hors and man to grounde, But in the laste shour, sooth for to telle, The folk of Troye hemselven so misledden, That with the worse at night homward they fledden. At whiche day was taken Antenor, Maugre Polydamas or Monesteo, Santippe, Sarpedon, Polynestor, Polyte, or eek the Troían daun Ripheo, And othere lasse folk,as Phebuseo. So that, for harm, that day the folk of Troye Dredden to lese a greet part of bir joye. Of Pryamus was yeve, at Greek requeste, A tyme of trewe, and tho they gonnen trete, Hir prisoneres to chaungen, moste and leste, And for the surplus yeven sommes grete. This thing anoon was couth in every strete, Bothe in thassege, in toune, and everywhere, And with the firste it cam to Calkas ere. Whan Calkas knew thís tretis sholde holde, In consistorie, among the Grekes, sone He gan in thringe forth, with lordes olde, And sette him thereas he was wont to done; And with a chaunged face hem bad a bone, for love of God, to don that reverence, To stínte noyse, and yeve hím audience. Thanne seyde he thus: Lol lordes myne, I was Troian, as it is knowen out of drede; And if that yow remembre, I am Calkas That alderfrst yaf comfort to your nede, And tolde wel how that ye sholden spede. for dredelees, thorugh yow, shal, in a stounde, Ben Troye ybrend, and beten doun to grounde. Bycause be nolde payen bem hir byre, Telling his tale alwey, this olde greye, Humble in speche, and in bis lokingee The salte teres from bis eyen tweye ful faste ronnen doun by eytber cheke So longe be gan of socour bem byseke They yave him Antenor, withoute m Sires, she nis no prisoner, he seyde; The noyse of peple upstirte thanne at ones, of straw yset on fyre; volde, for the nones, They sholden hir confusioun desyre. DEctor, quod they, what goost may yow And windowe eek, and tho this sorweful man Apon his beddes syde adoun him sette, ful lyk a deed image pale and wan; And in bis brest the beped wo bigan Outbreste, and he to werken in this wyse In his woodnesse, as I shal yow devyse. And in what forme, or in what maner wyse This town to shende, and al your lust to acheve, Ye han er this wel herd it me devyse; This knowe ye, my lordes, as I leve. And for the Grekes weren me so leve, I com myself in my propre persone, To teche in this how yow was best to done; But who was glad ynough but Calkas tho? And of this thing ful sone his nedes leyde On hem that sholden for the tretis go, And bem for Antenor ful ofte preyde To bringen hoom king Toas and Criseyde; And whan Pryam his savegarde sente, Thembassadours to Troye streyght they went Chis womman thus to shilde and doon us lese Daun Antenor? a wrong wey now ye chese, Right as the wilde bole biginneth springe Now here, now there, ydarted to the herte, And of his deeth roreth in compleyninge, Right so gan he aboute the chaumbre sterte, Smyting his brest ay with his festes smerte; His heed to the wal, his body to the grounde ful ofte he swapte, himselven to confounde. Havinge unto my tresour ne my rente Right no resport, to respect of your ese. Thus al my good I loste and to yow wente, Wening in this you, lordes, for to plese. But al that los ne doth me no disese. I vouchesauf, as wísly have I joye, for you to lese al that I have in Troye, The cause ytold of hir cominge, the olde Pryam the king ful sone in genera Let hereupon his parlement to holde Of which the effect rehersen yow I shal Chembassadours ben answered for fynal Theschaunge of prisoners and al this nede Hem lyketh wel, and forth in they procede That is so wys, and eek so bold baroun, ban nede of folk, as men may see; the grettest of this toun; tor, lat tho fantasyës be O king Pryam, quod they, thus seggen we, Thatalour voys is to forgon Criseyde; And to deliveren Antenor they preyde. His eyen two, for pitee of his herte, Out stremeden as swifte welles tweye; The heighe sobbes of his sorwes smerte His speche him rafte, unnethes mighte he seye: O deeth, allas1 why niltow do me deye? Hcursed be the day wbich that nature Shoop me to ben a lyves creature1 Save of a doughter, that I lafte, allas Slepinge at hoom, whanne out of Troye I sterte. O sterne, O cruel fader that I was1 How mighte I have in that so hard an berte? Allas1 I ne hadde ybrought hir in bir sherte1 for sorwe of which I wol not live to morwe, But if ye lordes rewe upon my sorwe. This Troilus was present in the place, Wban axed was for Antenor Criseyde for which ful sone chaungen gan bis face, As he that with tho wordes wel neigh deyde But nathelees, he no word to it seyde, Lest men sholde his affeccioun espye; With mannes herte he gan his sorwes drye. O Juvenal, lord1 trewe is thy sentence, witen folk what is to yerne inde in bir desyr offence; f errour lat hem not descerne What best is; and lo, here ensample as yerne. This folk desiren now deliveraunce Of Antenor, that broughte bem to mischauncel But after, whan the furie and the rage Which that his herte twiste and faste threste, By lengthe of tyme somwhat gan asswage, Upon his bed he leyde hím doun to reste; But tho bigonne his teres more outbreste, That wonderis, the body may suffyse To half this wo, which that I yow devyse. for, by that cause I say no tyme er now Hir to delivere, I holden have my pees; But now or never, if that it lyke yow, I may hir have right sone, doutelees. O help and gracel amonges al thís prees, Rewe on this olde caitif in destresse, Sin I through yow have al this hevinesse1 And ful of anguísh and of grisly drede Abood what lordes wolde unto it seye; And if they wolde graunte, as God forbede, Theschaunge of hir, than thoughte he thinges tweye, for he was after traytour to the toun ; allas1 they quitte him out to rathe; Id, lo, thy díscrecioun yde, which that never dide hem skathe, hal now no lenger in hir blisse bathe; But Antenor, he shal com hoom to toune, And she shal out; thus seyden bere and howne. Than seyde he thus: fortunel allas the whylel Wbat have I doon, what have I thus agilt? How mightestow for reuthe me bigyle? Is ther no grace, and shal I thus be spilt? Shal thus Criseyde awey, for that thou wilt? Allas1 how maystow in thyn herte finde To been to me thus cruel and unkinde? Ye have now caught and fetered in prisoun Troians ynowe; and if your willes be, My child with oon may have redempcioun. Now for the love of God and of bountee, Oon of so fele, allas1 so yeve hím me. What nede were it this preyere for to werne, Sin ye shul bothe han folk and toun as yerne? first, how to save hir honour, and what weye He mighte best theschaunge of hir wi ful faste he caste how al this for which delibered was by parlement, for Antenor to yelden up Criseyde, Andit pronounced by the president, Altheigh that Ector Nay ful ofte preyde. And fynaly, what wight that it withseyde, It was for nought, it moste been, and sholde; for substaunce of the parlement it wolde. Have I thee nought honoured al my lyve, Hs thou wel wost, above the goddes alle? Why wiltow me fro joye thus depryve? O Troilus, what may men now thee calle But wrecche of wrecches, out of honour falle Into miserie, in which I wol biwayle Criseyde, allas1 til that the breeth me fayle? On peril of my lyf, I shal not lye, Appollo hath me told it feithfully; I have eek founde it by astronomye, By sort, and by augurie eek trewely, And dar wel seye, the tyme is faste by, Chat fyr and flaumbe on al the toun shal sprede; And thus shal Troye turne in asshen dede. Love hím made al prest to doon hír byde, And rather dye than she sholde go But resoun seyde him, on that other syde: Withoute assent of hir ne do not so, Lest for thy werk she wolde be thy fo, And seyn, that thorugh thy medling is yblowe Your bother love, there it was erst unknowe Departed out of parlement echone, This Troilus, withoute wordes mo, Anto his chaumbre spedde hím faste allone, But if it were a man of his or two, The whiche he bad out faste for to go, Bycause he wolde slepen,as he seyde, And hastely upon hís bed hím leyde. Allas, fortunel if that my lyf in joye Displesed hadde unto thy foule envye, Why ne haddestow my fader, king of Troye, Byraft the lyf, or doon my bretheren dye, Or slayn myself, that thus compleyne and crye, I, combresworld, that may of nothing serve, But ever dye, and never fully sterve? for certeyn, Pbebus and Neptunus bothe, That makeden the walles of the toun, Ben with the folk of Troye alwey so wrothe, That thei wol bringe it to confusioun, Right in despyt of king Lameadoun. for which he gan deliberen, for the beste, That though the lordes wolde that she wente, He wolde late hem graunte what hem leste, And telle his lady first what that they mente. And wban that she had seyd him hir entente, Therafter wolde he werken also blyve, Though al the world ayein it wolde stryve. Ector, which that wel the Grekes herde, for Hntenor how they wolde han Criseyde, Gan it withstonde, and sobrely answerde: And as in winter leves been biraft, Echeafter other, til the tree be bare, So that ther nis but bark and braunche ylaft, Lyth Troilus, biraft of ech welfare, Ybounden in the blake bark of care, Disposed wood out of his wit to breyde, So sore him sat the chaunginge of Criseyde. If that Críseyde allone were me laft, Nought roughte I whider thou woldest me stere; He rist hím up, and every dore he shette And hír, allas1 than hastow me bíraft. But evermore, lol this is thy manere, To reve a wight that most is to him dere, To preve in that thy gerful violence, Thus am I lost, ther helpeth no defencel Troilus and O verray Lord of love, O God, allas1 Criseyde. Chat knowest best myn berte and al my thought, LiberIV. What shal my sorwful lyf don in this cas If I forgo that I so dere have bought? Sin ye Čriseyde and me han fully brought Into your grace, and bothe our hertes seled, How may ye suffre, allasl it be repeled? Pandare, which that in the parlement But in a rees to What I may doon, I shal, whyl I may dure On lyve in torment and in cruel peyne, This infortune or this disaventure, Allone as I was born, ywis, compleyne; Ne never wil I seen it shyne or reyne; But ende I wil, as Edippe, in derknesse My sorwful lyf, and dyen in distresse. And overal this, as thou wel wost thyselve, This town is fulof ladies al aboute; And, to my doom, fairer than swiche twelve As ever she was, shal I finde, in som route, ye, oon or two, withouten any doute. forthy be glad, myn owene dere brother, If she be lost, we shul recovere another. Acerteyn knight, that for the tyme kepte The chaumbrerdore, undide it bim anoon; And Pandare, that ful tendreliche wepte, Toward the bed gan softely to goon So confus, that he niste what to seye; for verray wo his wit was neigh aweye What, God forbede alwey that ech plesaunce In o thi ing were, and in non other wight1 If oon can singe, another can wel daunce; If this be goodly, she is glad and light; And this is fayr, and that can good aright. Ech for bis vertu bolden is for dere, Bothe heroner and faucon for rívere. O wery goost, that errest to and fro, Why niltow fleen out of the wofulleste Body, that ever mighte on grounde go? O soule, lurkinge in this wo, unneste, flee forth out of myn herte, and lat it breste, And folwe alwey Criseyde, thy lady dere; Thy righte place is now no lenger here1 And with his chere and loking al totorn for sorwe of this, and with bis armes folden He stood this woful Troilus biforn, And on his pitous face be gan bibolden; But Lord, so often gan bis herte colden, Seing his freend in wo, whos hevinesse His herte slow, as thoughte him, for distresse And eek, as writ Zanzis, that was ful wys: The love out chaceth ofte the olde; cas lyth newe avys eek, thyself to saven artow holde; r, by proces, shal of kinde colde. for sin it is but casuel plesaunce, Som cas shal putte it out of remembraunce. O wofulle eyen two, sin your disport Was al to seen Criseydes eyen brighte, What shal ye doon but, for my discomfort, Stonden for nought, and wepen out your sighte? Sin she is queynt, that wont was yow to lighte, In veyn frosthissforth have I eyen tweye Yformed, sin your vertue is aweye. This woful wight, this Troilus, that felte His freend Pandare ycomen bim to see, Gan as the snow ayein the sonne melte, for which this sorwful Pandare, of pitee, Gan for to wepe as tendreliche as he; And specheles thus been thise ilke tweye, That neyther mighte o word for sorwe seye. for also seur as day cometh after níght, The newe love, labour or other wo, de seinge of a wight, ffecciouns alle overgo. And, for thy part, thou shalt have oon of tho Toabrigge with thy bittre peynes smerte; Absence of hir shal dryve hir out of herte. Criseyde,Olady sovere Of thilke woful soule that thus cryeth, Who shal now yeven comfort to my peyne? Allas, no wight; but when myn herte dyeth, My spirit, which that so unto yow hyeth, Receyve in gree, for that shal ay yow serve; forthy no fors is, though the body sterve. But at the laste this woful Troilus, Ney deed for smert, gan bresten out to rore, And with a sorwful noyse he seyde thus, Among his sobbes and his sykes sore: Lol Pandare, I am deed, withouten more Hastow nought herd at parlement, beseyde, for Antenor how lost is my Criseyde? Thise wordes seyde he for the nones alle, elpe his freend, lest he for sorwe deyde to doon bis wo to fal not what unthrift that he seyde Croilus, that neigh for sorwe deyde, Cok litel bedeof al that ever he mente; Oon ere it herde, at the other out it wente: O ye loveres, that heighe upon the wheel Ben set of fortune, in good aventure, God leve that ye finde ay love of steel, And longe mot your lyf in joye endurel But whan ye comen by my sepulture, Remembreth that your felawe resteth there; for I lovede eek, though I unworthy were. This Pandarus, fut deed and pale of hewe, ful pitously answerde and seyde As wisly were it fals as it is trewe That I have herd, and wot al how it is. O mercy, God, who wolde have trowed this? Who wolde have wend that, in so litela throwe, fortune our joye wolde han overthrowe? Thow hast here maad an argument, for fyn, How that it sholde lasse peyne be Criseyde to forgoon, for she was myn, And líve in ese and in felicitee. Wby gabbestow, that seydest thus to me That Him is wors that is fro wele ythrowe, Than he hadde erst non of that wele yknowe? O olde unholsom and míslyved man, Calkas I mene, allas1 what eyleth thee To been a Greek, sin thou art born Troian? O Calkas, which that wilt my bane be, In cursed tyme was thou born for me1 Hs wolde blisful Jove, for his joye, That I thee hadde, where I wolde, in Troyel Butat the laste answerde and seyde: freend, Chis lechecraft, or heled thus to be, Were wel sitting, if that I were a feend, Co traysen hir that trewe is unto me I pray God, lat this consayl never ythee; But do me rather sterve anonsright here ErI thus do as thoume woldest lere. But tel me now, sin that thee thinketh so light To chaungen so in love, ay to and fro, Why hastow not don bisily thy might To chaungen hir that doth thee al thy wo? Why niltow lete hir fro thyn berte go? Why niltow love another lady swete, That may thyn berte setten in quiete? for in thís world ther is no creature, As to my doom, that ever saw ruyne Straungere than this, thorugh cas or aventure But who may al eschewe or al devyne? Swich is this world; forthy I thus defyne, Ne trust no wight to finden in fortune Hy propretee; hir yeftes been comune. H thousand sykes, hottere than the glede, Out of his brest ech after other wente, Medled with pleyntes newe, his wo to fede, for which his woful teres never stente; And shortly, so his peynes hím torente, And wex so mat, that joye nor penaunce He feleth noon, but lyth forth in a traunce. 522 But tel me this, why thou art now so mad To sorwen thus? Why lystow in this wyse, Sin thy desyr al holly hastow had, So that, by right, it oughte ynow suffyse? But I, that never felte in my servyse A frendly chere or loking of an yë, Lat me thus wepe and wayle, til I dye. Thow biddest me I sholde love another Hl freshly newe, and lat Criseyde go It lyth not in my power, leve brother And though I mighte, I woldenot do so. But canstow pleyen raket, to and fro, Netle in, dokke out, now this, now that, Pandare? Now foule falle hir, for thy wo that carel She that I serve, ywis, what so thou seye, To whom myn herte enhabit is by right Shal han me holly hires til that I deye for, Pandarus, sin I have trouthe hir hight, Iwolnot been untrewe for no wight; But as hir man I wol ay líve and sterve, And never other creature serve. Thow farest eek by me, thou Pandarus, As he, that whan a wight is wo bigoon He cometh to him a pas, and seyth right thus: Thenk not on smert, and thou sbalt fele noon Thou most me first transmuwen in a stoon, And reve me my passiounes alle, Er thou so lightly do my wo to falle. If thou hast had in love ay yet mischaunce, And canst it not out of thyn herte dryve, I, that livede in lust and in plesaunce With hir as muche as creature onlyve, How sholde I that foryete, and that so blyve? O where hastow ben hid so longe in muwe, That canst so weland formely arguwe? The deeth may wel out of my brest departe The lyf, so longe may this sorwe myne; But fro my soule shal Criseydes darte Out nevermo; but doun with Proserpyne, Whan I am deed, I wol go wone in pyne; And ther I woleternally compleyne My wo, and how that twinned be we tweyne. And ther thou seyst, thou shalt as faire fnde As she, lat be, make no comparisoun creature yformed here by kinde. leve Pandare, in conclusioun, I wol not be of thyn opinioun, Touching al this; for whiche I thee biseche, So hold thy pees; thou sleest me with thy speche. Nay, nay, God wot, nought worth is al thy reed, for which, for what that ever may bifalle, Withouten wordes mo,I wol be deed O deeth, that endere art of sorwes alle, Com now, sin I so ofte after thee calle; for sely is that deeth, soth for to seyne, That, ofte ycleped, cometh and endeth peyne. Wel wot I, whyl my lyf was in quiete, Er thou me slowe, I wolde have yeven hyre; But now thy cominge is to me so swete, That in this world I nothing so desyre. O deeth, sin with thís sorwe I am afyre, Thou outher do me anoon in teres drenche, Or with thy colde strook myn hete quenchel Sin that thou sleest so fele in sondry wyse Hyens hir wil, unpreyed, day and night, Do me, at my requeste, this servyse, Delivere now the world, so dostow right, Of me, that am the wofulleste wight That ever was; for tyme is that I sterve, Sin in this world of right nought may I serve. Thus am I lost, for ought that I can see; for certeyn is, sin that I am bir knight, I moste hir honour levere ban than me In every cas, as lovere oughte of right. Thus am I with desyr and reson twight; Desyr for to distourben bir me redeth, And reson nil not, so myn berte dredeth Chis Troilus in teres gan distille, Hs licour out of alambyk ful faste; And Pandarus gan holde hís tunge stille, And to the ground hís eyen doun he caste. But nathelees, thus thoughte he at the laste: What, parde, rather than my felawe deye, Yet shal I somwhat more unto him seye: Thus wepinge that he coude never cesse He seyde: Hllas1 how shal I, wrecche for wel fele I alwey my love encr And hope is lasse and lasse alwey, Pandarel Encressen eek the causes of my care; So welawey, why nil myn herte breste? for, as in love, ther is but litel rest And seyde: freend, sín thou hast swich distresse, And sin thee list myn arguments to blame, Why nilt thyselven helpen doon redresse And with thy manbod letten al this grame? Go ravisshe hir ne canstow not for shame1 And outher lat hir out of toune fare, Or hold hir stille, and leve thy nyce fare. Pandareanswerde freend,thoumayst,forme, Don as thee list; but hadde ich it so hote And thyn estat, she sholde go with I nolde sette at al that noyse a grote A wonder last but nyne night never in Artow in Troye, and hast non bardíment To take a womman which that loveth thee, And wolde birselven been of thyn assent? Now is not this a nyce vanitee? Rys up anoon, and lat this weping be And kyth thou art a man, for in this houre I wil be deed, or she shal bleven oure. Devyne not in reson ay so depe Ne curteysly, but help thyself anoon; Bet is that othere than thyselven wepe And namely, sin ye two been al oon Rys up, for by myn beed, she shal not goon; And rather be in blame a lyte yfounde Than sterve here as a gnat, withoute wounde. To this answerde him Troilus ful softe, And seyde: Parde, leve brother dere, Al this have I myself yet thought ful ofte, And more thing than thou devysest here. But why this thing is laft, thou shalt wel bere; And whan thou me hast yeve an audience, Therafter mayst thou telle al thy sentence. It is no shame unto yow, ne no vyce Hir to witholden, that ye loveth most. Paraunter, she mighte holden thee fornyce To lete hir go thus to the Grekes ost. Thenk eek fortune,as wel thyselven wost, Helpeth an hardy man to his empryse And weyveth wrecches, for hir cowardyse. This Troilus gan with tho wordes quiken, And seyde: freend, graunt mercy, ich assente; Ne peyne noon ne may me so tormente, That, for no cas, it is not myn entente, At shorte wordes, though I dyen sholde, Coravisshe hir, but if hirself it wolde. And though thy lady wolde a litel hir greve Thou shalt thy pees ful wel hereafter make, But as for me, certayn, I can not leve That she wolde it as now for yvel take Wby sholde than for ferd thyn herte quake? Thenk eek how Paris hath, that is thy brother, A love; and why shaltow not have another? first, sin thou wost this toun hath al this werre for ravisshing of wommen so by might, It sholde not be suffred me to erre As it stant now, ne doon so gret unright I sholde han also blame of every wight, My fadres graunt if that I so withstode, Sin she is chaunged for the tounes goode. forthy rys up, as nought ne were, anoon, y face, and to the king thou wende, dren whider thou art goon most with wisdom him and othere blende; Or, upon cas, be may after thee sende Er thou be war; and shortly, brother dere, Be glad, and lat me werke in this matere. I wolmyself be with thee at this dede, Thoughich and almy kin, upon a stounde, Shullein a strete as dogges liggen dede, Adieu, the devel spede him that it recchel Wby, so mene I, quod Pandarus, al this day. But tel me than, bastow bir wel assayed, That sorwest thus?And be answerde: Nay. Wberof artow, quod Pandare, than amayed, Thatnostnot that she wol ben yvel apayed Coravisshe bir, sin thou bast not ben there, But if that Jove tolde it in thyn ere? Tho wordes and tho wommannisshe thinges, She herde hem right as though she thennes were; for, God it wot, hir herte on other thing is, Hlthough the body sat among hem there. Hir advertence is alwey elleswhere; for Troilus ful faste hir soule soughte; Withouten word,alwey on him she thoughte. Thise wommen, that thus wenden hir to plese, Aboute nought gonne alle hir tales spende; Swich vanitee ne can don bir non ese, As she that,al this mene whyle, brende Of other passioun than that they wende, So that she felte almost hir herte dye for wo, and wery of that companye. I have eek thought, so it were hir assent, To aske hir at my fader, of his grace; Than thenke I, this were hir accusement, Sin wel I woot I may hir not purchace. for sin my fader, in so heigh a place Hs parlement, hath bir eschaunge enseled, He nil for me his lettre be repeled. for I shal shape it so, that sikerly alt this night som tyme, in som manere, ke with thy lady prevely, wordes eek, and by hir chere, ful sone aparceyve and wel here Al hir entente, and in this cas the beste; And fare now wel, for in this point I reste. And Troilus, o thing I dar thee swere, That if Criseyde, whiche that is thy leef, Now loveth thee as wel as thou dost here, God helpe me so, she nil not take agreef, Though thou do bote anoon in this mischeef. And if she wilneth fro thee for to passe, Thanne is she fals; so love hir wel the lasse. Yet drede I most hir herte to pertourbe With violence, if I do swich a game; for if I wolde it openly distourbe, It moste been disclaundre to hir name. And me were lever deed than hir defame, Hs nolde God but if I sholde have Hir honour lever than my lyf to savel 524 forthy tak berte, and thenk, right as a knight, Thourgh love is broken alday every lawe. Kyth now sumwhat thy corage and thy might, Have mercy on thyself, for any awe. Lat not this wrecched wo thin herte gnawe, But manly set the world on sixe and sevene; And, if thou deye a martír, go to hevene. for which no lenger mighte she restreyne Hir teres, so they gonnen up to welle, That yeven signes of the bitter peyne In whiche hir spirit was, and moste dwelle; Remembring hir, fro heven unto which helle She fallen was, sith she forgoth the sighte OfTroilus, and sorowfully she sighte. Quod first that oon: Iam glad, trewely, Bycause of yow, that shal your fader see. Hnother seyde: Ywis, so nam not I; for al to litel hath she with us be. Quod tho the thridde: I hope, ywis, that she Shal bringen us the pees on every syde, That, whan she gooth, almighty God hir gyde1 But as men seen in toune, and alaboute, That wommen usen frendes to visyte, So to Criseyde of wommen com a route for pitous joye, and wenden hir delyte; And with hir tales, dere ynough a myte, These wommen, whiche that in the cite dwelle, They sette hem doun, and seyde as I shal telle. The swifte fame, whiche that false thinges Egalreporteth lyk the thinges trewe, hout Troye yfled with preste winges man, and made thís tale al newe, Calkas doughter, with hir brighte hewe, Atparlement, withoute wordes more, Igraunted was in chaunge of Antenore. And thilke foles sittinge hir aboute Wenden, that she so wepte and syked sore Bycause that she sholde out of that route Departe, and never pleye with hem more. And they that badde yknowen hir of yore Seye hir so wepe, and thoughte it kindenesse, And eche of hem wepte eek for hir distresse; Che whiche tale anoonsright as Criseyde Had herd, she which that of hir fader roughte, As in this cas, right nought, ne wbanne he deyde, fulbisily to Juppiter bisoughte Yeve him mischaunce that this tretis broughte. But shortly, lest thise tales sothe were, She dorste at no wight asken it, for fere. As she that hadde hir berte and al hir minde On Troilus yset so wonder faste, That al this world ne mighte hir love unbinde, Ne Troilus out of hir herte caste; She wol ben bis, whyl that bir lyf may laste. And thus she brenneth bothe in love and drede, So that she niste what was best to rede. And bisily they gonnen hir conforten Of thing, God wot, on which she litel thoughte; And with hir tales wenden hir disporten, And to be glad they often bir bisoughte. But swich an ese therwith they hir wroughte Right as a man is esed for to fele, for ache of heed, to clawen hím on his hele1 But after al this nyce vanitee They took hir leve, and hoom they wenten alle. Criseyde, ful of sorweful pitee, Into hir chaumbre up wente out of the halle, And on bir bed she gan for deed to falle, In purpos never thennes for to ryse; And thus she wroughte, as I shal yow devyse. Hir ounded heer, that sonnish was of hewe, She rente, and eek hir fingres longe and smale She wrong ful ofte, and bad God on hir rewe, And with the deeth to doon bote on hir bale. Hir hewe, whylom bright, that tho was pale, Bar witnes of hir wo and hir constreynte; And thus she spak, sobbinge, in hir compleynte: Alas1 quod she, out of this regioun I, woful wrecche and infortuned wight, And born in corsed constellacioun, Mot goon, and thus departen fro my knight; Wo worth, allas1 that ilke dayes light On which I saw him frst with eyen tweyne, Chat causeth me, and I him, al this peyne1 Thus herte myn, for Antenor, allas1 I sone shal be chaunged, as I wer But how shul ye don in this sorwful cas, How shal your tendre herte this suste But herte myn, foryet this sorwe and tene, And me also; for, soothly for to seye, So ye wel fare, I recche not How mighte it ever yred ben or ysonge, The pleynte that she made in bir distresse? I noot; but, as for me, my litel tonge If I discreven wolde hir hevinesse It sholde make bir sorwe seme lesse Than that it was, and childishly deface Hir heigh compleynte, and therfore I it pace. And thou,my suster, ful of discomfort, Quod Pandarus, what thenkestow to do? hy woltow thus thyselve,allas, fordor That I shal seyn,and berkne, of good entente, This, which by me thy Troilus thee sente. Therwith the teres from hir eyen two Doun fille, as shour in Aperill, ful swythe; Hir wbyte brest she bet, and for the wo After the deeth she cryed a thousand sythe, Sin he that wont hir wo was for to lythe, She mot forgoon; for which disaventure She held hirself a forlost creature. Pandare, which that sent from Troilus Was to Criseyde, as ye han herd devyse That for the beste it was accorded And he ful glad to doon him that serv Unto Criseyde, in a ful secree wyse Theras she lay in torment and in rage, Com hir to telle al hoolly his message Torned hir tho Criseyde, a wo makinge Sogreet that ita deeth was for to see: Allas quod she, what wordes may ye bringei What wol my dere herte seyn to me, Which that I drede nevermo to see? Wolbe bave pleynte or teres, er I wende? I have ynowe, if he therafter sendel And fond that she hirselven gan to trete ful pitously; for with bir salte teres Hir brest, hir face ybathed was ful wet The mighty tresses of hir sonnish bere Anbroyden, hangen al aboute hir eres Which yaf hím verray signal of martyre Of deeth, which that hir herte gan desyre. She seyde: How shal he doon, and I also? How sholde I live, if that I from him twinne? O dere berte eek, that I love so, Who shal that sorwe sleen that ye ben inne? O Calkas, fader, thyn be al thís sínne1 O moder myn, that cleped were Argyve, Wo worth that day that thou me bere on lyve1 She was right swich to seen in hir visage Hsist t that men on bere binde; ed in another kinde. ye, the laughtre men was wont to finde Ben fled, and thus lyth now Criseyde allone. How sholde a fish withoute water dure? Wbatis Criseyde worth, from Troilus? How sholde a plaunte or lyves creature Live, withoute bis kinde noriture? for wbich ful oft a byword here I seye, That, Rotelees, mot grene sone deye I shal don thus, sin neither swerd ne darte Dar I non bandle, for the crueltee, That ilke day that I from yow departe, If sorwe of that nil not my bane be, Than shal no mete or drinke come in me Til I my soule out of my breste unshethe; And thus myselven wol I do to dethe. And, Troilus, my clothes everichoon Shul blake been, in tokeninge, herte swete, That I am as out of this world agoon, That wont was yow to setten in quiete; And of myn ordre, ay til deeth me mete, The observaunce ever, in your absence, Shal sorwe been, compleynte, and abstinence. Whan she him saw, she gan for sorwe anoon Hir tery face atwixe hir armest for which this Pandare is so wo Chat in the hous he mighte unnethe As he that pitee felte on every sy for if Criseyde hadde erst compleyned sore, Tho gan she pleyne a thousand tymes more. Myn berte and eek the woful goost therinne Biquethe I, with your spirit to compleyne Eternally, for they shul never twinne. for though in erthe ytwinned be we tweyne, Yet in the feld of pitee, out of peyne, That hight Elysos, shul we been yfere, Hs Orpheus and Erudice his fere. 526 Aboute hir eyen two a purpre ring in sothfast tokninge of hir peyne, e it was a dedly thing, ch Pandare mighte not restreyne The teres from bis eyen for to reyne. But nathelees, as be best mighte, he seyde from Troilus thise wordes to Criseyde: And in hír aspre pleynte than she seyde: Pandare first of joyes mo than two as cause causinge unto me, Criseyde That now transmuwed been in cruel wo. Wher shal I seye to yow Wel come or no, Chat alderfirst me broughte into servyse Of love, allas1 that endeth in swich wyse? Lo,nece, I trowe ye ban herd al how , with othere lordes, for the beste, eschaunge of Hntenor and yow ause is of this sorwe and this unreste But how this cas doth Troilus moleste, That may non erthely mannes tonge seye; for verray wo hís wit is al aweye. Endeth than love in wo? Ye, or men lyeth1 And alle worldly blisse, as thinketh me, The ende of blisse ay sorwe it occupyeth And whoso troweth not that it so be, Lat hím upon me, woful wrecche, ysee, That myself hate, and ay my birthe acorse, felinge alwey, fro wikke I go to worse. for which we han so sorwed, he and I, Chat into litel bothe it hadde us slawe; But thurgh my conseil this day, fynally, hesomwbat is fro weping now withdrawe. And semeth me that he desyreth fawe With yow to been al night, for to devyse Remede in this, if ther were any wyse. Whoso me seeth, be seeth sorwe al at ones, Peyne, torment, pleynte, wo, distresse. Out of my woful body harm ther noon is, As anguish, langour, cruel bitternesse, Hnoy, smert, drede, fury, and eek siknesse. I trowe, ywis, from hevene teres reyne, for pitee of myn aspre and cruel peynel Thise wordes seyd, she on hir armes two fil gruf, and gan to wepe pitously. Quod Pandarus: Allas1 why do ye so, Syn wel ye wot the tyme is faste by, That he shal come? Arys up hastely, That he yow nat biwopen thus ne finde, But ye wol han him wood out of his mindel Chis, short and pleyne, theffect of my message, As ferforth as my wit can comprehende. for ye, that been of torment in swich rage, May to no long prologe as now entende; And herupon ye may answere hím sende. And, for the love of God, my nece dere, So leef this wo er Troilus be bere. Grevous to me, God wot, is for to twinne, Quod she, but yet it hardere is to me Co seen that sorwe which that he is inne; for wel wot I, it wol my bane be; And deye I wol in certayn, tho quod she; But bidde him come,er deeth,that thus me threteth, Dryve out that goost,which in myn herte beteth. Gret is my wo, quod she, and sighte sore, As she that feleth dedly sbarp distresse; But yet to me his sorwe is muchel more, Chat love him bet than he himself, I gesse. Allas1 for me hath he swich hevinesse? Can he for me so pitously compleyne? Ywis, this sorwe doubleth al my peyne. for wiste he that ye ferde in this manere, He wolde himselve slee; and if I wende To han this fare, he sholde not come here for al the good that Pryam may despende. for to what fyn he wolde anoon pretende, That knowe I wel; and forthy yet I seye, So leef this sorwe, or platly he wol deye. And shapeth yow his sorwe for to abregge, And nought encresse, leve nece swete; Beth rather to him cause of flat than egge, And with som wysdom ye his sorwes bete. What helpeth it to wepen ful a strete, Or though ye bothe in salte teres dreynte? Bet is a tyme of cure ay than of pleynte. I mene thus; whan I him hider bringe, Sin ye ben wyse, and bothe of oon assent, So shapeth how distourbe your goinge, Or come ayen, sone after ye be went. Wommen ben wyse in short avysement; And lat sen how your wit shal now avayle; And what that I may helpe, it shal not fayle. Go, quod Criseyde, and uncle, trewely, I shal don al my might, me to restreyne from weping in his sight, and bisily, Hím for to glade, I shal don al my peyne, And in myn berte seken every veyne; If to this soor ther may be founden salve, It shal not lakken, certain, on myn balve. Goth Pandarus, and Troilus he soughte, Til in a temple he fond him al allone, Hs be that of his lyf no lenger roughte; But to the pitouse goddes everichone ful tendrely he preyde, and made his mone, To doon him sone out of this world to pace; for wel he thoughte ther was non other grace. And shortly, al the sothe for to seye, He was so fallen in despeyr that day, That outrely he shoop bim for to deye. for right thus was his argument alwey: He seyde, he nas but loren, waylawey1 for al that comth, comth by necessitee; Thus to be lorn, it is my destinee. Troílus and for certaynly, this wot I wel, he seyde, Criseyde. That forsight of divyne purveyaunce LiberIV. Hath seyn alwey me to forgon Criseyde, Sin God seeth every thing, out of doutaunce, And bem desponeth, thourgh his ordenaunce, In hir merytes sothly for to be, As they shul comen by predestinee. But nathelees, allas1 whom shal I leve? for ther ben grete clerkes many oon, That destinee thorugh argumentes preve; And som men seyn that nedely ther is noon; But that free chois is yeven us everichoon. O, welaway1 so sleye arn clerkes olde, That I not whos opinion I may holde. for som men seyn, if God seth al biforn, Ne God may not deceyved ben, pardee, Than moot it fallen, though men badde it sworn, That purveyaunce hath seyn bifore to be. Wherfor I seye, that from eterne if be Hath wist biforn our thought eek as our dede, We have no free chois, as these clerkes rede. ## for other thought nor other dede also Might never be, but swich as purveyaunce, Which may not ben deceyved nevermo, Hath feled biforn, withouten ignoraunce. for if ther mighte been a variaunce To wrythen out fro Goddes purveyinge, There nere no prescience of thing cominge; ## But it were rather an opinioun Ancerteyn, and no stedfast forseinge; And certes, that were an abusioun, That God shuld han no parfit cleer witinge More than we men that han doutous weninge. But swich an errour upon God to gesse Were fals and foul, and wikked corsednesse. ## Eek thís is an opínioun of somme That han bir top ful beighe and smothe yshore; Chey seyn right thus, that thing is not to come for that the prescience hath seyn bifore Chat it shal come; but they seyn, that therfore Chat it shal come, therfore the purveyaunce Wot it biforn withouten ignoraunce; And in this manere this necessitee Retorneth in his part contrarie agayn. for needfully bihoveth it not to be Chat thilke thinges fallen in certayn That ben purveyed; but nedely, as they seyn, Bihoveth it that thinges, whiche that falle, That they in certayn ben purveyed alle. I mene as though I laboured me in thís, To enqueren which thing cause of which thing be; As whether that the prescience of God is The certayn cause of the necessitee Of thinges that to comen been, pardee; Or if necessitee of thing cominge Be cause certeyn of the purveyinge. 528 But now ne enforce I me nat in shewinge How the ordre of causes stant; but wel wot I That it biboveth that the bifallin Of thinges wist biforen certeyn Be necessarie, al seme it not therby That prescience put falling necessaire To thing to come, al falle it foule or faire. for if ther sit a man yond on a see, Than by necessitee bihoveth it That, certes, thyn opinioun soth be, That wenest or conjectest that he sit; And fertherover now ayenward yit, Lo, right so it is of the part contrarie, As thus; now herkne, for I wol not tarie: I seye, that if the opinioun of thee Be sooth, for that he sit, than seye I this Chat he mot sitten by And thus necessitee in Ther moot necessitee ben in yow But thou mayst seyn, the man sit not therfore, That thyn opinion of sitting sot But rather, for the man sit th Therfore is thyn opinion sooth, And I seye, though the cause of sooth of this Comth of his sitting, yet necessite Is entrechaunged, bothe in him and thee. Thus on thís same wyse, out of doutaunce, I may wel maken, as it semeth me, My resoninge of Goddes purveyaunce, And of the thinges that to comen be; By whiche reson men may wel ysee, That thilke thinges that in erthe falle, That by necessitee they comen alle. for although that, for thing shal come, ywis Therfore is it purveyed, certaynly Nat that it comth for it purveyed is: Yet nathelees, bihoveth it nedfully, That thing to come be purveyed, trewely; Or elles, thinges that purveyed be, That they bityden by necessitee. And this suffyseth right ynow, certeyn, for to destroye our free chois every del. But now is this abusion to seyn, That fallinge of the thinges temporel Is cause of Goddes prescience eternel. Now trewely, that is a fals sentence, That thing to come sholde cause his prescience. What mighte I wene, and I hadde swich a thought, But that God purveyth thing that is to come for that it is to come, and elles nought? So mighte I wene that thinges alle and some, That wbylom been bifalle and overcome, Ben cause of thilke sovereyn purveyaunce, Chat forwot al withouten ignoraunce. And overal this, yet seye I more herto, ywis, that thing mot nedefi right so, whan I woot a thing coming, of thinges that ben wist bifore the tyde, Than seyde he thus: Almighty Jove in trone, Or bring Criseyde and me fro O mighty God, quod Pandarus, in trone, Eyl who seigh ever a wys man faren so? roilus, what thenkestow to done? ow swich lust to been thyn owene fo? What, parde, yet is not Criseyde agon Why lust thee so thyself fordoon for drede, That in thyn heed thyn eyen semen dede? hastow not lived many a yeer biforn Withouten hir, and ferd ful wel at ese? tow for hir and for non other born? inde thee wroughte alonly hir to plese? Lat be, and thenk right thus in thy disese, That, in the dees right as ther fallen chaunces, Right so in love, ther come and goon plesaunces. And yet this is a wonder most of alle, thus sorwest, sin thou nost not yit, goinge, how that it shal falle, hirself distorben it. hast not yet assayed al hir wit. Aman may al by tyme his nekke bede Whan it shal of, and sorwen at the nede. forthy take hede of that that I shal seye; Ihave with hir yspoke and longe ybe, Soas accorded was bitwixe us tweye. And evermo me thinketh thus, that she Hath somwhat in hir hertes prevetee, Wherwith she can, if I shal right arede, Distorbe al this, of which thou art in drede. for which my counseil is, whan it is night, Thou to hir go, and make of this an ende; And blisful Juno, thourgh hir grete mighte, Shal,as I hope, hír grace unto us sende. Myn herte seyth, Certeyn, she shal not wende And forthy put thyn herte a whyle in reste; And hold this purpos, for it is the beste. This Troilus answerde, and sighte sore: Chou seyst right wel, and I wil do right so; And what him liste, he seyde unto it more. And whan that it was tyme for to go, ful prevely himself, withouten mo, Anto hír com,as he was wont to done; And how they wroughte, I shal yow telle sone. mm1 Soth is, that whan they gonne first to mete, So gan the peyne hir hertes for to twiste, Chat neither of hem other mighte grete, But bem in armes toke and after kiste. The lasse wofulle of hem bothe niste Wher that he was, ne mighte o word outbringe, As I seyde erst, for wo and for sobbinge. Tho woful teres that they leten falle As bittre weren, out of teres kinde, for peyne, as is ligne aloës or galle. So bittre teres weep nought, as I finde, The woful Myrra through the bark and rinde. That in this world ther nis so hard an berte, That nolde han rewed on hir peynes smerte. But whan hir woful wery gostes tweyne Retorned been theras hem oughte dwelle, And that somwhat to wayken gan the peyne By lengthe of pleynte, and ebben gan the welle Of hire teres, and the herte unswelle, With broken voys, al hoors forsshright, Criseyde To Troilus thise ilke wordes seyde: O Jove, I deye, and mercy I beseche1 Help, Troilusand therwithal hir face Apon his brest she leyde, and loste speche; Hír woful spirit from his propre place, Right with the word, alwey up poynt to pace. And thus she lyth with hewes pale and grene, That whylom fresh and fairest was to sene. This Troilus, that on hir gan bibolde, Clepinge hir name, and she lay as for deed, Withoute answere, and felte hir limes colde Hir eyen throwen upward to hir heed, This sorwful man can now noon other reed, But ofte tyme hir colde mouth be kiste; Wher him was wo, God and himself it wiste1 He rist him up, and long streight he hir leyde; for signe of lyf, for ought he can or may, Can he noon finde in nothing on Criseyde, for which his song ful ofte is Weylaway But whan he saugh that specheles she lay With sorwful voys, and herte of blisse al bare, He seyde how she was fro this world yfare1 So after that he longe hadde hir compleyned, His hondes wronge, and seyd that was to seye And with his teres salte hir brest bireyned, He gan tho teris wypen of ful dreye, And pitously gan for the soule preye, And seyde: O Lord, that set art in thy trone, Rewe eek on me, for I shal folwe hir sonel She cold was and withouten sentement, for aught he woot, for breeth ne felte he noon; And this was him a preignant argument That she was forth out of this world agoon; And whan he seigh ther was non other woon, He gan hir limes dresse in swich manere Hs men don hem that shul be leyd on bere. Croilus and And after this, with sterne and cruel herte, Criseyde. His swerd anoon out of his shethe he twighte, LiberIV. Himself to sleen, how sore that him smerte, So that his sowle hir sowle folwen mighte, Theras the doom of Mynos wolde it dighte; Sin love and cruel fortune it ne wolde, That in this world he lenger liven sholde. But with this selve swerd, which that here is Myselve I wolde bave slayn1 quod she tho But bo, for we han right ynow of this And late us ryse and streight to bedde go, And therë lat us speken of our wo. for, by the morter which that I see brenne, Knowe I ful wel that day is not fer henne Channe seyde he thus, fulfild of heigh desdayn: O cruel Jove, and thou, fortune adverse, This al and som, that falsly have ye slayn Criseyde, and sín ye may do me no werse, fy on your might and werkes so diverse Thus cowardly ye shul me never winne; Ther shal no deeth me fro my lady twínne. han they were in hir bedde, in armes folde Nought was it lyk tho nightes here for pitously ech other gan bi Hs they that hadden al hir bl Biwaylinge ay the day that they were bo To Troilus these ilke worde for I this world, sín ye han slayn hir thus, Wol lete, and folowe bir spirit lowe or hye; Shal never lover seyn that Troilus Dar not, for fere, with his lady dye; for certeyn, I wol bere hir companye. But sin ye wol not suffre us liven bere, Yet suffreth that our soules ben yfere. The sothe is, that the twinninge of us tweyne love, if that he wol have joye. I shal no ferthere out of Troye Than I may ryde ayein on balf a morwe, It oughte lasse causen us to sorwe. Now berkeneth this, ye han wel understonde My going graunted is by parlement So ferforth, that it may not be withstonde And sin ther belpeth noon avysement Coletten it, lat it passe out of minde; And lat us shape a bettre wey to finde. And though so be that pees ther may be noon, Yet hider, though ther never pees ne were, I moste come; for whider sholde I goon, Or how mischaunce sholde I dwelle there Among tho men of armes ever in fere? for which, as wisly God my soule rede, I can not seen wherof ye sholden drede. Lo, herte myn, wel wot ye this, quod she, That if a wight alwey his wo compl And seketh nought how holpen for It nis but folye and encrees And sin that here assembled be To finde bote of wo that we ben inne, It were al tyme sone to bigi And thou, citee, whiche that I leve in wo, And thou, Pryam, and bretheren al yfere, And thou, my moder, farewell for I go; And Httropos, make redy thou my berel And thou, Criseyde, o swete herte dere, Receyve now my spirit1wolde he seye, With swerd at herte, al redy for to deye. Have here another wey, if it so be That al this thing ne may yow not suffyse. My fader, as ye knowen wel, pardee, Is old, and elde is ful of coveityse. And I right now have founden al the gyse, Withoute net, wherwith I shal him hente; And herkeneth how, if that ye wole assente. Soas I shal not so ben bid in muwe, hat it is now a truwe, ful welal myn estat ybere. And er that truwe is doon, I shal ben here, And thanne have ye bothe Antenor ywonne for if that it be pees, myn berte dere, The nature of the pees mot nedes dryve That men moste entrecomunen yfere, And to and fro eek ryde and gon as blyve Hlday as thikke as been flen from an hyve; And every wight han libertee to bleve Wheras him list the bet, withouten leve. I am a womman, as ful wel ye woot, And as I am avysed sodeynly So wol I telle yow, whylit is hoot. Me thinketh thus, that neither ye nor I Oughte half this wo to make skilfully for there is art ynow for to redress That yet is mis, and sleen this hevinesse. But as God wolde, of swough therwith she abreyde, And gan to syke, and Troilus she cryde; And he answerde: Lady myn Criseyde, Live ye yet?and leet his swerd doun glyde. Ye, herte myn, that thanked be Cupyde Quod she, and therwithal she sore sighte; And he bigan to glade hir as he mighte; And thenk right thus: Criseyde is now agoon, e shal come hastely ayeyn by God, lo, right anoon, his dar I saufly seyn erste shul we been so fayn, Soas we shulle togederes ever dwelle, Thatal this world ne mighte our blisse telle. Sooth is, the wo, the whiche that we ben inne, for ought I woot, for nothing elle But for the cause that we sho Considered al, ther nis no m But wbat is thanne a remede unto this But that we shape us sone for to mete? This al and som, my dere herte swete. Lo, Troilus, men seyn that hard it is The wolf ful, and the wether hool to have; This is to seyn, that men ful ofte, ywis, Mot spenden part, the remenaunt for to save. for ay with gold men may the herte grave Of him that set is upon coveityse; And how I mene, I shal it yow devyse. Took hir in armes two, and kiste hír ofte, And hir to glade he dide al his entente; for which hir goost, that flikered ay onlofte, Into hir woful berte ayein it wente. But at the laste, as that hir eyen glente Hsyde, anoon she gan hís swerd aspye, Asit lay bare, and gan for fere crye, And asked bim, why he it badde outdrawe? And Troilus anoon the cause bir tolde And how himself therwith he wolde have slawe. for which Criseyde upon him gan bíholde, And gan him in hir armes faste folde And seyde: O mercy, God, lo, which a dede1 Allas1 how neigh we were bothe dedel Channe if I ne hadde spoken, as grace was, Ye wolde han slayn yourself anoon? quod she. Ye, douteleesHnd she answerde: Hllas1 for, by that ilke Lord that made me, I nolde a forlong wey onlyve han be, Hfter your deeth, to han be crowned quene Of al the lond the sonne on shyneth shene. 530 The moeble which that I have in this toun Anto my fader shal I take, and seye, That right for trust and for savacioun It sent is from a freend of his or tweye, The whiche freendes ferventliche him preye To senden after more, and that in hye, Whyl that this toun stant thus in jupartye. Now that I shal wel bringen it aboute To come ayein, sone after that I go, Therof am I no maner thing in doute for dredeles, withinne a wouke or two, I shal ben here; and, that it may be so By alle right, and in a wordes fewe I shal yow wel an heep of weyes shewe. Isee that ofte, theras we ben now, e beste, our conseil for to hyde, with me, nor I with yow ght; ne see yow go ne ryde. ye not ten dayes thanne abyde, formyn honour, in swich an aventure? γwis, ye mowen elles lite endure1 for which I wol not make long sermoun, for tyme ylost may not recovered be; But I wol gon to my conclusioun And to the beste, in ought that I can see And, for the love of God, foryeve it me If I speke ought ayein your bertes reste; for trewely, I speke it for the beste; Ve knowe eek how that al my kin is here, Butif that onliche it my fader be; And eek myn othere thinges alle yfere, I nameliche, my dere herte, ye, @hom that I nolde leven for to see foral this world, as wyd as it hath space; Orelles, seeich never Joves facel Makínge alwey a protestacioun, That now these wordes, whiche that I shal seye, Nis but to shewe yow my mocioun, Co finde unto our belpe the beste weye; And taketh it non other wyse, I preye. for in effect whatso ye me comaunde, That wol I doon, for that is no demaunde. And that shal been an buge quantitee, Thus shal I seyn, but, lest it folk aspyde Chis may be sent by no wight but by me; I shal eek shewen him, if pees bityde, What frendes that ich have on every syde Toward the court, to doon the wrathe pace Of Priamus, and doon bim stonde in grace. Why trowe ye my fader in this wyse Coveiteth so to see me, but for drede Lestin this toun that folkes me dispyse Bycause of hím, for his unbappy dede? @hat woot my fader what lyf that I lede? forif he wiste in Troye how wel I fare, As neded for my wending nought to care. Ye seen that every day eek, more and more, Men trete of pees; and it supposed is, Chat men the quene Eleyne shal restore, And Grekes us restore that is mis So though ther nere comfort noon but this, Chat men purposen pees on every syde, Vemay the bettre at ese of herte abyde. mm2 So, what for o thing and for other, swete, I shal him so enchaunten with my sawes, That right in hevene his sowle is, shal he mete for al Hppollo, or his clerkes lawes, Or calculinge avayleth nought three hawes; Desyr of gold shal so his sowle blende, That, as me lyst, I shal wel make an ende. And if he wolde ought by his sort it preve If that I lye, in certayn I shal fonde Distorben him, and plukke him by the sleve Makinge his sort, and beren bim on honde, He hath not wel the goddes understonde. for goddes speken in amphibologyes, And, for a sooth, they tellen twenty lyes. Eek drede fond first goddes, I suppose, Thus shal I seyn, and that his cowarde herte Made hím amis the goddes text to glose, Whan he for ferde out of his Delphos sterte. And but I make him sone to converte, And doon my reed withinne a day or tweye, I wol to yow oblige me to deye. I noot if pees shal evermo bityde; But, pees or no, for ernest ne for gam I woot, sin Calkas on the Grekes sy To trusten on, nis but And treweliche,as writen wel I finde, Chat al this thing was seyd of good entente; And that hir herte trewe was and kinde Cowardes hím, and spak right as she mente, And that she starf for wo neigh, whan she wente, And was in purpos ever to be trewe; Thus writen they that of hir werkes knewe. Ye shal eek seen, your fader shal yow glose To been a wyf, and as be can wel preche, He shal som Grek so preyse and wel alose, That ravisshen be shal yow with bis speche, Or do yow doon by force as he shal teche. And Troilus, of whom ye nil han routhe, Shal causeles so sterven in his trouthel And bardily, ne dredeth no poverte, for I have kin and freendes elleswhere Chat, though we comen in our bare sherte, as sholde neither lakke gold ne gere, But been honoured whyl we dwelten there. And go we anoon, for, as in myn entente, This is the beste, if that ye wole assente. Criseyde, with a syk,right in this wyse Answerde: Vwis, my dere berte trewe, aemay wel stele away, as ye devyse, And finde swiche untbrifty weyes newe; Butafterward, ful sore it wol us rewe. And belp me God so at my moste nede As causeles ye suffren al this drede1 This Troilus, with herte and eres spradde, Herde al this thing devysen to and fro And verraylich bim semed that he badde The selve wit; but yet to lete hir go His herte misforyaf hím evermo But fynally, he gan his herte wreste To trusten bir, and took it for the beste. And forthy slee with reson al this hete; Men seyn, The suffraunt overcometh, pardee; Eek Wboso wol han leef, he leef mot lete; Thus maketh vertue of necessitee By pacience, and thenk that lord is he Of fortune ay, that nought wol of hir recche; And she ne daunteth no wight but a wrecche. And over al this, your fader shal despyse Us alle, and seyn this citee nis but l And that thassege never shal forwhy the Grekes han it all Til we be slayn, and doun our walles tor And thus he shat you with bis wordes fere That ay drede I, that ye wol t for thilke day that I for cherisshinge Or drede of fader, or of other wight, elyt, or for weddinge yow,my Troilus, my knight, Saturnes doughter, Juno, thorugh bir might, As wood as Athamante do me dwelle Eternaly in Stix, the put of helle1 for which the grete furie of his penaunce Was queynt with hope, and therwith hem bitwene Bigan for joye the amorouse daunce. And as the briddes, whan the sonne is shene, Delyten in hir song in leves grene, Right so the wordes that they spake yfere Delyted hem, and made hir hertes clere. And trusteth this, that certes, herte swete, Er Phebus suster, Lucina the shene, The Leoun passe out ofthis Ariete, I wol ben here, withouten any wene. I mene, as helpe me Juno, hevenes quene, The tenthe day, but if that deeth me assayle, I wol yow seen, withouten any fayle. Ye shul eek seen so many a lusty knight Among the Grekes, ful of worthinesse And eche of hem with herte, wit, and To plesen yow don al his besine That ye shul dullen of the Of us sely Troíanes, but if routh Remorde yow, or vertue of your trouthe. And also thenketh on myn honestee, That floureth yet, how foule I sholde it shende, nd with what filthe it spotted sholde be, If in this forme I sholde with yow wende.
