Ne though I livede unto the worldes ende, My name sholde I never ayeinward winne; Thus were I lost, and that were routhe and sinne. thatheles,thewenaingorCriseyd for al this world, may nought out of his minde; for which ful ofte he pitously hir preyde, Chat of hir heste he might hir trewe finde. And seyde hir: Certes,if ye be unkinde, And but ye come at day set into Troye, Ne shal I never have hele, honour, ne joye. And this on every god celestíal Iswere it yow, and eek on eche goddesse, On every Nymphe and deite infernal, On Satiry and fauny more and lesse, That halve goddes been of wildernesse; And Attropos my threed of lyf tobreste IfI be fals; now trowe me if thow lestel And this to me so grevous is to thinke, That fro my brest it wol my soulerende; Ne dredeles, in me ther may not A good opinioun, if that ye wend forwhy your faderes sleighte wol us shende. And if ye goon, as I have told yow yore, So thenk I nam but deed, withoute more. And now, so this be sooth, quod Troilus, I shal wel suffre unto the tenthe day Sin that I see that nede it moot be thus. But, for the love of God, if it be may, So lat us stele prívely away; for ever in oon, as for to live in reste, Myn herte seyth that it wol been the beste. for also sooth as sonne uprist on morwe, And, God1 so wisly thou me, woful wrecche, To reste bringe out of this cruel sorwe, I wol myselven slee if that ye drecche. But of my deeth though litel be to recche, Yet, er that ye me cause so to smerte, Dwelrather bere, myn owene swete herte1 And thou, Simoys, that as an arwe clere oye rennest ay downward to the see, this word that seyd is here, ilke day that ich untrewe be Troilus, myn owene herte free, That thou retorne bakwarde to thy welle, And I with body and soule sinke in helle1 for which, with humble, trewe, and pitous herte A thousand tymes mercy I yow prey So reweth on myn aspre peyn And doth somwbat, as that I shal yow seye, And lat us stele away bitwixe us tweye; And thenk that folye is, whan man may chese, for accident his substaunce ay to lese. O mercy, God, what lyf is this? quod she; Allas, ye slee me thus for verray tene1 I see wel now that ye mistrusten me; for by your wordes itis wel ysene. Now, for the love of Cynthia the shene, Mistrust me not thus causeles, for routhe; Sin to be trewe I have yow plight my trouthe. for trewely, myn owene lady dere, Tho sleightes yet that I have herd yow stere ful shaply been to failen alle yfere. for thus men seyn: That oon thenketh the bere, But al another thenketh his ledere Your sire is wys, and seyd is, out of drede: Men may the wyse atsrenne, and not atsrede It is ful bard to halten unespyed Bífore a crepul, for he can the craft; Your fader is in sleighte as Argus yëd; for al be that his moeble is him biraft, His olde sleighte is yet so with him laft, Ye shal not blende him for your womanhede, Ne feyne aright, and that is al my drede. 532 I mene this, that sín we mowe er day Wel stele away, and been togider so, What wit were it to putten in assay, In cas ye sholden to your fader go, If that ye mighte come ayein or no? Chus mene I, that it were a gret folye To putte that sikernesse in jupartye. But that ye speke, awey thus for to go And leten alle your freendes, God forbede, forany womman, that ye sholden so, And namely, sin Troye hath now swich nede Of help; and eek of o thing taketh hede, If this were wist, my lif laye in balaunce, And your honour; God shilde us fro mischaunce1 And thenketh wel, that som tyme it is wit To spende a tyme, a tyme for to winne; Ne, pardee, lorn am I nought fro yow yit, Though that we been a day or two atwinne. Dryf out the fantasyes yow withinne; And trusteth me, and leveth eek your sorwe, Or here my trouthe, I wol not live til morwe. And vulgarly to speken of substaunce Of tresour, may we bothe with us lede Ynough to líve in honour and plesaunce Til into tyme that we shul ben dede; And thus we may eschewen al this drede. for everich other wey ye can recorde, Myn herte, ywis, may not therwith acorde. Andif so be that pees herafter take, Asalday happeth, after anger, game, Why, Lordi the sorwe and wo ye wolden make, That ye ne dorste come ayein for shamel And er that ye juparten so your name, Beth nought to bhasty in this hote fare; for hasty man ne wanteth never care. What trowe ye the peple eek alaboute Wolde of it seye? It is ful light to arede. They wolden seye, and swere it, out of doute, That love ne droof yownought to doon this dede, But lust voluptuous and coward drede. Thus were al lost, ywis, myn herte dere, Your honour, which that now shyneth so clere. mm3 forif ye wiste how sore it doth me smerte, Ye wolde cesse of this; for God, thou wost, The pure spirit wepeth in myn herte, To see yow wepen that I love most, And that I moot gon to the Grekes ost. Ye, nere it that I wiste remedye To come ayein,right here I wolde dyel But certes, I am not so nyce a wight That I ne can imaginen a way To come ayein that day that I have hight. for who may holde thing that wol away? My fadernougbt, foral bis queynte pley. And by my thrift, my wending out of Troye Another day shal torne us alle to joye. forthy, with al myn herte I yow beseke, If that yow list don ought for my preyere, And for the love which that I love yow eke, That er that I departe fro yow here, That of so good a comfort and a chere I may you seen, that ye may bringe at reste Myn herte, which that is at point to breste. And over al this, I pray yow, quod she tho, Myn owene hertes soothfast suffisaunce, Sin I am thyn al hool, withouten mo Chat wbyl that I am absent, no plesaunce Of othere do me fro your remembraunce. for Iam ever agast, forwhy men rede, That Love is thing ay ful of bisy drede. for in this world ther liveth lady noon, If that ye were untrewe, as God defende! That so bitraysed were or wo bigoon Hs I, that alle trouthe in yow entende. And douteles, if that ich other wende, I nere but deed; and er ye cause finde, for Goddes love, so beth me not unkinde. To this answerde Troilus and seyde: Now God, to whom ther nis no cause ywrye, Me glade, as wis I never unto Criseyde, Sin thilke day I saw hir first with ye, Was fals, ne never shal til that I dye. At shorte wordes, wel ye may me leve; I can no more, it shal be founde at preve. ## Graunt mercy, goode myn,ywis, quod she, And blisful Venus lat me never sterve Er I may stonde of plesaunce in degree To quyte him wel, that so wel can deserve; And whyl that God my wit wol me conserve, I shal so doon, so trewe I have yow founde, That ay honour to meward shal rebounde. for trusteth wel, that your estat royal Ne veyn delyt, nor only worthinesse Of yow in werre, or torney marcial, Ne pompe, array, nobley, or eek richesse, Ne made me to rewe on your distresse; But moral vertue, grounded upon trouthe, That was the cause I first hadde on yow rou Eek gentil berte and manbod that ye badde, And that ye hadde, as me thoughte, in despyt Every thing that souned into badd Hs rudenesse and poeplish appet And that your reson brydled your delyt, This made, aboven every creature, That I was your, and shal, whyl I may dure. And thís may lengthe of yeres not fordo, Ne remuable fortune deface But Juppiter, that of his might may do The sorwful to be glad, so yeve us gr. Er nightes ten, to meten in this place So that it may your berte and myn suffyse And fareth now wel, for tyme is that ye And after that they longe ypleyned hadde And ofte ykist and streite in armes fold Che day gan ryse, and Troilus bim cla And rewfulliche his lady gan b As he that felte dethes cares And to hir grace he gan bim recomaunde Wher hím was wo, this holde I no demaunde. for mannes beed imaginen ne can, Ne entendement considere, ne tonge telle The cruel peynes of this sorwful man, That passen every torment doun in helle. for whan he saugh that she ne mighte dwelle, Which that his soule out of his berte rente, Withouten more, out of the chaumbre he wente. Explicit Liber Quartus. CROILAS HND CRISEYDE LIBER QUINCAS. Chat on his hors unnethe he sat for peyne. ![](images/a9e6563733b65ba3777723c000ae3bd7a107dceabcc13fa7d68a8434bc4b5e2d.jpg) GHNTHEFHCHLDESTINEE That Joves hath in disposicioun, And to yow, angry Parcas, sustren three, Committeth, to don execucioun; for which Criseyde moste out of the toun, And Troilus shal dwelle forth in pyne Cil Lachesis his threed no lenger twyne. ![](images/7ffc4b68d9654216d8549a58613719c3bfba3d6720f745c42cd801088fe22ef4.jpg) HE goldenstressed Phebus heighe onlofte Thryës hadde alle with his bemes shene The snowes molte, and Zephírus as ofte Ybroughtayein the tendre leves grene, Sin that the sone of Ecuba the quene Bigan to love hir frst, for whom his sorwe Was al, that she departe sholde amorwe. fulredy was at pryme Dyomede, Criseyde unto the Grekes ost to lede, for sorwe of which she felte hir herte blede, As she that niste what was best to rede. And trewely, as men in bokes rede, Men wíste never womman han the care, Ne was so looth out of a toun to fare. This Troilus, withouten reed or lore, As man that hath his joyes eek forlore, Was waytinge on his lady evermore As she that was the soothfast crop and more Ofal his lust, or joyes heretofore. But Troilus, now farewel al thy joye, for shaltow never seen hir eft in Troyel Soth is, that whyl he bood in this manere, He gan his wo ful manly for to hyde, That wel unnethe it seen was in his chere; Butat the yate ther she sholde oute ryde With certeyn folk, he hoved hir tabyde, So wo bigoon, al wolde he nought him pleyne for ire he quook, so gan his herte gnawe, Whan Diomede on horse gan him dresse, And seyde unto bimself this ilke sawe: Allas, quod he, thus foula wrecchednesse Wby suffre ich it, wby nil ich it redresse? Were it not bet at onés for to dye Than evermore in langour thus to drye? Why nil I make at ones riche and pore To have ynough to done, er that she go? Why nil I bringe al Troye upon a rore? Why nil I sleen this Diomede also? Why nil I rather with a man or two Stele hir away? Why wol I this endure? Why nil I helpen to myn owene cure? But why he nolde doon so fela dede, Chat shal I seyn, and wby him liste it spare: He hadde in berte alwey a maner drede, Lest that Criseyde, in rumour of this fare, Sholde han ben slayn; lo, thís was al hís care. And elles, certeyn,as I seyde yore, He hadde it doon, withouten wordes more. Críseyde, whan she redy was to ryde, ful sorwfully she sighte, and seyde Hllas1 But forth she moot, for ought that may bityde, And forth she rit ful sorwfully a pas. Ther nis non other remedie in this cas. What wonder is though that hir sore smerte, Whan she forgoth hir owene swete herte? This Troilus, in wyse of curteisye, With hauke on bond, and with an huge route Of knightes, rood and dide hir companye, Passinge al the valey fer withoute. And ferther wolde han riden, out of doute, ful fayn, and wo was him to goon so sone; But torne he moste, and it was eek to done. And right with that was Antenor ycome Out of the Grekes ost, and every wight Was ofit glad, and seyde he was welcome. And Troilus, al nere his berte light, He peyned bim with al his fulle might Him to withholde of wepinge at the leste, And Antenor he kiste, and made feste. And therwithal he moste his leve take, And caste his eye upon hir pitously, And neer he rood, his cause for to make, To take hir by the honde al sobrely And Lord1 so she gan wepen tendrely1 And he ful softe and sleighly gan hir seye: Now hold your day, and dooth me not to deye. With that his courser torned he aboute With face pale, and unto Diomede No word he spak, ne noon of al his route; ![](images/f85bdbf016d57b409423516da43df7e5ead4a89436cadd78043ae5ee94ab8cc8.jpg) ![](images/18507a713ab35062e1fc901f40695e5866a760b8da5ebef62fd7b486f1decef5.jpg) Of which the sone of Tydeus took hede, As he that coude more than the crede In swich a craft, and by the reyne hír hente; And Troilus to Troye homward he wente. This Diomede, that ladde hír by the brydel, Whan that he saw the folk of Troye aweye, Thoughte:Hlmylabourshalnot beenonydel If that I may, for somwhat shal I seye. for at the worste it may yet shorte our weye. I have herd seyd, eek tymes twyës twelve: He is a fool that wol foryete himselve. But natheles this thoughte he wel ynough: Chat certaynly I am aboute nought If that I speke of love, or make it tough; for douteles, if she have in hir thought Him that I gesse, he may not been ybrought So sone awey; but I shal finde a mene, That she not wite as yet shal what I mene. Thís Díomede, as he that coude his good, Whan this was doon, gan fallen forth in speche Of this and that, and asked why she stood In swich disese, and gan hir eek bíseche, Chat if that he encrese mighte or eche With any thing hir ese, that she sholde Comaunde it hím, and seyde he doon it wolde for trewely he swoor hir, as a knight, That ther nas thing with whiche he mighte hir plese, That he nolde doon his peyne & al bis might To doon it, for to doon hir herte an ese. And preyede hir, she wolde hir sorwe apese And seyde: Ywis, we Grekes con bave joye To honouren yow, as wel as folk of Troye. He seyde eek thus: Iwoot, yowthinketh straunge, No wonder is, for it is to yow newe, Thaqueintaunce of these Troíanes to chaunge, for folk of Grece, that ye never knewe. But wolde never God but if as trewe A Greek ye shulde among us alle finde Hs any Troían is, and eek as kinde. And by the cause I swoor yow right, lo, now, To been your freend,and helply, to my might And for that more acqueintaunce eek of yow Have ich had than another straunger wight, So fro thís forth I pray yow, day and night, Comaundeth me, how sore that me smerte, To doon al that may lyke unto your herte: And that ye me wolde as your brother trete, And taketh not my frendship in despyt; And though your sorwes be for thinges grete tInot wby, but outofmorerespyt Myn herte hath for to amende it greet delyt. Andif I may your harmes not redresse, Iam right sory for your hevinesse. And though ye Troians withus Grekes wrothe Han many a day be, alwey yet, pardee, O god of love in sooth we serven bothe. And, for the love of God, my lady free, Wbom so ye hate, as beth not wroth with me. for trewely, ther can no wight yow serve, Chat half so looth your wraththe wolde deserve. And nere it that we been so neigh the tente Of Calkas. wbich that seen us bothe may, I wolde of this yow telle al myn entente; But this enseled til another day. Veve me vour hond, I am, and shal ben ay, God belp me so, wbyl that my lyf may dure, Your owene aboven every creature. Thus seyde I never er now to womman born; for God myn herte as wisly glade so, I lovede never womman herebiforn Hs paramours, ne never shal no mo. And, for the love of God, beth not my fo; Al can I not to yow, my lady dere, Compleyne aright, for I am yet to lere. And wondreth not, myn owene lady bright, ThoughthatIspekeoflovetoyouthusblyve for I have herd or this of many a wight, Hath loved thing be never saugh his lyve. Eek I am not of power for to stryve Ayens the god of love, but him obeye I wol alwey, and mercy I yow preye. Ther been so worthy knightes in this place And ye so fair, that everich of hem alle Wol peynen bim to stonden in your grace But mighte me so fair a grace falle That ye me for your servaunt wolde calle, So lowly ne so trewely you serve Nil noon of hem, as I shal, til I sterve. Criseide unto that purpos lyte answerde, Hs she that was with sorwe oppressed so That,in effect, shenougbt his tales herde, But here and there, now here a word or two. Hir thoughte hir sorwful herte brast atwo. for whan she gan bir fader fer aspye, Wel neigh doun of hir hors she gan to sye. But natheles she thonked Diomede Of al his travaile, and hís goode chere, And that him liste his friendship hir to bede; And she accepteth it in good manere, And wolde do fayn that is hím leef and dere; And trusten him she wolde, and wel she mighte, As seyde she, and from hir hors she alighte. Hir fader hath hir in his armes nome, And tweynty tyme he kiste his doughter swete, And seyde: O dere doughter myn, welcome1 She seyde eek, she was fayn with him to mete, And stood forth mewet, mildë, and mansuete. But here I leve hir with hir fader dwelle, And forth I wol of Troilus yow telle. Now shulde I thus ten dayes ful endure Whan I the firste night have al this tene? How shal she doon eek, sorwful creature? for tendernesse, how shal she this sustene Swich wo for me? O pitous, pale, and grene Shal been your fresshe wommanliche face for langour, er ye torne unto this place. And whan be fil in any slomeringes, Anoon biginne he sholde for to grone And dremen of the dredfulleste thinges That mighte been; as, mete he were allone In place horrible, makinge ay bis mone, Or meten that he was amonges alle His enemys, and in hír hondes falle. To Troye is come thís woful Troilus, In sorwe aboven alle sorwes smerte, With felon look, and face dispitous Tho sodeinly doun from his hors he sterte, And thorugh his paleys, with a swollen herte, To chambre he wente; of nothing took he hede, Ne noon to hím dar speke a word for drede. And therwithal his body sholde sterte, And with the stert al sodeinliche awake, And swich a tremour fele aboute bis herte, That of the feer bis body sholde quake; And therewithal he sholde a noyse make, And seme as though he sholde falle depe from heighe alofte; and than he wolde wepe. And there his sorwes that he spared hadde He yaf an issue large, and Deeth1 he cryde; And in bis throwes frenetyk and madde Be cursed Jove, Appollo, and eek Cupyde, He cursed Ceres, Bacus, and Cipryde, His burthe, himself, his fate, and eek nature, And, save hís lady, every creature. To bedde he goth, and weyleth there and torneth In furie, as dooth he, Ixion, in belle; And in this wyse he neigh til day sojorneth. But tho bigan his herte a lyte unswelle Chorugh teres which that gonnen up to welle; And pitously he cryde upon Criseyde, And to himself right thus he spak, and seyde: Wber is myn owene lady lief and dere, Wher is hir whyte brest, wher is it, where? Wher ben hir armes and hir eyen clere, Chat yesternight this tyme with me were? Now may I wepe allone many a tere, And graspe aboute I may, but in this place, Save a pilowe, I finde nought tenbrace. How shal I do? ban shal she com ayeyn? I noot, allas1 why leet ich bir to go? As wolde God, ich badde as tho be sleyn1 O herte myn, Criseyde, O swete fol O lady myn, that I love and no mol To whom for evermo myn herte I dowe; See how I deye, ye nil me not rescowel Who seeth yow now, my righte lodessterre? Who sit right now or stant in your presence? Who can conforten now your hertes werre? Now I am gon, whom yeve ye audience? Who speketh for me right now in myn absence? Allas, no wight; and that is al my care; for wel wot I, as yvelas I ye fare. And rewen on himself so pitously, That wonder was to here his fantasye Another tyme he sholde mig Conforte himself, and seyn it was folye, So causeles swich drede for to drye, And eft biginne his aspre sorwes newe, That every man mighte on his sorwes rewe. Who coude telle aright or ful discryve His wo, his pleynte, his langour, and his pyne Nought al the men that han or been onlyve Thou, redere, mayst thyself ful wel devyne That swich a wo my wit can not defyne. On ydel for to wryte it sholde I swinke, Whan that my wit is wery it to thinke. My Pandarus, quod Troilus, the sorwe Which that I drye, I may not longe endure. I trowe I shalnot liven til tomorwe; forwhicheI wolde alwey, on aventure, Cothee devysen of my sepulture The forme, and of my moeble thou dispone Right as thee semeth best is for to done. On hevene yet the sterres were sene, Although ful pale ywaxen was the mone; And wbyten gan the orisonte shene Hlestward, as it woned is to done. And Phebus with his rosy carte sone Gan after that to dresse him up to fare, Whan Troilus hath sent after Pandare. But of the fyr and flaumbe funeral In whiche my body brenne shal to glede, And of the feste and pleyes palestral Atmy vigile, I pray thee take good hede That al be wel; and offre Mars my stede, My swerd, mynbelm, and, leve brother dere, My sheld to Pallas yef, that shyneth clere. The poudre in which myn herte ybrend shal torne, Chat hee thou take and it conserve men clepeth an urne, I to my lady that I serve, r love of whom thus pitously I sterve, So yeve it hir, and do me this plesaunce, To preye hir kepe it for a remembraunce. This Pandare, that of al the day biforn Ne mighte have comen Troilus to see, Hlthough he on his beed it hadde yswor for with the king Pryam alday was he, So that it lay not in his libertee Nowher to gon, but on the morwe he wente To Troilus, whan that he for him sente. for in his herte he coude wel devyne, That Troilus al night for sorwe wook; And that he wolde telle him of his pyne This knew he wel ynough, withoute book. for which to chaumbre streight the wey he took, And Troilus tho sobreliche he grette, And on the bed ful sone he gan him sette. for wel I fele, by my maladye, And by my dremes now and yore ago, Alcerteinly, that I mot nedes dye. Cheowle eek, which that hight Hscaphilo, hath after me sbright alle thise nightes two. And, god Mercuriel of me now, woful wrecche, The soule gyde, and, whan thee list, it fecche1 Pandare answerde, and seyde: Troilus, My dere freend, as I bave told thee yore, lye for to sorwen thus, useles, for whiche I can no more. But whoso wol not trowen reed ne lore, I can not seen in bim no remedye, But lete him worthen with his fantasye. But Troilus, I pray thee tel me now, If that thou trowe, er this, that any wight Bath loved paramours as wel as thou? Ve, God wot, and fro many a worthy knight ħath his lady goon a fourtenight, And be not yet made halvendel the fare. Wbat nede is thee to maken al this care? Sin day by day thou mayst thyselven see That from his love, or elles from his wyf, Aman mot twinnen of necessitee, Ve, though he love hir as his owene lyf; Vet nil be with bimself thus maken stryf. for wel thow wost, my leve brother dere, Thatalwey freendes may nought been yfere. Thy swevenes eek and al swich fantasye Dryf out, and lat hem faren to mischaunce; for they procede of thy malencolye, That doth thee fele in sleep al this penaunce. A straw for alle swevenes signifiaunce1 God helpe me so, I counte hem not a bene, Ther woot no man aright what dremes mene. So sholdestow endure, and late slyde The tyme, and fonde to ben glad and light. Ten dayes nís so longe not tabyde. And sin she thee to comen hath bibight, She nil hir hestes breken for no wight. for dred thee not that she nil finden weye To come ayein, my lyf that dorste I leye. how doon this folk that seen hir loves wedded By freendes might, as it bitit ful ofte, And seen hem in bir spouses bed ybedded? God woot, they take it wysly, faire and softe. forwby good hope halt up hir herte onlofte, And for they can a tyme of sorwe endure; As tyme hem hurt, a tyme doth hem cure. Eek othere seyn that thorugh impressiouns, Hs if a wight hath faste a thing in minde, That therof cometh swiche avisiouns; Hnd othere seyn, as they in bokes finde, That, after tymes of the yeer by kinde, Men dreme, and that theffect goth by the mone; But leve no dreem, for it is nought to done. for prestes of the temple tellen this, That dremes been the revelaciouns Of goddes, and as wel they telle, ywis, That they ben infernals illusiouns; And leches seyn, that of complexiouns Proceden they, or fast, or glotonye. Who woot in sooth thus what they signifye? Wel worth of dremes ay thise olde wyves, And treweliche eek augurie of thise foules; for fere of which men wenen lese her lyves, As ravenes qualm, or shryking of thise oules. To trowen on it bothe fals and foul is. Hllas,allas, so noble a creature Hs ís a man, shal drede swích ordure1 for which with al myn herte I thee beseche, Unto thyself that al this thou foryive; And rys up now withoute more speche, And lat us caste how forth may best be drive This tyme, and eek how freshly we may live Whan that she cometh, the which shal be right sone; God help me so, the beste is thus to done. Rys, lat us speke of lusty lyf in Troye That we han lad, and forth the tyme dryve; And eek of tyme cominge us rejoye, That bringen shal our blisse now so blyve; And langour of these twyës dayes fyve We shal therwith so foryete or oppresse, That wel unnethe it doon shal us duresse. Thís toun is ful of lordes alaboute, And trewes lasten al this mene wbyle. Go we and pleye us in som lusty route To Sarpedon,not hennes but a myle. And thus thou shalt the tyme wel bigyle, And dryve it forth unto that blisful morwe, That thou hir see, that cause is of thy sorwe. 530 Troilus and Now rys, my dere brother Troílus; Criseyde. for certes, it noon honour is to thee Liber V. To wepe, and in thy bed to jouken thus. for trewely, of o thing trust to me, If thou thus ligge a day, or two, or three, The folk wol wene that thou, for cowardyse, Thee feynest syk, and that thou darst not ryse. This Troilus answerde: O brother dere, This knowen folk that han ysuffred peyne, That though he wepe and make sorwful chere, That feleth harm and smert in every veyne, No wonder is; and though I ever pleyne, Or alwey wepe, I am nothing to blame, Sin I have lost the cause of al my game. Nor ther nas houre in al the day or night, Whan he was theras no wight mighte That he ne seyde: O lufsom lady bri How have ye faren, sin that ye were h Welcome, ywis, myn owene lady de But welaway, al this nas but a mase; fortune his howve entended bet to glase. But sin of fyne force I moot aryse, I shalaryse, as sone as ever I may; And God, to whom myn herte I sacrifyse, So sende us hastely the tenthe day for was ther never fowl so fayn of May, As I shal been, whan that she cometh in Troye, Chat cause is of my torment and my joye. The lettres eek, that she of olde tyme Hadde him ysent, be wolde allone rede An bundred sythe, atwixen noon and pryme Refiguringe hir shap, hir womanbede, Withinne bis berte, and every word and dede Chat passed was, and thus he droof toan ende The ferthe day, and seyde, he wolde we But whider is thy reed, quod Troilus, That we may pleye us best in al this toun? By God, my conseilis, quod Pandarus, Co ryde and pleye us with king Sarpedoun So longe of this they speken up and doun, Til Troilus gan at the laste assente To ryse, and forth to Sarpedoun they wente. And seyde: Leve brother Pandarus, Intendestow that we shul herë bleve Til Sarpedoun wol forth co Yet were it fairer that we toke our for Goddes love, lat us now soneat ev Our leve take, and homward lat us torne; for trewely, I nil not thus sojorne This Sarpedoun, as he that honourable Was ever hís lyve, and ful of heígh prowesse, With al that mighte yserved been on table, That deyntee was, al coste it greet richesse, He fedde hem day by day, that swich noblesse, As seyden bothe the moste and eek the leste, Was never er that day wíst at any feste. These ladies eek that at this feste been, Sin that he saw hís lady was aweye, It was his sorwe upon hem for to seen, Or for to here on instrumentz so pleye. for she, that of his herte berth the keye, Was absent, lo, thís was hís fantasye, That no wight sholde make melodye. 540 But what avayleth this to Troilus, That for his sorwe nothing ofit roughte? for ever in oon his herte piëtous ful bisily Críseyde his lady soughte. On hir was ever al that his herte thoughte. Now this, now that, so faste imagininge, That glade, ywis, can him no festeyinge. Pandare answerde: Be we comen bider To fecchen fyr, and rennen hoom ay God helpe me so, I can not tel We mighten goon, if I shal soothly seyn Ther any wight is of us more fayn Than Sarpedoun; and if we hennes bye Thus sodeínly, I holde it vilanye, Nor in this world ther is non instrument Delicious, through wind, or touche, or corde, Hs fer as any wight hath ever ywent, That tonge telle or herte may recorde, Chat at that feste it nas wel herd acorde; Ne of ladíes eek so fayr a companye On daunce, er tho, was never yseyn with yë. On morwe,as sone as day bigan to clere, This Troilus gan of bis sleep tabreyde, for love of God, ful pitously he seyde, As go we seen the paleys of Criseyde; for sin we yet may have namore feste, So lat us seen hir paleys at the leste. And therwithal, his meyne for to blende, in toune for to go, des hous they gonnen wende. rdi this sely Troilus was wol him thoughte his sorweful berte braste atwo. for whan he saugh hir dores sperred alle, Welneigh for sorwe adoun be gan to falle. Thus Pandarus, with alle peyne and wo, Made him to dwelle; and at the woukes ende, Of Sarpedoun they toke hir leve tho, And on hir wey they spedden hem to wende. Quod Troilus: Now God me grace sende, Chat I may finden, at myn homscominge, Criseyde comen1and therwith gan he singe. Sin that we seyden that we wolde bleve With him a wouke; and now, thus sodeinly, The ferthe day to take of him our leve, He wolde wondren on it, trewely Lat us holde forth our purpos fermely; And sin that ye bibighten him to byde, Hold forward now, and after lat us ryde. Ye, hasel wodel thoughte this Pandare, And to himself ful softely he seyde: God woot, refreyden may this hote fare Er Calkas sende Troilus Criseyde But natheles, he japed thus, and seyde And swor,ywis, his herte him wel bihighte, She wolde come as sone as ever she mighte. Whan they unto the paleys were ycomen Of Troilus, they doun of hors alighte, And to the chambre hir wey than han they nomen. And into tyme that it gan to nighte, They spaken of Crisëyde the brighte. And after this, wban that bem bothe leste, They spedde hem fro the soper unto reste. Therwith whan he was war and gan biholde how shet was every windowe of the place, st, him thoughte, his herte gan to colde; which with chaunged deedlich pale face, Withouten word, be forth bigan to pace; And, as God wolde, he gan so faste ryde, That no wight of bis contenaunce aspyde. Than seyde he thus: O paleys desolat, Obou uses whylom best yhight, and disconsolat, rne, of which queynt is the light O paleys, whylom day, that now art night, Weloughtestow to falle, and I to dye, Sin she is went that wont was us to gyel O paleys, whylom croune of houses alle, with sonne of alle blissel which the ruby is outfalle, of wo, that cause hast been of lissel Yet, sin I may no bet, fayn wolde I kisse Thy colde dores, dorste I for this route; And farewel shryne, of which the seyntis oute1 Therwith be caste on Pandarus bis yë With chaunged face, and pitous to bibolde; And whan he mighte his tyme aright aspye, Hvas be rood, to Pandarus be tolde his newe sorwe, and eek his joyes olde, So pitously and with so dede an hewe, That every wight mighte on his sorwe rewe. fro thennesforth he rydeth up and doun, And every thing com him to remembraunce As herood forth by places of the toun In whiche he wbylom hadde al his plesaunce. Lo, yond saugh I myn owene lady daunce; And in that temple, with bir eyen clere, Me caughte first my righte lady dere. And yonder have I herd ful lustíly My dere berte laughe, and yonder pleye Saugh I hir ones eek ful blisfully. And yonder ones to me gan she seye: Now goode swete, love me wel, I preye And yond so goodly gan she me bibolde, That to the deeth myn herte is to hir holde. Thanne thoughte he thus:Oblisful lord Cupyde, Whanne I the proces have in my memorie, ow thou me hast werreyed on every syde, len mighte a book make of it, lyk a storie. Ihat nede is thee to seke on me victorie, Sin I am thyn, and hoolly at thy wille? What joye hastow thyn owene folk to spille? And at that corner, in the yonder hous, Herde I myn alderlevest lady dere So wommanly, with voys melodious, Singen so wel, so goodly, and so clere, That in my soule yet me thinketh I here The blisful soun; and, in that yonder place, My lady first me took unto bir grace. Wel bastow, lord, ywroke on me thyn ire, Thou mighty god, and dredful for to grevel Now mercy, lord,thou wost welI desire Chy grace most, of alle lustes leve. And live and deye I wol in thy bileve; for which I naxe in guerdon but a bone, That thou Criseyde ayein me sende sone. Distreyne hir herte as faste to retorne As thou dost myn to longen bir to see; Than woot I wel, that she nil not sojorne. Now, blisful lord, so cruel thou ne be Anto the blood of Troye, I preye thee, Hs Juno was unto the blood Thebane, for which the folk of Thebes caughte hir bane. And after this he to the yates wente Theras Críseyde outrood a ful good paas, And up and doun ther made he many a wente, Hnd to bimself fulofte be seyde: Hllas from hennes rood my blisse and my solas1 As wolde blisful God now, for his joye, I mighte hir seen ayein come into Troye. And to the yonder hille I gan hir gyde, Allas1 and there I took of hir my level And yond I saugh hir to bir fader ryde, for sorwe of wbich myn herte shal tocleve. And hider hoom I com whan it was eve; And here I dwelle outcast from alle joye, And shal, til I may seen hír eft in Troye. Hnd of himselfimagined be ofte To ben defet,and pale, and waxen lesse Than he was wont, and that men seyde softe: What may it be? who can the sothe gesse Wby Troilus hath al this hevinesse? And al thís nas but his malencolye, Chat he hadde of bimself swich fantasye. Another tyme imaginen he wolde That every wight that wente by the weye Had of him routhe, and that they seyen sholde: I am right sory Troilus wol deye And thus he droof a day yet forth or tweye. Hs ye have herd, swich lyf right gan be lede, As he that stood bitwixen hope and drede. for which bim lyked in bis songes shewe Thencheson of his wo, as he best mighte, And make a song of wordes but a fewe, Somwbat his woful berte for to lighte. And whan he was from every mannes sighte, With softe voys he, of his lady dere, That was absent, gan sínge as ye may here. Chat I was born1 After my deeth; for now live I to lo for now is wors O sterre, of which I lost have al the light, With herte soor wel oughte I to bewayle, That ever derk in torment, night by night Toward my deeth with wind in stere I sayle; for which the tenthe night if that I fayle The gyding of thy bemes brighte an houre, My ship and me Caribdis wol devoure. Colateis now to speke of this matere; prudence, allas con of thyn eyen three On tyme ypassed, wel remembred me; And present tyme eek coude I wel ysee. But futur tyme, er I was in the snare, Coude I not seen; that causeth now my care. My fader nil for nothing do me grace And if so be that I my terme p My Troilus shal in bis herte d That I am fals, and so it may wel seme Thus shal I have untbank on every syde; That I was born, so weylawey the But natheles, bityde what bityde, Ishal tomorwe at night, by est or weste, go with Troilus wheras him les This song when he thus songen hadde, sone He fil ayein into bis sykes olde; And every night, as was hís wone to done, He stood the brighte mone to beholde, And al his sorwe he to the mone tolde; And seyde: Ywis, whan thou art horned newe, I shal be glad, if al the world be trewe! Criseyde mene was of hír stature, Therto of shap, of face, and eek of chere, Cher mighte been no fairer creature. And ofte tyme this was hir manere, To gon ytressed with hir heres clere Doun by hir coler at hir bak bihinde, Which with a threde of golde she wolde binde. And if that I me putte in jupartye, To stele awey by nighte, and it bifalle That I be caught, I shal be holde a spye Or elles, lo, this drede I most of all If in the hondes of som wrecche I falle I am but lost,al be myn berte trewe; Now mighty God, thou on my sorwe rewel for whoso wole of every word take hede, Orrewlen him by every wightes wit, Neshal be never thryven, out of drede. for that that som men blamen ever yit, Lo, other maner folk commenden it. And as for me, for al swich variaunce, felicitee clepe I my suffisaunce. And, save hir browes joyneden yfere, Ther nas no lak, in ought I can espyen; But for to speken of hir eyen clere, Lo, trewely, they writen that hir syen, That Paradys stood formed in hir yën. And with hir riche beautee evermore Strof love in hir, ay which of hem was more. I saugh thyn hornes olde eek by the morwe, Wban bennes rood my righte lady dere, Chat cause is of my torment and my sorwe; for whiche, O brighte Lucina the clere, for love of God, ren faste aboute thy spere for whan thyn hornes newe ginne springe, Than shal she come, that may my blisse bringe1 Thís Díomede, as bokes us declare, Was in hís nedes prest and corageous; With sterne voys and mighty limes square, Hardy, testif, strong,and chevalrous Of dedes, lyk hís fader Tideus. And som men seyn, he was of tunge large; And heir be was of Calidoine and Hrge. ful pale ywaxen was bir brighte face, Hir limes lene, as she that al the day Ther she was born, and ther she dwelt haddeay And al the night wepinge, allas1 she lay And thus despeired, out of alle cure She ladde hir tyf, this woful creature for which, withouten any wordes mo, ye I wol, as for conclusioun. d it wot, er fully monthes two, She was ful fer fro that entencioun. for bothe Troilus and Troye toun Shal knotteles throughout hir berte slyde; for she wol take a purpos for tabyde. The day is more, and lenger every night, Than they be wont to be, him thoughte tho; And that the sonne wente his course unright By lenger wey than it was wont to go And seyde: Ywis, me dredeth evermo, The sonnes sone, Pheton, be onlyve, And that hís fadres cart amis he dryve. She sobre was, eek simple, and wys withal, The beste ynorisshed eek that mighte be, And goodly of hir speche in general, Charitable, estatliche, lusty,and free; Ne nevermo ne lakkede hir pitee; Tendresherted,slydinge ofcorage; But trewely, I can not telle hir age. ful ofte a day she sighte eek for destresse, And in birself she wente ay portrayin Of Troilus the grete worthir And alle his goodly wordes recordinge Sin first that day hir love bigan to springe And thus she sette hir woful herte afyr Thorugh remembraunce of that she gan desvre. This Diomede, of whom yow telle I gan, Goth now, withinne himself ay arguinge the sleighte and al that ever he can, he may best, with shortest taryinge, Into his net Criseydes herte bringe. Co this entente be coude never fyne; Cofisshen hir, he leyde out hook and lyne. And Troilus wel waxen was in highte, And complet formed by proporcioun So wel, that kinde it not amenden mighte; Yong, fresshe, strong, and hardy as lyoun; Trewe as steelin ech condicioun; On of the beste enteched creature, That is, or shal, whyl that the world may dure. Upon the walles faste eek wolde he walke, And on the Grekes ost he wolde see, And to himself right thus he wolde talke: Lo, yonder is myn owene lady free, r elles yonder, ther tho tentes be And thennes comth this eyr, that is so sote, That in my soule I fele it doth me bote. In al this world ther nis so cruel herte That hir hadde herd compleynen in hir sorwe, That nolde han wopen for hir peynes smerte So tendrely she weep, bothe eve and morwe Hir nedede no teres for to borwe And this was yet the worste of al hir peyne, Ther was no wight to whom she dorste hir pleyne. But natheles, wel in his herte he thoughte, That she nas nat withoute a love in Troye. n be bir thennes broughte, en ber laughe or make joye. te how best hir herte for tacoye. But for to assaye, he seyde, it nought ne for he that nought nassayeth, nought nacheveth. And hardely this wind, that more and more Thus stoundemele encreseth in my face, Is of my ladyes depe sykes sore I preve it thus, for in non othere place Of al thís toun, save onliche in this space, fele I no wind that souneth so lyk peyne; It seyth: Allas1 why twinned be we tweyne? And certainty in storie it is yfounde, That Troilus was never unto no wight, As in bis tyme, in no degree secounde In durring don that longeth to a knight. Al mighte a geaunt passen him ofmight, His herte ay with the firste and with the beste Stod paregal, to durre don that him leste. This longe tyme he dryveth forth right thus, Til fully passed was the nynthe night; And ay bísyde hím was thís Pandarus, That bisily dide alle bis fulle might Him to comforte,and make his herte light; Yevinge him hope alwey, the tenthe morwe That she shal come, and stínten al his sorwe. ful rewfully she loked upon Troye, Bibeld the toures heighe and eek the balles; Hllas1 quod she, the plesaunce and the joye The whiche that now al torned into galle is, Have I had ofte withinne yonder walles O Troilus, what dostow now, she seyde; Lord1 whether yet thou thenke upon Criseyde? Allas1 I ne hadde trowed on your lore, And went with yow, as ye me radde er this Channe hadde I now not syked half so sore. Who mighte have seyd, that I had doon amis To stele awey with swich on as heis? But al to late cometh the letuarie Whan men the cors únto the grave carie. Yet seide he to himself upon a night: Now am I not a fool, that woot wel how Hir wo for love is of another wight, And bereupon to goon assaye hir now? Imay wel wite, it nil not been my prow. for wyse folk in bokes it expresse: Men shall not wowe a wight in hevinesse But whoso mighte winnen swich a flour from him, for whom she morneth night and day, Hemighte seyn, he were a conquerour. Hnd right anoon, as he that bold was ay, Choughte in his herte: Happe, how happe may, Alsholde I deye, I wole hir herte seche; Ishal no more lesen but my speche. But for to tellen forth of Diomede: It fil that after, on the tenthe day, Sin that Criseyde out of the citee yede, Chis Diomede, as fresshe as braunche in May, Come to the tente theras Calkas lay, And feyned bim with Calkas han to done; But wbat he mente, I shal yow telle sone. Criseyde, at shorte wordes for to telle, Welcomed bim, and doun by bir him sette; And he was ethe ynough to maken dwelle. And after this, withouten longe lette, The spyces and the wyn men forth hem fette And forth they speke of this and that yfere, Hs freendes doon, of which som shal ye here. 543 Troilus and He gan first fallen of the werre in speche Criseyde. Bitwixe hem and the folk of Troye toun; LiberV. And of thassege he gan bir eek byseche, To telle him what was hir opinioun fro that demaunde he so descendeth doun To asken bir, if that hir straunge thoughte The Grekes gyse, & werkes that they wroughte? What wole ye more, lufsom lady dere? And clepe ayein the beautee dchere That ye with salte teres so defac That, it to save And why hir fader tarieth so longe To wedden hir unto som worthy wight? Criseyde, that was in hir peynes stronge for love of Troilus, hir owene knight, As ferforth as she conning hadde or might, Answerde him tho; but, as of his entente, It semed not she wiste what he mente. And thenketh wel, ye shal in Grekes finde A more parfit love, er it be night Chan any Troyan is, and more ki And bet to serven yow wol doon his mig And if ye vouche sauf, my lady brig I wol ben he to serven yow my Ye, lever than be lord of Greces But natheles, this ilke Diomede Gan in bimself assure, and thus he seyde: If ich aright have taken of yow hede, Me thinketh thus, O lady myn, Criseyde, That sin I first hond on your brydel leyde, Whan ye out come of Troye by the morwe, Ne coude I never seen yow but in sorwe. And with that word he gan to waxen reed And in his speche a litel wight he quook And caste asyde a litel wight his heed And stinte a whyle; and afterward awook, And sobreliche on bir he threw bis look, And seyde: I am, al be it yow no joye, As gentil man as any wight in Troye. Can I not seyn what may the cause be But if for love of som Troyan it were, The which right sore wolde athinken me That ye, for any wight that dwelleth there, Sholden spille a quarter of a tere, Or pitously yourselven so bigyle; for dredelees, it is nought worth the whyle. The folk of Troye, as who seyth, alle and some In preson been, as ye yourselven see; for thennes shal not oon onlyve come for al the gold bitwixen sonne and see. Trusteth wel,and understondeth me, Ther shal not oon to mercy goon onlyve, Al were he lord of worldes twyës fyvel Swich wreche on hem, for fecching of Eleyne, Ther shal be take, er that we hennes wende, That Manes, which that goddes ben of peyne Shal been agast that Grekes wol hem shende. And men shul drede, unto the worldes ende, from hennesforth to ravisshe any quene, So cruel shal our wreche on hem be sene. for if my fader Tydeus, he seyde, Ylived hadde, I hadde been, er t And so hope I that I shal But he was slayn, allas1 the more harm is, Unhappily at Thebes al to rathe Polymites and many a man to scathe. And but if Calkas lede us with ambages, That is to seyn, with double wordes slye, Swich as men clepe a word with two visages, Ye shul wel knowen that I nought ne lye, And al this thing right seen it with your yë, And that anoon; ye nil not trowe how sone; Now taketh heed, for it is for to done. But herte myn, sín that I am your man, And been the ferste of whom I seche grace, To serven you as hertely as And ever shal, whyl I to live have space, So, er that I departe out of this place, Ye wol me graunte, that I may tomorwe At bettre leyser, telle yow my sorwe. But as to speke of love, ywis, she seyde, Ihaddea lord, to whom I wedded was, The whos myn herteal was, til that he deyde; And other love, as helpe me now Pallas, Ther in myn berte nis, ne never was. And that ye been of noble and heigh kinrede, Ihave wel berd it tellen, out of drede. Chat Grekes been of beigh condicioun, I woot eek wel; but certein, men shal finde Asconning, and as parfit and as kinde, As been bitwixen Orcades and Inde. And that ye coude wel your lady serve, Itroweeek wel, hir thank for to deserve. What wene ye your wyse fader wolde Han yeven Äntenor for yow anoon, If he ne wiste that the citee sholde Destroyed been? Why, nay, so moteI goon1 He knew ful wel ther shal not scapen oon That Troyan is; and for the grete fere, He dorste not, ye dwelte lenger there. 544 And that doth me to han so gret a wonder, That ye wol scornen any womman so. Eek, God wot, love and I be fer asonder; Lam disposed bet, so mote I go, Unto my deeth, to pleyne and maken wo. @hat I shal after doon, I can not seye; But trewely, as yet me list not pleye. And after this the story telleth us, That she him yaf the faire baye stede, The which she ones wan of Troilus; And eek a broche, and that was litel nede, That Troilus was, she yaf this Diomede. And eek, the bet from sorwe him to releve, She made hím were a pencel of hir sleve. Myn berte is now in tribulacioun, Andy in armes bísy, day by day. ye wonnen han the toun, , thanne so it happen may, That whan I see that I never er say, Than wole I werke that I never wroughtel This word to yow ynough suffysen oughte. Wbat shold I telle his wordes that he seyde? He spak ynow, for o day at the mest It preveth wel, he spak so th Graunted, on the morwe, at his requeste for to speken with him at the leste, So that he nolde speke of swich matere; And thus to hím she seyde, as ye may here: I fnde eek in the stories elleswhere, Whan through the body hurt was Diomede Of Troilus, tho weep she many a tere, Whan that she saugh his wyde woundes blede; And that she took to kepen him good bhede, And for to hele him of his sorwes smerte. Men seyn, I not, that she yaf him bir herte. Tomorwe eek wol I speke with yow fayn, So that ye touchen nought of this matere. And wban yow list, ye may come here ayeyn; And, er ye gon, thus muche I seye yow here: As helpe me Pallas with bir heres clere, If that I sholde of any Greek ban routhe, Itsholde be yourselven, by my trouthel The morwe com, and goostly for to speke, This Diomede is come unto Criseyde, And shortly, lest that ye my tale breke, So wel he for himselve spak and seyde, That alle hir sykes sore adoun he leyde. And fynally, the sothe for to seyne, He refte hir of the grete of al hir peyne. As she that hadde hir herte on Troilus So faste, that ther may it noon arace Hnd straungely she spak, and seyde thus: O Diomede, I love that ilke place Ther I was born; and Joves, for his grace, Delivere it sone of al that doth it care God, for thy might, so leve it wel to farel But trewely, the story telleth us, Ther made never womman more wo Than she, whan that she falsed Troilus. She seyde: Hllas1 for nowis clene ago My name of trouthe in love, for evermol for I have falsed oon, the gentileste That ever was, and oon the worthiestel That Grekes wolde hir wraththe on Troye wreke, If that they mighte, I knowe it wel, ywis. But it shal not bifallen as ye speke; And God toforn, and ferther over this, I wot my fader wys and redy is; And that he me hath bought, as ye me tolde, So dere, I am the more unto hím holde. Retorning in bir soule ay up and doun The wordes of this sodein Diomede, His greet estat, and peril of the toun, And that she was allone and badde nede Of freendes help; and thus bigan to brede The cause why, the sothe for to telle, That she tok fully purpos for to dwelle. Isey not therfore that I wol yow love, NeI sey not nay, but in conclusioun, Imene wel, by God that sit above: And therwithal she caste hir eyen doun, And gan to syke, and seyde: O Troye toun, Yet bidde I God, in quiete and in reste Imay yow seen, or do myn herte breste. Allas, of me, unto the worldes ende, Shalneither been ywriten nor ysonge No good word, for thise bokes wol me shende. O, rolled sbal I been on many a tonge; Throughout the world my belle shal beronge; And wommen most wol hate me of alle. Allas, that swich a cas me sholde falle1 But in effect, and shortly for to seye, This Diomede al freshly newe ayeyn Gan pressen on, and faste hir mercy preye; And after this, the sothe for to seyn, Hir glove he took, of which he was ful fayn. And fynally, whan it was waxen eve, And al was wel, he roos and took his leve. The brighte Venus folwede and ay taughte The wey, ther brode Phebus doun alighte; And Cynthea hir charshors overraughte To whirle out of the Lyon, if she mighte; And Signifer his candeles shewed brighte, Whan that Criseyde unto hir bedde wente Inwith hir fadres faire brighte tente. nn1 They wol seyn, in as muche as in me is, I have hem don dishonour, weylawey Al be I not the frste that dide amis, Wbat helpeth that to do my blame awey? But sin I see there is no bettre way, And that to late is now for me to rewe, To Diomede algate I wol be trewe. But Troilus, sin I no better may, And sin that thus departen ye and I Yet preye I God, so yeve yow right good day As for the gentileste, trewely, That ever I say, to serven feithfully, And best can ay hís lady honour kepe: And with that word she brast anon to wepe. 545 And certes, yow ne haten shal I never, And freendes love, that shal ye han of me, And my good word, al mighte I liven ever. And, trewely, I wolde sory be for to seen yow in any adversitee. And giltelees, I woot wel, I yow leve; But al shal passe; and thus take I my leve. Quod Troilus: I see wel now, that she Is taried with hir olde fader so That er she come, it wol neigh even be. Com forth, I wol unto the yate Thise portours been unkonninge evermo; And I wol doon hem holden up the yate As nought ne were, although she come late. for which she may yet holde al bir biheste. And on the morwe unto the yate he wente, apon the walles made be many a wente. he wente him hoom, withouten any more. But trewely, how longe it was bitwene, That she forsook him for this Diomede, Ther is non auctor telleth it, I wene. Take every man now to his bokes hede; He shal no terme finden, out of drede. for though that he bigan to wowe hir sone, Er he hir wan, yet was ther more to done. The day goth faste, andafter that comth eve, And yet com nought to Troilus Crisey He loketh forth by begge, by tree, And fer his heed over the wal And at the laste he torned bim By God, I woot hir mening now, Pandare! Himost, ywis, al newe was my care My lady bright Criseyde hath me bitrayed, In whom I trusted most of any wight, She elleswbere hath now hir herte apayed; The blisful goddes, through bir grete might, Han in my dreem yshewed it ful right. Thus in my dreem Criseyde I have bibolde. Hnd al this thing to Pandarus he tolde. Chis bope alclene out of his herte fledde, henath wheron now lenger for to bonge; for when he saugh that she abood so longe, heniste what he juggen of it mighte, Sin she hath broken that she bim bibighte. O my Criseyde,allas1 what subtiltee, What newe lust, what beautee, what science, What wratthe of juste cause have ye to me? What gilt of me, what fel experience Hath fro me raft, allas1 thyn advertence? O trust, O feyth, O depe asêuraunce, Who hath me reft Criseyde, al my plesaunce? Ne me ne list thís sely womman chyde ferther than the story wol devyse. Hir name, allasl is publisshed so wyde, That for hir gilt it oughte ynow suffyse. And if I mighte excuse hir any wyse, for she so sory was for hir untrouthe, Ywis, I wolde excuse hir yet for routhe. Now douteles, thís lady can hir good; I woot, she meneth ryden prively. I comende hir wysdom, by myn hood She wolnot maken peplenycely Gaure on bir, whan she comth; but softely By nighte into the toun she thenketh ryde And, dere brother, thenk not longe to abyde Che thridde, ferthe, ffte, sixte day After tho dayes ten, of which I tolde, be and drede his herte lay mwhat trustinge on bir hestes olde But whan he saugh she nolde hir terme holde, Allas1 wby leet I you from hennes go, for which wel neigh out of my wit I breyde? Who shal now trowe on any othes mo? God wot I wende, O lady bright, Criseyde That every word was gospel that ye seydel But who may bet bigylen, if him liste, Than he on whom men weneth best to triste? This Troilus, as I bíforn have told Thus dryveth forth, as wel as he hath might. But often was his herte hoot and cold, And namely, that ilke nynthe night, Wbich on the morwe she hadde him byhight To come ayein: God wot, ful litel reste Hadde he that night; nothing to slepe him leste. We han nought elles for to don, ywis. And Pandarus, now woltow trowen me? Have here my trouthe, I see birl yond she is Heve up thyn eyen, man1 maystow no Pandare answerde: Nay, so mote I theel Hl wrong, by God; what seystow, man, wherart? Chat I see yond nis but a farercart. Cherwith the wikked spirit, God us blesse, that men clepeth wode jalousye, crepe, in al this hevinesse ich, bycause he wolde sone dye And eek from every companye he fledde; This was the lyf that al the tyme he ledde. What shal I doon, my Pandarus, allas1 I fele now so sharpe a newe peyne, Sin that ther is no remedie in this cas, That bet were it I with myn bondes tweyne Myselven slow, than alwey thus to pleyne. for through my deeth my wo sholde han an ende, Ther every day with lyf myself I shende. The laurerscrouned Phebus, with his hete, Gan, in hís course ay upward as he wente, To warmen of the est see the wawes wete; And Nisus doughter song with fresh entente, Wban Troilus his Pandare after sente; And on the walles of the toun they pleyde, To loke if they can seen ought of Criseyde. Hllas, thou seist right sooth, quod Troilus; But hardely, it is not al for nought Chat in myn herte I now rejoyse thus It is ayein som good I have a thought. Noot I not how, but sin that I was wrought, Ne felte I swich a confort, dar I seye; She comth tonight, my lyf, that dorste I leyel he so defet was, that no maner man Annethe mighte him knowe ther he wente; So was he lene, and therto pale and wan, And feble, that he walketh by potente; And with his ire he thus bimselven shente. And whoso axed him wherof him smerte, He seyde, hís harm was alaboute his herte. Pandare answerde and seyde: Hllas the wbyle That I was born; have I not seyd er this, That dremes many a maner man bigyle? And wby? for folk expounden hem amis. How darstow seyn that fals thy lady is, for any dreem, right for thyn owene drede? Lat be this thougbt, thou canst no dremes rede. Til it was noon, they stoden for to see Who that ther come; and every maner wight, That cam fro fer, they seyden it was she, Til that they coude knowen bim aright. Now was his herte dul, now was it light; And thus byjaped stonden for to stare Aboute nought, this Troilus and Pandare. Pandare answerde: It may be wel, ynough; Änd held with him ofal that ever he seyd But in his herte he thoughte, and softe lou And to himself ful sobrely he seyde from hasel wode, ther joly Robin pleyde, Shal come al that that thou abydest here; Ye, farewel al the snow of ferne yerel Pryam ful ofte, and eek his moder dere, his bretheren and bis sustren gonne him freyne he so sorwful wasin al his chere, d what thing was the cause of al his peyne? But al for nought; he nolde hís cause pleyne, But seyde, he felte a grevous maladye Aboute his herte, and fayn he wolde dye. Paraunter, ther thou dremest of this boor, It may so be that it may signifye Hir fader, which that old is and eek hoor, Hyein the sonne lyth, on poynt to dye, And she for sorwe ginneth wepe and crye, And kisseth bim, ther he lyth on the grounde; Thus shuldestow thy dreem aright expounde. To Pandarus thís Troílus tho seyde: for ought I wot, bifor noon, sikerly, Into this toun ne comth nought here Criseyde. She hath ynow to done, hardily, To winnen from hir fader, so trowe I; Hir olde fader wol yet make hir dyne Er that she go; God yeve his herte pynel Pandare answerde: It may wel be, certeyn; And forthy lat us dyne, I thee biseche; And after noon than mayst thou come ayeyn. Hnd hoom they go, withoute more speche; And comen ayein, but longe may they seche Er that they finde that they after cape; fortune hem bothe thenketh for to jape. 546 The wardein of the yates gan to calle The folk which that withoute the yates were, And bad hem dryven in bir bestes alle, Or al the night they moste bleven there. And fer within the night, with many a tere, This Troilus gan hoomward for to ryde; for wel he seeth it helpeth nought tabyde. But natheles, he gladded hím in this; He thoughte he misacounted badde his day, And seyde: I understonde bave alamís. for thilke night I last Criseyde say, She seyde: I shal ben here, if that I may, Er that the mone, O dere herte swetel The Lyon passe, out of this Hriete So on a day he leyde hím doun to slepe, And so bifel that in his sleep him thoughte, Chat in a forest faste he welk to wepe forlove of bir that him thise peynes wroughte; And up and doun as he the forest soughte, Hemette he saugh a boor with tuskes grete, That sleep ayein the brighte sonnes hete. How mighte I thanne do? quod Troilus, To knowe of this, ye, wereit never so lyte? Now seystow wysly, quod this Pandarus, My reed is this, sin thou canst wel endyte, That hastely a lettre thou hir wryte, Thorugh which thou shalt wel bringen it aboute, To knowe a sooth of that thou art in doute. And by this boor, faste in his armes folde, Lay kissing ay bis lady bright Criseyde: for sorwe of wbich, whan he it gan bibolde, And for despyt, out of his slepe he breyde, And loude he cryde on Pandarus, and seyde: OPandarus, now knowe I crop and rote1 Inam but deed, ther nis non other botel nn2 And see now why; for this I dar wel seyn, That if so is that she untrewe be, I can not trowe that she wol wryte ayeyn. And if she wryte, thou shalt ful sone see, Hs wbether she hath any libertee To come ayein, or elles in som clause, If she be let, she wolassigne a cause. Thou hast not writen hir sin that she wente, Nor she to thee, and this I dorste leye, Cher may swich cause been in hir entente, That hardely thou wolt thyselven seye, That hir abood the beste is for yow tweye. Now wryte hir thanne, and thou shalt fele sone A sothe of al; ther is no more to done. The whos welfare and bele eek God encress In honour swich, that upward in de It growe alwey, so that it never Right as your berte ay can Devyse, I prey to God so moteit be And graunte it that ye sone upon me rewe As wisly as in al I am yow ith hele swich that, but ye yeven me The same hele, I shal noon bele have. Me from disese of alle peynes smerte; Le vostre T. She, for that Grekes gonne hir so dispyse, rak hir in a wonder cruel wyse. for with a boor as greet as oxe in stalle She made up frete hir corn and vynes alle. Acorded been to this conclusioun, And that anoon, thise ilke lordes two; And hastely sit Troilus adoun And rolleth in his herte to and fro, How he may best discryven hir his wo. And to Criseyde, his owene lady dere, He wroot right thus, and seyde as ye may here. And if yow lyketh knowen of the fare Of me, whos wo ther may no wight discryve I can no more but, cheste of every car At wrytinge of this lettre I was Alredy out my woful gost to dry Which I delaye, and bolde bim yet in bonde, Upon the sight of matere of your so This lettre forth was sent unto Criseyde, of which hir answere in effect was this; ful pitously she wroot ayein, and seyde, That also sone as that she might, ywis, She wolde come, and mende al that was mis. And fynally she wroot and seyde bim thanne, She wolde come, ye, but she niste whanne. Co slee this boor was al the contree reysed, Amonges which ther com, this boor to see, A mayde, oon of this world the best ypreysed; And Meleagre, lord of that contree, He lovede so this fresshe mayden free That with his manbood, er he wolde stente, This boor he slow, and hir the heed he sente; Right fresshe flour, whos I have been and shal, Withouten part of elleswhere servyse, With herte, body, lyf, lust, thought, and al; I, woful wight, in every humble wyse Chat tonge telle or herte may devyse, As ofte as matere occupyeth place, Merecomaunde unto your noble grace. Myn eyen two, in veyn with which I see Of sorweful teres salte arn waxen well My song, in pleynte of myn adv My good, in barm; myn ese eek waxen belle is My joye, in wo; I can sey yow nought elles, But turned is, for which my lyf I warie, Everich joye or ese in his contrarie. Of which, as olde bokes tellen us, Ther roos a contek and a greet envye; And of this lord descended Tydeus By ligne, or elles olde bokes lye; But how this Meleagre gan to dye Thorugh his moder, wol I yow not telle, for al to long it were for to dwelle But in bir lettre made she swich festes, nd but botmelees bihestes. , thou mayst now, est or west, Pype in an ivy leef, if that thee lest; Thus gooth the world; God shilde us fro Lyketh it yow to witen, swete herte, As ye wel knowe how longe tyme agoon That ye me lafte in aspre peynes smerte, Whan that ye wente, of which yet bote noon Have I non had, but ever wers bigoon fro day to day am I, and so mot dwelle, While it yow list, of wele and wo my welle1 Which with your cominge hoom ayein to Troye Ye may redresse, and, more a thousand s Than ever ich hadde, encressen i for was ther never herte yet so bly To han his lyf, as I shal been as swythe As I yow see; and, though no maner routhe Commeve yow, yet thinketh on your trouthe. And every wight that meneth trouthe avaunce1 for which to yow, with dredful herte trewe, I wryte, as he that sorwe dryfth to wryte, My wo, that every houre encreseth newe, Compleyninge as I dar or can endyte. And that defaced is, that may ye wyte The teres, which that fro myn eyen reyne, That wolde speke, if that they coude, and pleyne. Argument of the 12 Books of Statius' Thebais. Assocíat profugum Tideo prímus Polímítem; Tidea legatum docet insidiasque secundus; Tercíus Hemoniden canit et vates latítantes; Quartus habet reges ineuntes prelia septem; Mox furie Lenne quinto narratur et anguis; Archimori bustum sexto ludique leguntur; Dat Graios Thebes et vatem septímus umbrís; Octavo cecidít Tideus, spes, víta Pelasgis; Ypomedon nono moritur cum Parthonopeo; fulmine percussus, decímo Capaneus superatur; Andecimo sese perímunt per vulnera fratres; Argivam flentem narrat duodenus etignem. Encresen gan the wo fro day to night Of Troilus, for taryinge of Criseyde; And lessen gan his hope and eek his might, for which al doun he in his bed him leyde; Hene eet, ne dronk, ne sleep, ne word he seyde, Imagininge ay that she was unkinde; for which wel neigh he wex out of his minde. And if so be my gilt hath deeth deserved, Orif you list no more upon me see, In guerdon yet of that I have you served Biseche I yow, myn hertes lady free, That hereupon ye wolden wryte me, for love of God, my righte lodessterre, Ther deeth may make an ende of al my werre. Yow first biseche I, that your eyen clere To look on this defouled ye not holde; And over al this, that ye, my lady dere, Wol vouchesauf this lettre to bibolde. And by the cause eek of my cares colde, That sleeth my wit, if ought amis me asterte, foryeve it me, myn owene swete herte. She toldë eek how Tydeus, er she stente, Anto the stronge citee of Thebes, To cleyme kingdom of the citee, wente, for bis felawe, daun Polymites, Of which the brother, daun Ethyocles, ful wrongfully of Thebes helde the strengthe; This tolde she by proces, al by lengthe. Chis dreem, of which I told have eek biforn, May never come out of his remembraunce; wel he hadde his lady lorn s,of his purveyaunce hadde in sleep the signifiaunce Of hir untrouthe and bis disaventure, And that the boor was shewed him in figure. If other cause aught doth yow for to dwelle, That with your lettre ye me recomforte for though to me your absence is an helle, With pacience I wol my wo comporte And with your lettre of hope I wol desporte. Now wryteth, swete, and lat me thus not pleyne; With hope, or deeth, delivereth me fro peyne. If any servant dorste or oughte of right Upon his lady pitously compleyne, Than wene I, that ich oughte be that wight, Considered this, that ye these monthes tweyne Han taried, ther ye seyden, sooth to seyne, But dayes ten ye nolde in ost sojourne, But in two monthes yet ye not retourne. She tolde eek how Hemonides asterte, Whan Tydeus slough fifty knightes stoute. She tolde eek al the propbesyes by berte, And how that sevene kinges, with hir route, Bisegeden the citee alaboute; And of the holy serpent,and the welle, And of the furies, al she gan him telle. for which he for Sibille his suster sente, Chat called was Cassandre eek alaboute; And al hís dreem he tolde hír er he stente, And bir bisoughte assoilen bim the doute Of the stronge boor, with tuskes stoute; And fynally, withinne a litel stounde, Cassandre him gan right thus his dreem expounde. But forasmuche as me mot nedes lyke Al that yow list, I dar not pleyne more, But humbely with sorwful sykes syke; Yow wryteich myn unresty sorwes sore, fro day to day desyríng evermore To knowen fully, if your wil it were How ye han ferd and doon, whyl ye be there. 548 Ywís, myn owene dere herte trewe, I woot that, wban ye next upon me see, So lost have I myn hele and eek myn bewe Criseyde shal nought conne knowe mel Ywís, myn hertes day, my lady free, So thursteth ay myn herte to bibolde Your beautee, that my lyf unnethe I holde. I sey no more, al have I for to seye To you wel more than I telle may; But whether that ye do me live or deye, Yet pray I God, so yeve yow right good day. And fareth wel, goodly fayre fressbe may, As ye that lyf or deeth me may comaunde; And to your trouthe ay I me recomaunde She gan first smyle, and seyde: O brother dere, Ifthou a sooth of this desyrest knowe, Chou most a fewe of olde stories here, Co purpos, how that fortune overthrowe Hath lordes olde; through which, withinne a throwe, Thou wel this boor shalt knowe, & of what kinde Thou wel this boor shalt knowe, & of what kinde He comen is, as men in bokes fnde. Of Archímoris buryinge and the pleyes, And how Amphiorax fil through the grounde, How Tydeus was slayn, lord of Hrgeyes, And how Ypomedoun in litel stounde Was dreynt, and deed Parthonope of wounde; And also how Cappanëus the proude With thondersdint was slayn, that cryde loude. Diane, which that wrooth was and in ire for Grekes nolde doon bir sacrifyse, Ne encens upon hír auter sette afyre, nn3 She gan eek telle him how that either brother, Ethyocles and Polimyte also, At a scarmyche, eche of hem slough other, And of Arayves wepinge and hir wo; 549 Troilus and And how the town was brent she tolde eek tho. Criseyde. And so descendeth doun from gestes olde iomede, and thus she spak and to This ilke boor bitokneth Diomede, Tydeus sone, that doun descended is fro Meleagre, that made the boor to blede. And thy lady, wherso she be, ywis, This Diomede hir herte hath, and she his. Weep if thou wolt, or leef; for out of doute, This Diomede is inne, and thou art oute. And namely, the sorwe of Troilus, That next him was of worthinesse welle And in this wo gan Troilus to dwelle That, what for sorwe, and love, and for unreste, ful ofte a day be bad his herte breste. Thou seyst nat sooth, quod he, thou sorceresse, But natheles, though be gan bim dispeyre, And dradde ay that hís lady was unt Yet ay on hir his herte gan rep To gete ayein Criseyde, bright of hewe. And in his herte he wente bir excusinge, Chat Calkas causede al bir tary Comen I wol, but yet in swich disjoynte Istonde as now, that what yeer or what day That this shal be, that can I not apoynte. But in effect, I prey yow, as I may, for trewely, whyl that my lyf may dure, As for a freend, ye may in me assure. yet preye I yow on yvel ye ne take, Thatit is short which that I to yow wryte; Idar not, ther I am, wel lettres make, Nenever yetne coude I wel endyte. Eck greet effect men wryte in place lyte. Chentente is al, and nought the lettres space; And fareth now wel, God have you in his gracel La vostre C. Chan spak be thus: O lady myn Criseyde, Wher is your feyth, and wber is your bibeste? Wher is your love, wheris your trouthe, he seyde; Of Diomede have ye now al this festel Allas, I wolde have trowed at the leste, That, sin ye nolde in trouthe to me stonde, That ye thus nolde han holden me in honde1 Who shal now trowe on any othes mo? Allas, I never wolde han wend, er this, That ye, Criseyde, coude han chaunged so; Ne, but I hadde agilt and doon amís, So cruel wende I not your herte, ywis, To slee me thus; allas, your name of trouthe Is now fordoon, and that is al my routhe. With al thy false goost of prophesye Thou wenest been a greet devyneresse; Now seestow not this fool of fantasye Peyneth hir on ladyes for to lye? Awey, quod he, ther Joves yeve thee sorwe1 Thou shalt be fals, paraunter, yet tomorwe1 And ofte tyme he was in purpos grete Himselven lyk a pilgrim to disgyse To seen hir; but be may not con To been unknowen of folk that weren wyse, Ne finde excuse aright that may suffyse If he among the Grekes knowen were; for which he weep ful ofte many a tere. As wel thou mightest lyen on Alceste, That was of creatures, but men lye, That ever weren, kindest and the beste. for wbanne hir housbonde was in jupartye To dye himself, but if she wolde dye She chees for him to dye and go to helle, And starf anoon, as us the bokes telle. This Troilus this lettre thoughte al straunge, han he ne wolde him holden that she highte; I wil list him to leve That loveth in swich cas, though hím greve. Was ther non other broche yow liste lete To feffe with your newe love, quod he, But thilke broche that I, with teres wete Yow yaf, as for aremembraunce ofme? Non other cause, allas, ne hadde ye But for despyt, and eek for that ye mente Alsoutrely to shewen your ententel To hir he wroot yet ofte tyme al newe ful pitously, he lefte it nought for slouthe, Biseching hir that, sin that he was trew She wolde come ayein and holde hir trouthe for which Criseyde upon a day, forrouthe, I take it so, touchinge al this matere, Wrot hím ayein, and seyde as ye may here. Cassandre goth, and he with cruel herte foryat his wo, for angre of hir speche; And from his bed al sodeinly he sterte, Hs though al hool him badde ymad a leche. And day by day be gan enquere and seche A sooth of this, with al his fulle cure; And thus he dryeth forth his aventure. But natbeles, men seyn that, at the laste, h a cas bítidde, and that as faste, oilus wel understood that she Nas not so kinde as that hir oughte be. And fynally, he woot now, out of doute, That al is lost that he hath been aboute. Through which I see that clene out of your minde Ye han me cast, and I ne can nor may, for al this world, within myn herte finde Co unloven yow a quarter ofa day In cursed tyme I born was, weylaway1 That ye, that doon me al this wo endure, Yet love I best of any creature. Cupydes sone, ensample of goodlibede, O swerd of knighthod, sours of gentilesse How mighte a wight in torment and in And helelees, yow sende as yet gladnesse? I hertelees, I syke, Iin distresse; Sin ye with me, nor I with yow may dele, Yow neither sende ich herte may nor hele. Stood on a day in hís malencolye This Troilus, and in suspecioun Of hir for whom he wende for to dye. And so bifel, that throughout Troye toun, As was the gyse, ybore was up and doun Amaner cotesarmure, as seyth the storie, Biforn Deiphebe, in signe of his victorie, fortune, wbiche that permutacioun Of thinges hath, as it is hir committed Chrough purveyaunce and disposicioun Of heighe Jove, as regnes shal ben flitted fro folk in folk, or whan they shal ben smitted Gan pulleawey the fetheres brighte of Troye fro day to day, til they ben bare of joye. Now God, quod he, me sende yet the grace That I may meten with this Diomedel And trewely,if I have might and space, Yet shal I make, I hope, his sydes blede. God, quod he, that oughtest taken hede To fortheren trouthe, and wronges to punyce, Why niltow doon a vengeaunce on this vyce? Your lettres ful, the papír al ypleynted, Conseyved hath myn hertes piëtee; I have eek seyn with teres al depeynted Your lettre, and how that ye requeren me To come ayein, which yet ne may not be. But why, lest that this lettre founden were, No mencioun ne make I now, for fere. Among al this, the fyn of the parodie Of Ector gan approchen wonder blyve; The fate wolde his soule sholde unbodíe, And shapen hadde a mene it out to dryve; Ayeins which fate him helpeth not to stryve; But on a day to fighten gan he wende, At wbich, allas1 he caughte hís lyves ende. The whiche cote, as telleth Lollius, Deiphebe it hadde yrent from Diomede The same day; and whan this Troilus It saugh, he gan to taken of it hede, Avysing of the lengthe and of the brede, And al the werk; but as he gan bibolde, ful sodeinly his herte gan to colde, O Pandare, that in dremes for to triste Me blamed hast, and wont art ofte upbreyde, Now maystow see thyselve, if that thee liste, How trewe is now thy nece, bright Criseyde In sondry formes, God it woot, he seyde, The goddes shewen bothe joye and tene In slepe, and by my dreme it is now sene. for which me thínketh every maner wight That haunteth armes oughte to biwayle The deeth of him that was so noble a knight; for as he drough a king by thaventayle, Anwar of this, Achilles through the mayle And through the body gan him for to ryve; And thus this worthy knight was brought of lyve. Grevous to me, God woot, is your unreste, Your haste, and that, the goddes ordenaunce, It semeth not ye take it for the beste. Nor other thing nis in your remembraunce, As thinketh me, but only your plesaunce. But beth not wrooth, and that I yow biseche; for that I tarie, is al for wikked speche. for I have herd wel more than I wende, Touchinge us two, how thinges han ystonde; Which I shal with dissímulinge amende. And beth nought wrooth, I have eek understonde, for whom, as olde bokes tellen us, Was mad swich wo, that tonge it may not telle; As he that on the coler fond withinne Abroche, that he Criseyde yaf that morwe Chat she from Troye moste nedes twinne, In remembraunce of hím and of hís sorwe; And she him leyde ayein bir feyth to borwe Co kepeit ay; but now, ful wel he wiste, His lady nas no lenger on to triste. How ye ne doon but holden me in honde. But now no fors, I can not in yow gesse But alle trouthe and alle gentilesse. And certaynly, withoute more speche, from hennesforth, as ferforth as I may, Myn owene deeth in armes wol I seche; I recche not how sone be the day But trewely, Criseyde, swete may, Whom I have ay with al my might yserved, That ye thus doon, I haveit nought deserved. be gooth him hoom, and gan ful sone sende for Pandarus; and al this newe chaunce, And of this broche, he tolde bim word and ende, Compleyninge of hir hertes variaunce, his longe love, bís trouthe, and his penaunce; And after deeth, withouten wordes more, ful faste he cryde, his reste him to restore. This Pandarus, that alle thise thinges herde, And wiste wel he seyde a sooth of this, He nought a word ayein to him answerde; for sory of his frendes sorwe he is, And shamed, for his nece hath doon amis; And stant, astoned of thise causes tweye, Hs stille as stoon; a word ne coude he seye. Troílus and Criseyde. But at the laste thus he spak, and seyde: My brother dere, I may thee do no more What shulde I seyn? I hate, ywis, Criseyde1 And God wot, I wol hate hir evermore And that thou me bisoughtest doon of yore, Havinge unto myn honour ne my reste Right no reward, I dide al that thee leste. If I dide ought that mighte lyken thee, It is me leef; and of this treson now, God woot, that it a sorwe is unto mel And dredelees, for hertes ese of yow Right fayn wolde I amende it, wiste I how. And fro this world, almighty God I preye, Delivere bir sone; I can no more seye. Gret was the sorwe and pleynt of Troilus; But forth hir cours fortune ay gan to holde Criseyde loveth the sone of Tydeus, And Troilus mot wepe in cares colde Swich is this world; whoso it can bibolde, In eche estat is litel hertes reste; God leve us for to take it for the beste1 In many cruel batayle, out of drede, Of Troilus, this ilke noble knight Hs men may in thise olde bokes rede, Was sene his knighthod and his grete might. And dredelees, hís ire, day and night, ful cruelly the Grekes ay aboughte; And alwey most this Diomede he soughte. And ofte tyme, I finde that they mette With blody strokes and with wordes grete, Assayinge how hir speres weren whette; And God it woot, with many a cruel hete Gan Troilus upon hís helm to bete. But natheles, fortune it nought ne wolde, Of otheres hond that either deyen sholde. And if I hadde ytaken for to wryte The armes of this ilke worthy man, Than wolde I of his batailles endyte. But for that I to wryte first bigan Of hís love, I have seyd as that I can. His worthy dedes, whoso list hem here, Reed Dares, he can telle hem alle yfere. Bisechinge every lady bright of hewe, And every gentil womman, what she be, That al be that Criseyde was untrewe Chat for that gilt she be not wrooth with me. Ye may hir gilt in othere bokes see; And gladlier I wol wryten, if yow leste, Penelopeës trouthe and good Alceste. NeI sey not this alonly for thise men, But most for wommen that bitraysed be Through false folk; God yeve hem sorwe, amen1 That with hir grete wit and subtiltee Bitrayse yowl and this commeveth me To speke, and in effect yow alle I preye, Beth war of men, and herkeneth what I seye1 552 Go, litel book, go litel myn tregedie, Ther God thy maker yet, er that he So sende might to maken som But litel book, no makin But subgit be to alle poe Virgile, Ovyde, Omer, Lucan,an And for ther is so greet diversitee In English and in wryting of our tonge So preye I God that noon miswryte th Ne thee mismetre for defaute of to And red wherso thou be, or elles son That thou be understonde I God beseche But yet to purpos of my rather spe The wraththe, as I began yow for to seye, Of Troilus, the Grekes boughten dere for thousandes his hondes maden deye As he that was withouten any Save Ector, in hís tyme, as I can here But weylaway, save only Goddes wille, Dispitously him slough the fiers Achille. And whan that he was slayn in this manere, His lighte goost ful blisfully is wen Up to the holownesse of the seventh spere In convers letinge every element And ther he saugh, with ful avysement, The erratik sterres, herkeninge armonye With sownes fulle of hevenish melodye. And doun from thennes faste he gan avyse This litel spot of erthe, that with the see Enbraced is, and fully gan despys This wrecched world, and held al vanitee To respect of the pleyn felicitee That is in hevene above; and at the laste, Ther he was slayn, his loking doun he caste; And in himself he lough right at the wo Of hem that wepten for his deeth so faste; And dampned al our werk that folweth so The blinde lust, the which that may not laste, And sholden al our berte on hevene caste. And forth he wente, shortly for to telle, Ther as Mercurie sorted him to dwelle. Swich fyn hath, lo, thís Troilus for love, Swich fyn bath al his grete worthinesse; Swich fyn hath hís estat real above, Swich fyn his lust, swich fyn hath his noblesse; Swich fyn hath false worldes brotelnesse. And thus bigan his lovinge of Criseyde, Hs I have told, and in this wyse he deyde. O yonge fresshe folkes, he or she, In which that love up groweth with your age, Repeyreth hoom from worldly vanitee, And of your herte upcasteth the visage To thilke God that after his image Yow made, and thinketh al nis but a fayre This world, that passeth sone as floures fayre. And loveth him, the which that right for love apon a cros, our soules for to beye heveneabove; alsen no wight, dar I seye at wol his herte al hoolly on him leye. And sin be best to loveis,and most meke, feyned loves for to seke? Lo here, of Payens corsed olde rytes, Lo here, what alle hir goddes may availle; Lo here, these wrecched worldes appetytes; Lo here, the fyn and guerdon for travaille Of Jove, Appollo, of Mars of swich rascaille1 Lo bere, the forme of olde clerkes speche In poetrye, if ye bir bokes seche. To thee, and to the philosophical Strode, To vouchen sauf, ther nedeis, to corecte, Of your benignitees and zeles gode. And to that sothfast Crist, that starf on rode, With al myn herte of mercy ever I preye; And to the Lord right thus I speke and seye: Thou oon, and two, and three, eterne on lyve, That regnest ay in three and two and oon, Ancircumscript, and al mayst circumscryve, Us from visible and invisible foon Defende; and to thy mercy, everichoon, So make us, Jesus, for thy grace digne, for love of mayde and moder thyn benigne1 Amen. Explicit Liber Troili et Criseydis.
